sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The sentence has the logical form: if one person is related to a second and the second is related to a third, then the first is related to the third. In symbols, xRy and yRz imply xRz. This is an implication expressing transitivity; among the combinations offered, statement (iii), 'implicative', is the intended description.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The sentence alone does not establish symmetry/commutativity, because it does not assert every reversed pair. Nor does it establish reflexivity, which is required along with symmetry and transitivity for an equivalence relation. Hence options A and C claim unsupported properties, and D overlooks the implication form.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Translate the natural-language conditional into xRy ∧ yRz ⇒ xRz before classifying it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A vertex is a distinguished corner or meeting point of edges. A geometric circle is a smooth closed curve with no corners or endpoints, so it has no vertices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A circle does not have one distinguished corner, and its infinitely many points are not all called vertices in ordinary geometry.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Points on a smooth curve are not vertices unless a graph structure explicitly declares them so.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Let the message contain word blocks w₁,…,wₖ with total stored length n (ignoring unchanged separators). The encoding stores each adjacent pair in both orders. Interior blocks occur four times, but the first and last blocks occur only twice. Its length is 4n−2(|w₁|+|wₖ|), which is strictly less than 4n, although asymptotically Θ(n).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 2n and 3n are not general totals because repetition depends on whether a block is an endpoint or interior. Exactly 4n overcounts the two endpoint blocks.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Count how many times endpoint and interior message blocks are duplicated.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A program draws its operations from the machine/language instruction set, so its distinct instructions form a subset. Execution arranges instruction occurrences in a sequence. Any total sequence order is also a partial order (it satisfies the partial-order conditions with additional comparability). Thus A, B, and C are compatible descriptions and all are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Each of A–C captures only one mathematical aspect—membership, sequence, or ordering—whereas D includes them together.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A sequence is a specially constrained ordered collection drawn from an instruction set.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Starting from MU, rule R2 doubles the suffix after M, so the number of U symbols can become 1,2,4,8,… . Applying U→IU to every U then produces M followed by that many IU blocks. String (i) contains five IU blocks and cannot arise by repeated doubling, while string (ii) contains eight IU blocks and can. Exactly one is valid.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Because five is not a power of two, the two strings cannot both be generated, so B and C fail. Since the eight-block string is generable, D also fails.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: R2 doubles the post-M suffix; target repetition counts must follow powers of two.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: First use absorption: X+Y+XY = X+Y because Y+XY=Y. Then apply (A+B)(A+C)=A+BC: (X+Y)(X+Z)=X+YZ. Hence the simplified expression is X+YZ.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other expressions either retain redundant terms, introduce subtraction (not Boolean OR/AND simplification), or omit required minterms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Absorption followed by (X+Y)(X+Z)=X+YZ gives the result in two steps.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: All gates shown are NOR gates. Let a=¬(x+y). The two middle outputs are b=¬(x+a) and c=¬(a+y), and the final output is f=¬(b+c). Evaluating the four input pairs gives f=1 for 00 and 11, but f=0 for 01 and 10. That is the XNOR (equivalence) truth table.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: XOR has the complementary truth table. NAND is zero only at 11, and NOR is one only at 00; neither matches the circuit's two equal-input cases.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Name intermediate gate outputs, then evaluate or algebraically simplify the NOR network.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: CMOS logic normally swings close to both supply rails, providing a large separation between guaranteed output levels and input thresholds. This produces a higher static noise margin than the listed bipolar logic families.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: TTL and ECL use smaller voltage-level separations; ECL prioritizes speed. BiCMOS combines technologies but the standard family comparison credits CMOS with the greatest noise immunity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Wide rail-to-rail logic swings generally provide generous noise margins.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: (10111)₂=16+4+2+1=23 and (1110)₂=14. Their product is 23×14=322. Repeated division or place values give 322=1×16²+4×16+2=(142)₁₆.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 150₁₆ and 241₁₆ represent different decimal values, while option D is a binary-looking string rather than the requested hexadecimal representation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Convert, multiply, and regroup the product into hexadecimal place values.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Write each coefficient in binary and shift: 3×512 contributes bits 10 and 9; 7×64 contributes bits 8,7,6; 5×8 contributes bits 5 and 3; and 3 contributes bits 1 and 0. These positions do not overlap, giving 2+3+2+2=9 one-bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other counts result from treating decimal coefficients as single bits or overlooking the multiple 1s in 3, 5, or 7.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Multiplication by powers of two shifts a coefficient's binary bit pattern without changing its popcount.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Each recursive call creates a new activation record on the call stack. The most recently created call must finish before its caller can resume, so records are removed in last-in, first-out order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: FIFO describes a queue, not nested calls. Recursion is not inherently parallel and has nothing to do with load balancing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Nested procedure calls are managed by a LIFO stack.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Each argv[i] has type char*, pointing to a command-line string. C does not define addition of two pointers, so argv[1]+argv[2]+argv[3] is a constraint violation and the program fails to compile as written. Converting the strings to integers would require a function such as strtol.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The program neither concatenates the strings into 123 nor parses and adds them to obtain 6. Quoted '123' is also not produced.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Command-line arguments are strings/pointers, not integers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A constructor is the special class operation invoked during object creation to establish the object's initial state, including initialization of instance variables.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A generic member function need not initialize an object; a destructor handles cleanup at the end of lifetime; a structure is a type construct, not an initialization method.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Construction initializes an object; destruction releases its resources.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Encapsulation bundles an object's data with the methods that operate on that data and controls access through the class interface. It is therefore the mechanism that associates code and data.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Dynamic binding selects implementations at runtime. Abstraction hides unnecessary detail and is supported by encapsulation but is not the bundling definition asked here. Creating a new class is class definition or derivation, not encapsulation itself.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Encapsulation packages state and behavior behind a controlled interface.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: I is true: overload resolution is normally compile-time polymorphism. II is true: derived-class members can access inherited protected members subject to language rules. III is false: constructors and destructors are not inherited in the ordinary C++ sense. IV is true: a nonmember friend function is called with ordinary function syntax. V is false: nesting does not imply inheritance. Thus I, II, and IV are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options A–C include statement III or V, both of which confuse object lifetime/nesting with inheritance.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Overloading is static; protected supports derivation; friendship and nesting are not inheritance.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The ER model describes entity sets, their attributes, and relationship sets connecting entities. These are statements I, II, and III. A 'functional relationship' is not a fourth basic modeling component in the same sense.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options A and B omit attributes or add the nonbasic item IV; option C is malformed and omits entities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: ER stands for entities, their properties, and relationships among them.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Specialization begins with a broad supertype and defines narrower subtypes based on distinguishing properties, so it proceeds top-down. For example, Employee may be specialized into Faculty and Staff.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Bottom-up combination of similar entity types into a supertype is generalization, not specialization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Specialization splits a general type; generalization combines specific types.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A determinant is the attribute group on the left side of a functional dependency (1-d). A candidate key uniquely identifies a row (2-b). The listed non-redundancy/minimality description pairs with no attribute being addable as stated (3-a). A functional dependency is a constraint between attribute sets (4-c). This yields 1-d,2-b,3-a,4-c.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The alternative mappings treat a key or dependency as the left-hand determinant and mismatch row identification with unrelated terms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: In X→Y, X is the determinant; a candidate key uniquely identifies tuples.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Second normal form requires a relation already in 1NF to have no partial dependency of a non-prime attribute on a proper subset of a candidate key. Therefore absence of partial functional dependencies is the defining 2NF condition.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 3NF additionally addresses transitive dependencies, BCNF strengthens determinant requirements, and 4NF addresses nontrivial multivalued dependencies.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: 2NF removes partial-key dependencies.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: I is true because basic 2PL can deadlock. II is generally true because timestamp ordering avoids waiting but may abort conflicting transactions. III is wrong: a DFD black hole is a process that consumes inputs without producing output, not a data store definition. IV is wrong because nontrivial multivalued dependencies are handled by 4NF, not 3NF. V is wrong because an ER diagram represents a data/conceptual model, not an event model. Thus III, IV, and V are wrong.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other groups mark statement I or II wrong even though those are recognized properties of the respective concurrency-control protocols, or omit one of III–V.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Distinguish transaction-control properties, DFD process errors, normalization levels, and data modeling.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In a strict (full) binary tree every internal node has exactly two children. If I is the number of internal nodes and L the number of leaves, L=I+1. Hence the total N=I+L=2L−1, which is always odd. In particular, an odd L still gives an odd N.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: N cannot be even. It may equal the number of leaves only in the one-node boundary case, so C is not universally justified by the stated condition. Also N=2L−1 is less than, not greater than, 2L.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A full binary tree satisfies L=I+1 and N=2L−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A complete graph has one edge for every unordered pair of distinct vertices. The number of such pairs is C(n,2)=n(n−1)/2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other expressions do not count unordered pairs: n counts only linearly, n(n+1)/2 includes extra self-like terms, and n²/2 is not generally even an integer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Edges of Kₙ correspond one-to-one with 2-element vertex subsets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The last car to enter is positioned nearest the exit end of the accessible chain and must leave before cars parked earlier behind it can leave. Thus entry and removal follow last-in, first-out order, exactly the behavior of a stack.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A queue is FIFO, while array and linked list describe storage layouts rather than the access discipline emphasized by the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: When the most recently inserted item must be removed first, the structure is a stack.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Deleting the first node of a singly linked list requires only changing the head pointer (and freeing the old node), so it is Θ(1), not Θ(n). Therefore statement B is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Searching an unsorted list and finding a maximum require a full Θ(n) traversal. Finding/deleting an unspecified middle node also requires Θ(n) traversal unless a direct pointer is supplied.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Head insertion/deletion is constant time; position/value discovery generally requires traversal.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Insert in order: 37→2, 38→3, 72 hashes to 2 and probes to 4, 48→6, and 98→0. Key 11 hashes to 4, finds it occupied by 72, and moves by linear probing to index 5. Thus 11 is stored at 5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Indices 3 and 4 are already occupied when 11 arrives, while index 6 is not reached because index 5 is the first free probe position.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Linear probing checks h(k),h(k)+1,… cyclically until the first empty slot.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A gateway connects dissimilar or separately administered networks and forwards/translates traffic between them, allowing a device on one network to communicate with another network.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A hub or ordinary switch connects devices within a local network segment. Utility and file servers provide services but are not the general inter-network forwarding device.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Inter-network communication requires a layer-3 gateway/router function.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Cannot be determined from the question — window size depends on sequence-number bits and protocol variant
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: There is no protocol-independent numeric maximum. With an m-bit sequence field, Go-Back-N normally permits at most 2^m−1 outstanding frames, while Selective Repeat normally limits the window to 2^(m−1) to avoid ambiguity. The stem supplies neither m nor the sliding-window variant, so 4, 8, 15, and 16 cannot be selected uniquely. For example, m=4 would give 15 for Go-Back-N but 8 for Selective Repeat.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Each listed number becomes plausible only after adding an unstated sequence-space size and protocol assumption.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Window limits are derived from the finite sequence-number space and retransmission protocol.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Ethernet, Frame Relay, and Token Ring define data-link framing/MAC behavior. HSSI is a high-speed serial physical interface, and 10BASE-T primarily specifies an Ethernet physical-layer medium/signaling implementation. Thus the intended data-link-only set is 1,3,5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options containing item 2 or 4 mix physical-layer interface/media standards into the data-link set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Separate framing and media-access protocols from physical connectors, signaling, and cabling variants.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A large bus/tree cable can suffer unequal attenuation and signal levels over distance. Dividing it into shorter segments connected by repeaters limits each electrical span and regenerates the signal, overcoming the balancing problem. This is segmentation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Polling controls access, modulation changes signal representation, and merely using a stronger transmitter does not correctly regenerate and isolate long cable sections.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Segment long shared media so repeaters can restore signal quality between spans.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Token Ring access is deterministic (ii-a). A cut-through switch begins forwarding before receiving the whole frame and can exploit full wire speed/low latency (iii-b). Spanning Tree prevents switching loops (iv-c). The remaining pairing is Ethernet with valid-address-code checking as worded (i-d). Therefore i-d, ii-a, iii-b, iv-c.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The alternatives assign determinism to Ethernet or exchange the established cut-through and spanning-tree functions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Token passing is deterministic; cut-through forwards early; spanning tree removes loops.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An absolute loader places object code at the addresses already prescribed in the object program. Address adjustment/relocation decisions must therefore be resolved before loading; in the standard scheme the assembler produces the absolute addresses. Option A's 're-allocation' is evidently the paper's wording for relocation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The loader itself performs the physical loading. In the classic absolute scheme, allocation and intermodule linking are typically assigned by the programmer, whereas the assembler translates/relocates address-dependent references into absolute form.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: An absolute loader does no run-time relocation; it obeys addresses prepared earlier.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Grammar A describes nested strings whose leading production choice is recoverable from the eventual handle/lookahead, and grammar B describes a^n c followed by n symbols from {a,b}; both have deterministic rightmost reductions with one-symbol lookahead and are LR(1). Grammar C, A→A+A|a, is ambiguous because a+a+a has two parse trees. Hence both A and B, but not C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options A and B omit the other LR(1) grammar. Option C selects an ambiguous grammar, and an ambiguous grammar cannot be LR(k).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: LR grammars must be unambiguous; canonical LR(1) lookahead can resolve deterministic nested handles.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The intended third production is W→Sz (the scan resembles 'S2'). Reduce the input xxxxyzz bottom-up: y→S prints 2; Sz→W prints 3; xxW→S prints 1; the next Sz→W prints 3; and the outer xxW→S prints 1. The translation is 2 3 1 3 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other sequences print actions in top-down derivation order or reorder reductions. A shift-reduce parser executes semantic actions when each handle is reduced, from the innermost phrase outward.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For bottom-up parsing, record actions in reduction order, not production-expansion order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A pushdown automaton recognizes exactly the context-free languages. A two-way linear-bounded automaton is at least as powerful for this purpose and can simulate the necessary stack within linearly bounded tape. Therefore both B and C can recognize CFG languages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A finite automaton recognizes only regular languages, a strict subset of CFLs, so A alone is insufficient.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: PDA = CFL recognition; stronger bounded-tape machines can also recognize CFLs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A synthesized attribute at a parent is computed from attributes of its children. Bottom-up LR parsing completes/reduces the children before reducing their parent, so these values can be stored on the parse stack and combined naturally at reduction time.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Top-down LL parsing reaches a parent before its children are complete and is more naturally associated with inherited information. Ambiguity is unrelated to attribute evaluation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Synthesized values flow upward, matching bottom-up reductions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Round-robin keeps ready processes in FIFO order, but adds a time quantum: when a running process exhausts its quantum, it is preempted and placed at the rear. It is therefore the preemptive form of FIFO/FCFS scheduling.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: FILO, shortest-seek-first, and longest-time-first do not describe the circular ready-queue discipline.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Round-robin = FIFO rotation plus timer-driven preemption.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process references a virtual page that is not currently resident in physical memory. The operating system must locate/load it (or reject the access if invalid) before the instruction can continue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A page fault is not inherently a programming error, nor is it defined by the previous reference or the vague occurrence descriptions in the other choices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Valid virtual address + absent resident page triggers a page fault.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the conventional semaphore interpretation, a negative value records how many wait operations could not proceed. If s=−n, its magnitude n is the number of processes blocked in the semaphore's waiting queue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: n+1 and n−1 introduce unsupported offsets, and zero would contradict the negative count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For a counting semaphore, a negative magnitude represents queued waiters.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Best-fit allocation searches the free holes and chooses the smallest hole large enough for the request. This leaves the least immediate unused remainder in the selected hole.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: First-fit uses the first sufficient hole; worst-fit uses the largest; buddy allocation chooses power-of-two blocks through splitting/coalescing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Best fit means the smallest adequate free block.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The Unix wc utility reports counts of lines, words, and bytes/characters; wc -c gives bytes and wc -m gives characters in implementations supporting it. Thus wc is the listed command used for the required count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: nc is commonly netcat, while chcnt and lc are not the standard Unix character-count utility.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Use wc with the appropriate count option, especially -m for characters.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Software engineering combines a defined development process, technical methods for analysis/design/testing, and supporting tools for automation and control. These three layers work together to produce and maintain software systematically, so all are integrated.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Process alone gives management structure, methods alone give techniques, and tools alone automate tasks; none by itself constitutes the full discipline.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Software engineering is commonly described as a layered technology of process, methods, and tools.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: If investigation finds no cause inside the software system, the failure is attributed to an event or condition outside the program's controlled boundary—an external error. Examples include unavailable devices, malformed external services, or environmental failures.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Internal and logic errors originate in the program's code/state. An inherent error is rooted in the problem statement or requirements, which is still an identifiable source rather than an unidentified internal cause.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Classify an error by whether its cause lies inside or outside the software boundary.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Measured software attributes—size, complexity, defect density, effort, change rate, and similar metrics—support cost/schedule estimation (i), reliability prediction (ii), and monitoring/control of development (iii). Therefore i, ii, and iii are valid uses.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Statement iv ('nowhere') contradicts all three established uses, so every option containing iv is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Software metrics support prediction as well as ongoing project management.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Black-box testing treats the implementation as hidden and checks observable behavior at its interfaces: supplied inputs, produced outputs, and conformance to requirements. Option A states this purpose.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Examining internal code structure is white-box testing. Black-box testing is not defined by being conducted only at a client's site.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Black box tests external behavior without using internal implementation knowledge.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The stem describes a particular batch/transaction-processing naming convention: after the operator supplies the source transaction filename and editing prepares the transactions for execution, the resulting executable transaction file is named Opt.Exe. Thus the requested stipulated filename is option C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Batch.Exe, Trans.Exe, and Edit.Exe are plausible descriptive names but are not the output-file name specified by this convention.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: This is a terminology/naming question about the edited transaction-processing output, not a general operating-system rule.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A single-stage interconnection network may need to send an item through the same switching stage several times before it reaches its destination. Because data can circulate through that stage repeatedly, it is called a recirculating network.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: One-sided/two-sided describe neither this routing behavior nor the standard name, and a pipeline normally uses a sequence of distinct stages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Repeated passes through one switching stage give the name recirculating network.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) supports multidimensional, aggregate, and exploratory queries over large databases/data warehouses to retrieve decision-support information.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: OLTP is optimized for numerous short operational transactions and updates. OLDP and OLPP are not the standard term for large-scale analytical querying.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: OLTP runs operations; OLAP analyzes accumulated data.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: MMDS (Multichannel Multipoint Distribution Service) distributes multiple channels from a fixed transmitter to subscriber antennas using microwave radio. Because it can provide cable-like multichannel service without physical coaxial cable, it has historically been called 'wireless cable.'
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: ATM is a cell-switching technology and CDMA a multiple-access technique. LMDS is also a fixed wireless access technology, but the historical nickname in this question is conventionally associated with MMDS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: MMDS is a point-to-multipoint microwave distribution system historically marketed as wireless cable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: IEEE 802.11a is one member of the IEEE 802.11 wireless LAN family marketed under the Wi-Fi interoperability name. It operates in the 5 GHz range and is therefore Wi-Fi.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: WiMAX is based on IEEE 802.16, Fast Ethernet is IEEE 802.3 networking, and 802.11g is a different Wi-Fi amendment rather than another name for 802.11a.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: 802.11 denotes Wi-Fi WLAN standards; 802.16 denotes WiMAX.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The Unlicensed National Information Infrastructure (U-NII) allocations are in the 5 GHz region (split into several sub-bands). Therefore the appropriate listed frequency is 5 GHz.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 2.4 GHz is associated with the ISM band used by 802.11b/g and other devices; 33 MHz and 5 MHz are far outside the U-NII microwave allocations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: U-NII is the unlicensed 5-GHz-band family used by technologies including 802.11a.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: “x¹ is a clone of x” means x¹ is identical to x in height, weight, and complexion. Given that these form a complete attribute set for the entity, is cloning an equivalence relation?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Represent each entity by the complete tuple (height, weight, complexion). Equality of tuples is reflexive (every tuple equals itself), symmetric (if x¹ equals x, then x equals x¹), and transitive (two tuples equal to the same tuple equal each other). Therefore 'is a clone of' is an equivalence relation under the stated definition.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: The complete attribute set makes the truth value decidable, so C is false. Since all three equivalence properties follow from tuple equality, B and D are also false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Equality on a complete attribute tuple is always an equivalence relation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: R can do only tasks that M can do, but it can do merely some of them. Thus Task(R) is a proper subset of Task(M). The correct set diagram is the small circle completely contained in the larger circle, shown in Diagram B.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Overlap in Diagram A allows R-only tasks, contradicting 'unable to do anything else.' External contact in Diagram C does not express task inclusion.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: 'Some of M's tasks and no others' translates to R⊂M.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Let n=3^x. Expression (i) is (aba)^n. The identity (aba)^n = a(baa)^(n−1)ba gives (ii): adjacent copies concatenate as aba|aba = a|baa|ba. Similarly, regrouping yields (aba)^n = ab(aab)^(n−1)a, which is (iii). Hence all three denote the same strings for the intended positive values of x.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options A–C deny at least one of these direct concatenation/regrouping identities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Cyclic regrouping of repeated 'aba' blocks produces both alternative forms without changing the string.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Create a vertex for each actor/entity and connect entities associated in a sentence. The statements include a triangle such as I–student–maths–I, so at least three colors are required. A consistent three-coloring exists for the remaining vertices (professor, algorithms, electronics, computer science), so the lower bound is attainable. The chromatic number is 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Two colors cannot color the triangle. No four-clique or other obstruction forces a fourth color, so 4 and 'none' are unnecessary.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Find a triangle for a lower bound, then exhibit a 3-coloring for the matching upper bound.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A four-decimal PIN has 10^4=10,000 possibilities. Four hexadecimal digits give 16^4=65,536 possibilities, a 6.5536× rise. Five decimal digits give 10^5=100,000 possibilities, a 10× rise. Halving terminal speed only doubles attack time. Therefore the five-decimal-digit PIN gives the largest increase.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Option A improves security less than B, option C only doubles time, and D is false because all three proposed changes affect brute-force time.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Brute-force time is proportional to keyspace size divided by trial rate.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.answerText: No listed option — the shown circuit simplifies to C + A̅B̅D
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: All four gates are NOR gates. Let p=(A+B)̅, q=(p+C)̅, and r=(C+D)̅. The output is F=(q+r)̅=q̅r̅=(p+C)(C+D). Using (X+C)(C+D)=C+XD gives F=C+pD=C+A̅B̅D. Direct truth-table checking confirms this expression, but it matches none of A–D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Each listed expression has a different truth table. For example, when C=1 the actual output is always 1, whereas several choices still depend on A, B, or D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Label each NOR output and apply De Morgan's law from the final gate backward.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A clock constrains when state changes may occur in a synchronous circuit. Designers can analyze signals relative to clock edges and allow combinational paths to settle between them, making races and hazards substantially easier to control than in asynchronous circuits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Asynchronous circuits may be faster because they need not wait for clocks and may use less clocking hardware. Noise immunity is primarily a logic-family/electrical property, not the defining synchronous advantage.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Clocked state transitions make timing behavior predictable and hazard control easier.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For a symmetric CMOS inverter, the switching/transition threshold is approximately halfway between the supply rails. With VDD=10 V and ground at 0 V, the transition voltage is about VDD/2=5 V.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 1 V and 2 V are too close to the low rail for the nominal symmetric threshold; 10 V is the supply rail, not the transition point.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: A balanced CMOS inverter switches near half the supply voltage.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The printed bit string is not already valid digit-by-digit 8421 BCD: padded groups are 0001 1011 . 1100, and 1011/1100 exceed 9. Applying standard BCD correction (+0110 to each invalid tetrad) gives 0001 (1011+0110) . (1100+0110) = 0010 0010 . 0010 after carries. These are decimal digits 2,2.2, so the intended result is 22.2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 22.0, 20.2, and 21.2 do not follow the correction/carry process. Strictly speaking, the original groups are invalid as a canonical BCD numeral; the answer relies on the exercise's intended correction interpretation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: A four-bit BCD group above 1001 is corrected by adding 0110 and propagating the carry.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.answerText: No listed option under standard K-map ordering — the function is C̅
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A three-variable K-map orders the BC columns as 00,01,11,10. Both A rows contain 1,0,0,1, so A is irrelevant. The first and fourth columns are adjacent by wraparound and are precisely the columns with C=0; grouping all four 1-cells therefore gives F=C̅. None of A–D states C̅. Option C would follow only if the four columns were incorrectly read in ordinary binary order 00,01,10,11 rather than K-map Gray order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A·B, AB+BC+CA, and A·B·C depend on A. (B⊕C)̅ has the Gray-order pattern 1,0,1,0 rather than the shown 1,0,0,1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Karnaugh-map columns use Gray order so adjacent cells differ in exactly one bit.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The conditional operator associates right-to-left, so the expression is 10 ? (0 ? 5 : 1) : 12. Since 10 is nonzero, evaluate the inner conditional; since 0 is false, it yields 1. Therefore printf prints 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 10 is the condition, not the returned true expression. The outer false branch 12 is not selected, and the inner true branch 5 is not selected.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Parse nested ?: operators right-to-left, then follow only the selected branches.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Under the question's intended older-C execution model, p points at the first character 'a'; ++*p increments that character code to 'b', assigns it to c, and printf prints b. However, modifying a string literal is undefined behavior in C and may fault on a real conforming implementation; robust code should use char p[] = "ayqm".
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: The prefix increment changes the first character before assignment, so neither a nor q is intended. There is no arithmetic route to c.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: ++*p means increment the character pointed to by p, but string literals must not be modified.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A private class member can be accessed directly only by the class's own member functions (and explicitly granted friends, depending on language). This is the access level described by the stem.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Public members are accessible to clients; protected members are also accessible in derived classes; 'derive' is not an access specifier.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Private hides implementation members behind the class's own methods.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Garbage collection is characteristic of Java (A-1). A temporary/nameless object can be created within a statement (B-5). Templates support generic programming (C-2). A forward reference appears while defining a class/member before its later declaration (D-3 as intended). A derived class inherits base-class member functions (E-4). The sequence is 1,5,2,3,4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: The other mappings confuse templates with member functions, garbage collection with statement temporaries, or inheritance with class-definition terminology.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Java→GC, template→generic programming, derivation→inherited members.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Data abstraction specifies a collection of values together with permitted operations while hiding representation details. The resulting conceptual type is called an abstract data type (ADT).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A class or structure may implement an ADT, and a user-defined type is broader; neither is the precise result named by the abstraction process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: An ADT defines behavior independently of representation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: An entity type describes a category such as Student; an entity instance is one particular occurrence, such as the student with roll number 42. Therefore it is a single occurrence of an entity type.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A relationship instance is an occurrence of a relationship type, not an entity instance, so B and C mix distinct ER concepts.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Type is the schema-level category; instance is one concrete member.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Generalization combines multiple lower-level entity types that share features into a higher-level supertype, so it proceeds bottom-up—for example, Car and Truck generalized as Vehicle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Top-down splitting of a broad type into narrower subtypes is specialization, the inverse process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Generalization combines specifics upward; specialization divides a general type downward.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: 2NF contains no partial dependency (I-c); 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies of non-key attributes (II-a); 4NF removes problematic nontrivial multivalued dependencies (III-b); and 5NF removes nontrivial join dependencies not implied by keys (IV-d). Thus c,a,b,d, printed as option A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: The other orders attach multivalued/join dependencies or partial/transitive dependencies to the wrong normalization levels.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: 2NF partial, 3NF transitive, 4NF multivalued, 5NF join dependencies.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The schema data-definition language (schema DDL) lets the database administrator define the conceptual schema: relations/entities, attributes, constraints, and related structural components.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: DML manipulates/query data. A subschema DDL defines user/application views rather than the overall database schema, so 'all' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: DDL defines structure; DML operates on stored data.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Relational PROJECT selects specified columns. The result therefore has no more fields than the original; it may have fewer. Projection also does not create additional rows and duplicate elimination may reduce their number. None of A–C is true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A and C wrongly claim more columns, while B and C wrongly claim more tuples.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Projection is a vertical subset of a relation, with duplicate result tuples removed.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Pushes must occur A before B before C, but an item may be popped before later pushes. ABC is possible by pushing and immediately popping each item. CBA is possible after pushing all three. BAC is possible by push A, push B, pop B, pop A, then push/pop C. CAB is impossible: making C the first pop requires all three pushes to have occurred, leaving C above B above A, so B must pop before A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Explicit legal operation sequences exist for ABC, CBA, and BAC. Only CAB conflicts with the forced LIFO order after C is popped first.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Once C is first to pop, all three pushes have occurred and the remaining B-before-A order is forced.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A binary heap supports insertion and removal of the highest/lowest-priority element in O(log n), while inspecting that element is O(1). It is compact and is the standard efficient priority-queue implementation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: An unsorted array/list makes deletion of the best element linear; a sorted list makes insertion linear. An arbitrary binary tree gives no priority guarantee, whereas a heap is the specifically constrained tree needed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Heap order keeps the extreme-priority item at the root while preserving logarithmic updates.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A complete binary tree with n nodes has height h=⌊log₂n⌋. The longest path can run from a deepest leaf through the root to a deepest leaf in the opposite subtree, using about 2h edges. Hence the diameter is about 2log₂n.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: log₂n estimates root-to-leaf height, only half the possible leaf-to-leaf route. The alternatives involving n are far too large for a logarithmic-height tree.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Tree diameter is at most twice its height and is nearly that value for a complete tree.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Separate chaining stores colliding entries in an auxiliary chain at each array index. A bucket can therefore hold arbitrarily many entries, limited only by available memory rather than the 100 array slots. There is no fixed upper limit from table size alone.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 100 is the bound for open addressing, not chaining. 200 and 10,000 have no basis without a separate memory/load-factor constraint.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Chaining decouples entry count from the number of hash buckets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For order 5, a node holds at most four keys. Insert 7,8,1,4; inserting 13 overflows [1,4,7,8,13], so promote 7, leaving [1,4] and [8,13]. Insert 20 on the right. Insert 2,6 on the left; inserting 5 overflows [1,2,4,5,6], so promote 4. The root becomes [4,7], containing two elements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Counts 1,3,4 result from omitting the second split, promoting the wrong key, or confusing key count with child count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: An order-m B-tree has at most m children and m−1 keys per node; promote the median on overflow.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: An SNMP response PDU uses the error-status field to state whether an error occurred and what class it belongs to. If relevant, error-index identifies the variable binding associated with that error.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Error-index locates an offending binding but does not itself report the error type. Set-request is a PDU operation, and agent-index is not the required response field.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: error-status says what happened; error-index says where in the variable list.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A URL identifies the retrieval scheme/method (such as http), the host name, and the path to the document. A port may be included but can be omitted when the scheme's default applies. DNS is a resolution service used to map the host; it is not a component that must appear in the URL. Thus I,II,IV.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options A and B include DNS as a URL component, while D omits the host/path and includes optional or external mechanisms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: URL structure is scheme://host[:port]/path; DNS operates behind the name.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The transport layer provides logical end-to-end, process-to-process communication between applications on the two final hosts, including segmentation, reliability/flow control when supported, and port addressing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: The network layer provides host-to-host packet routing; data link is hop-to-hop; session manages dialogs above transport.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Transport is the OSI end-to-end process-delivery layer.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The Internet layer supplies logical addressing and routing, and its IP header includes service/traffic-class mechanisms used to request differentiated quality of service. It does not guarantee connection-oriented delivery, and framing belongs to the link/network-access layer. Therefore 1,2,3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options B–D include connection-oriented delivery, framing, or both, which are not Internet-layer IP services.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: IP provides addressing and routing with best-effort service; frames belong below it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The original IEEE 802.11 infrared physical layer specified data rates of 1 and 2 Mbit/s. Its maximum operating rate is therefore 2 Mbit/s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 1 Mbit/s is the lower supported rate; 5 and 11 Mbit/s are not rates of that original infrared PHY (11 Mbit/s is associated with 802.11b radio).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Legacy 802.11 infrared supported 1 or 2 Mbit/s.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: An absolute loader's task is deliberately simple: it reads absolute object code and places it into the exact memory addresses already specified. Thus the function actually performed by the loader is loading.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Allocation, linking, and relocation/address assignment must have been resolved before the absolute loader runs, by the programmer/translator toolchain.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Absolute loader = copy object words to predetermined addresses.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The parse-tree root is multiplication. Its left child is a plus node with leaves A and B; its right child is C. Reading children recursively gives (A+B)*C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: C omits the grouping and would root the tree at +. B is malformed prefix/infix notation, and D pairs different operands under multiplication.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Parenthesize each internal parse-tree node around the expressions represented by its children.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: Consider left-associative operators in decreasing precedence: − (subtraction), * (multiplication), and $ (exponentiation). Evaluate 3−2*4 $ 1*2 $ 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Apply the unusual stated precedence, not ordinary arithmetic. Highest: 3−2=1. Next, multiplication forms 1*4=4 and 1*2=2. Lowest, the two left-associative exponentiations give (4 $ 2) $ 3=(4²)³=16³=4096.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 64 and 512 come from different exponent associativity/grouping; −61 results from applying familiar precedence rather than the explicitly supplied order. The scan renders the second $ poorly, but 4096 and the declared operator set determine the intended expression.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: First group by declared precedence, then use left associativity within each level.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: In the Chomsky hierarchy, regular grammars are contained in context-free grammars, which are contained in context-sensitive grammars. Among the listed phrase-structure classes, context-sensitive grammar is therefore the most general.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Regular and context-free grammars generate strictly narrower language classes. 'None' is false because context-sensitive is offered.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Regular ⊂ context-free ⊂ context-sensitive ⊂ unrestricted.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The lexical analyzer, also called a scanner, reads the character stream and groups characters into lexemes, emitting tokens such as identifiers, keywords, and numeric literals.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A parser groups tokens into grammatical structures; optimization and code generation occur later and do not perform lexical grouping.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Characters→scanner→tokens→parser→syntax structure.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Disk scheduling uses SCAN (A-2). Traditional batch jobs are served FIFO (B-4). Time-sharing CPU service uses round-robin (C-1). Nested interrupt servicing follows a LIFO return/priority stack (D-3). Thus 2,4,1,3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Other mappings attach CPU round-robin to disk/interrupt work or swap the standard FIFO, SCAN, and LIFO roles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: SCAN→disk, FIFO→batch, RR→time sharing, LIFO→nested interrupts.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A semaphore is the synchronization object that controls concurrent access to critical resources. Its atomic wait/P and signal/V operations coordinate processes and can enforce mutual exclusion and resource-count limits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: P and V are operations on the semaphore, not the complete synchronization object. Swapping is memory/process management and does not protect a critical resource. Also, semaphores prevent races; avoiding deadlock still requires correct acquisition design.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Semaphore state plus atomic P/V operations regulate access to shared resources.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Round-robin is inherently preemptive: a timer interrupts a running process at the end of its quantum and returns it to the ready queue if unfinished.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Shortest-job/seek-first variants are not inherently preemptive, and priority scheduling may be implemented either preemptively or nonpreemptively, making it less uniquely correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: The time quantum is the explicit preemption mechanism in round-robin.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A binary semaphore used as a mutex is initialized to 1, representing one available permit. The first process performs wait and changes it to 0; others then block until the owner signals it back to 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Starting at 0 blocks every process initially, while 2 or 3 would permit multiple entrants and violate mutual exclusion.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Mutex semaphore: 1 means free, 0 means occupied.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A remote computing service lets a user interact with a distant computer while its resources are shared among users by time slicing. It therefore combines time sharing with interactive processing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Batch processing lacks the interactive remote dialogue; multiprocessing is an implementation possibility rather than the defining service mode; real-time processing imposes deadline constraints not implied here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Remote time-sharing provides an interactive terminal session on a shared host.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Software engineering applies disciplined processes and technical methods so that software both satisfies reliability/quality requirements and can be produced and maintained within realistic cost constraints. The two goals must be balanced, so reliable and cost-effective software is the best answer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Reliability without affordability may make a project infeasible, while cheap but unreliable software fails its purpose. Each single-goal option is incomplete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Engineering optimizes required quality under economic and schedule constraints.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Top-down design decomposes a system from high-level modules into progressively more detailed submodules. It relies on stepwise refinement and modularity, and may use flowcharts to express control. Loop invariants are a program-verification technique for proving loop correctness, not a requirement of top-down design.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Stepwise refinement is the essence of top-down decomposition; modularity is its structural result; flowcharting is a compatible design notation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Do not confuse a design/decomposition method with a formal loop-proof technique.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The classic waterfall model is the simplest conceptual lifecycle model: requirements, design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance proceed as a mostly linear sequence of phases.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Prototyping and iterative models add feedback cycles, repeated refinement, and version management, making their process structure more complex.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Waterfall is the basic linear phase-by-phase development model.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Software design usually begins with the overall architecture and successively decomposes it into subsystems, modules, interfaces, and detailed operations. This is a top-down progression.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Bottom-up design can be used when composing reusable components but is not the usual answer to the general lifecycle question. Random and 'centre fringing' are not disciplined design strategies.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Move from system architecture to increasingly detailed component design.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Application software is created to perform a user-oriented task, such as word processing, accounting, browsing, or image editing. Option C gives this defining property.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Controlling the operating system is a system-software role. Compilers, debuggers, and similar programmer aids are programming/system tools, so A and B do not define application software and D cannot be true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Application software serves an end-user task; system software operates/supports the computer platform.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For an interconnection/switching network, hardware cost is commonly modeled by switching complexity—the number and complexity of switching elements and interconnections required to realize its connectivity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Time complexity measures algorithmic running time. 'Circuit complexity' is broader and less specific than the standard switching-network cost measure used in this context.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Network topology cost grows with the switching fabric needed to implement it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A T1 digital carrier provides a nominal 1.544 Mbit/s leased line in the North American hierarchy. It consists of 24 DS0 channels plus framing overhead.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: T2, T3, and T4 are successively higher-rate aggregate levels; T3, for example, is about 44.736 Mbit/s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Remember the base high-speed leased digital carrier: T1 = 1.544 Mbit/s.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The intended IS-95 fact is that one 1.25-MHz CDMA forward carrier is channelized by 64 orthogonal Walsh codes, yielding 64 logical radio/code channels (including pilot, sync, paging, and traffic roles). Thus 64 is the intended count. The stem imprecisely calls these 'carriers'; technically it is one 1.25-MHz RF carrier containing up to 64 code channels.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 9, 18, and 22 are not the Walsh-code channelization count associated with an IS-95 1.25-MHz carrier.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: CDMA shares one wide RF carrier by orthogonal codes rather than assigning one separate carrier per user.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: PVM may manage multiple message buffers, but at any given instant a task designates exactly one active send buffer and one active receive buffer. Packing/sending operates on the active send buffer; receiving/unpacking operates on the active receive buffer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options B–D claim two simultaneously active buffers on one side, contrary to PVM's active-buffer model.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Multiple buffers can exist, but PVM exposes one active buffer in each direction at a time.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For a predictive model, the data set supplies training examples, the output set supplies the target values/classes the model must predict, and a separate test set measures generalization on unseen examples. Of the offered combinations, i,v,vi is the coherent supervised-learning workflow.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: An 'information set' and 'process set' are not the standard three datasets required to train and evaluate a predictive model. Options A, B, and D omit either the actual data or the held-out test set. The source terminology is loose; modern practice would more precisely say training inputs, target outputs, and validation/test data.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Predictive learning needs examples, targets, and unseen evaluation data.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: SRTF can indefinitely postpone a long job when shorter jobs keep arriving, so A is true. Priority-based preemptive policies can likewise starve low-priority work, so B's ‘may’ is true. Round robin gives every ready process an early time slice and normally improves interactive response time over FCFS, so C is true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one true statement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Preemption improves responsiveness but does not itself guarantee freedom from starvation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A process executes its entry section to obtain permission, then its critical section, then its exit section to release permission, and finally its remainder section. The three requested blanks are therefore entry, exit, remainder.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The other orders perform ordinary remainder work before permission acquisition or postpone release until after the remainder work.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Protocol order: entry → critical → exit → remainder.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Agile development manages uncertainty through short iterations that deliver working increments, obtain feedback, and permit priorities and design to adapt. Thus C is the defining practice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Exhaustive up-front requirements or risk analysis cannot eliminate unpredictability, while D directly contradicts agile adaptation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Short feedback cycles turn unpredictable change into manageable incremental decisions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A project manager plans work, tracks status, allocates resources, manages risks, and remains accountable for delivery within schedule and budget. All five listed duties belong to the role.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The restricted subsets omit genuine planning, risk, or delivery responsibilities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Project management integrates scope, schedule, cost, resources, monitoring, and risk.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.answerText: No listed option — A, B, and C are all compatible with the Open Source Definition
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A matches free redistribution. B reflects the permitted integrity-of-author-source-code condition, under which modifications may be distributed as patches or under changed names/versions. C is also true: open-source software may be sold. None of A–C is false, but no ‘none’ option is supplied.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 each label a valid open-source condition false; option 4 incorrectly says all three are false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Open source permits use, modification, redistribution, and sale; it does not mean zero price.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Circuit-SAT is in NP because an assignment is checked in polynomial time (A), and it is NP-hard—at least as hard as every NP language (B). Hence it is NP-complete (C). D is not a general size law, and P≠NP is consistent with, not contradictory to, NP-completeness, so E is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Every other choice includes D or E and/or omits one of A–C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Circuit-SAT is a canonical NP-complete decision problem.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: For ((A+B·D)/(E−F))+G, prefix is + / + A * B D − E F G, so A is true. Conversion moves operators but preserves operand order A,B,D,E,F,G, making C and D true. Prefix is not simply reversed postfix, so B is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 4 omit a true statement; option 3 includes the false reversal claim.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Prefix is expression-tree preorder and postfix is postorder; operand order remains stable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The displayed language description A is the intended set denoted by the expression, and grammar B generates its two component forms through S→AB|C. Statement C imposes one equality constraint across all four symbol counts, which is a different language. Thus A and B, but not C, are correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 omit one correct representation; option 4 incorrectly accepts C's all-counts-equal characterization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Derive the exponent pattern of each grammar branch, then compare it with the stated language.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Basic-block leaders are the first instruction, every jump target, and every instruction immediately following a conditional or unconditional jump. Hence A, C, and D are true. Being physically in the middle of the code has no control-flow significance, so B is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Alternatives containing B invent a leader rule; the remaining alternative omits an actual rule.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Leaders are determined by control-flow entry points, not list position.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.answerText: No listed option — A, B, C, and D are all correct
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.questionText: Which of the following identities for regular expressions/languages are correct? A. ∅ + L = L + ∅ = L. B. εL = Lε = L. C. ∅L = L∅ = ∅. D. ∅*L = L∅* = L.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: For any language L: ∅+L=L; εL=L; ∅L=∅; and ∅*={ε}, so ∅*L=L. Thus all four printed identities are correct, but no option lists A,B,C,D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Every offered choice omits at least one valid identity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Distinguish ∅ from ε: ∅ annihilates concatenation, whereas {ε} is its identity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: An 820-byte MTU permits 800 payload bytes, so a 1400-byte IP payload becomes 800- and 600-byte fragments; the second datagram is 620 bytes including its header (A). Routers do not retransmit lost fragments (B false). TCP at P eventually retransmits the segment (C), and only the first fragment contains the TCP ports (D false).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 assume router retransmission; option 3 says the TCP port can be obtained from a noninitial fragment.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Routers fragment, end hosts reassemble, and TCP endpoints recover loss.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Nagle's algorithm prevents a sender from emitting many tiny segments, while Clark's solution prevents a receiver from advertising tiny window increments. Together they address sender- and receiver-created silly-window syndrome.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Jacobson's work concerns TCP timing/congestion; piggybacking combines acknowledgements with reverse data. Neither is the standard SWS pair.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: SWS: Nagle at the sender, Clark at the receiver.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A genetic algorithm evolves a population through selection, crossover, and mutation, retaining fitter candidates across generations. It directly mimics biological evolution.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The neural-network alternatives learn weights or representations but do not use population-level evolutionary operators.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Evolutionary optimization = population + fitness + selection + variation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Logistic regression directly models class probability. Backpropagation trains neural networks used for classification. Linear regression predicts continuous values, while forward propagation is an inference computation rather than a separate learning algorithm. Thus A and B apply.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options containing C treat continuous regression as classification; those containing D misclassify an execution phase as a learning algorithm.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Backpropagation learns parameters; forward propagation computes outputs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: AND, OR, and NOR have linearly separable truth-table classes, so a single perceptron layer can implement them. XOR is not linearly separable and requires a hidden layer or transformed features.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 omit a separable operation; option 4 incorrectly includes XOR.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: A single perceptron represents a linear decision boundary.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Planar graph→4-colorable (A–III); bipartite graph→2-colorable (B–IV); PERT→probabilistic model (C–I); CPM→deterministic model (D–II).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings swap graph-coloring bounds or reverse PERT and CPM.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: PERT is probabilistic; CPM deterministic; bipartite means two-colorable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: STA FFFFH uses direct addressing (A–III); MOV A,M obtains memory through HL, so indirect (B–IV); MOV A,B is register addressing (C–II); and MVI A,FFH embeds immediate data (D–I).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The other options mismatch at least one mnemonic with its operand source.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Immediate embeds data; register names a register; direct embeds an address; indirect obtains it from a register pair.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Parameter-only variation suggests templates (A–III). Runtime-unknown types require a class hierarchy and dynamic polymorphism (B–I). Templates enable compile-time inlining and runtime efficiency (C–II). Recovering a derived instance through a base reference may require explicit casting (D–IV).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings confuse compile-time generic programming with runtime polymorphism.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Templates provide compile-time polymorphism; class hierarchies provide runtime polymorphism.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Kafka was created at LinkedIn (A–III); Pig supplies data-flow/ETL processing (B–I); Mahout supplies scalable machine-learning algorithms (C–IV); MapReduce is a distributed computation model/runtime (D–II).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings interchange platforms with distinct origins and purposes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Kafka: streaming; Pig: ETL; Mahout: ML; MapReduce: distributed computation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: IPC enables cooperation/computational speedup (A–II); demand paging implements virtual memory (B–IV); Banker's algorithm handles safe resource allocation (C–I); PCB is the task/process control block (D–III).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other choices confuse virtual memory with allocation or swap IPC and PCB roles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Banker→allocation; demand paging→virtual memory; PCB→process metadata; IPC→cooperation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Product complexity is a software cost-estimation factor (A–IV). Structured systems analysis develops system requirements/design (B–I). Coupling and cohesion are software-design measures (C–II). Symbolic execution is a validation technique (D–III).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Every other mapping assigns at least one concept to the wrong development activity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Coupling/cohesion assess design; symbolic execution validates program paths.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Dijkstra stores the best total source-to-current cost (A–III). Prim repeatedly stores/selects a minimum crossing edge (B–II). Warshall calculates reachability/path matrices (C–I). Kruskal finds a minimum spanning tree (D–IV).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other options confuse shortest-path, transitive-closure, and MST roles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Dijkstra: single-source shortest paths; Warshall: all-pairs reachability; Prim/Kruskal: MST.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: For A, base length 2 and each recursion adds 2, giving 2n (II). B adds 4 to base 2, giving 4n−2 (IV). C adds 6, giving 6n−4 (I). D adds 8, giving 8n−6 (III). Thus A–II, B–IV, C–I, D–III.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The alternatives mismatch the arithmetic progressions produced by recursive wrapping.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: For S→uSv|w, generated length is |w|+k(|u|+|v|).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: CDMA is a multiple-access mechanism for one shared channel (A–III). GSM combines FDMA and TDMA (B–IV). UMTS provides higher-rate mobile Internet (C–I). Wi-Fi connects devices over a wireless network (D–II).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings interchange cellular access methods, 3G service, and local wireless networking.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: CDMA separates users by codes; GSM uses frequency/time slots; UMTS is 3G; Wi-Fi is WLAN.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Text planning, sentence planning, and sentence generation are successive natural-language-generation activities, so A,B,C→II. Mapping input text into useful semantic representations is natural-language understanding, so D→I.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The other choices wrongly classify one or more generation stages as understanding.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: NLU maps language to meaning; NLG plans content and realizes it as sentences.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: General integer linear programming is NP-hard and no polynomial-time algorithm is known (under the standard P≠NP assumption), so A is treated as true in this context. PERT emphasizes schedule/time analysis under uncertain activity durations, so B is also true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 reject one or both intended textbook statements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: ILP is computationally hard; PERT is primarily time/schedule oriented.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The processor first fetches the instruction, decodes its opcode and operands, and then executes it. With B=Fetch, A=Decode, C=Execute, the sequence is B–A–C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Every other sequence decodes or executes an instruction before it has been fetched.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Instruction cycle: fetch → decode → execute.
