sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A do-while loop initializes its control state (C), executes the body at least once (B), and tests the condition afterward (D). Thus C–B–D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options beginning with D incorrectly test before the first body execution; option 3 inserts the irrelevant ‘commenting’ item.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Do-while is post-test: initialize → body → condition.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: DBSCAN first finds ε-neighborhoods and identifies core points (B). For an unassigned core it creates a cluster (D), recursively expands all density-connected points (A), then continues through remaining unvisited points (C). Hence B–D–A–C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other orders create a cluster before identifying cores or postpone expansion until after unrelated points.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: DBSCAN: neighborhood/core test → create cluster → density expansion → continue scan.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: After power-on, firmware in ROM runs and loads the boot block (C). That loads the bootstrap loader from disk (A), which loads the OS kernel into memory (B). Thus C–A–B.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other sequences require disk or kernel code before firmware has initialized the boot process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Firmware starts the chain; bootloader locates and loads the kernel.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.answerText: No listed option — both Assertion A and Reason R are incorrect
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Cohesion measures how strongly the responsibilities within one module belong together; it is not compactness or timely completion, so A is false. Connection to other modules/outside world describes coupling, not cohesion, so R is also false. No option states that both are false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–2 call both true; option 3 calls A true; option 4 calls R true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: High cohesion is internal unity; low coupling is weak inter-module dependence.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Kruskal processes edges by nondecreasing weight. Here A(a-b) and B(d-f) have weight 1; C(b-f) and D(d-c) have weight 2; E(d-e) has weight 3. Sequence B,A,C,E,D puts weight-3 E before weight-2 D and therefore cannot be the sorted addition order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The other sequences keep both weight-1 edges before the weight-2 pair and E last; ties may appear in either order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Kruskal permits arbitrary tie order but never processes a heavier edge before an available lighter edge.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Eliminate ε-productions first because doing so may create unit productions. Then eliminate unit productions because their removal may expose useless symbols. Removing useless symbols last avoids reintroducing them. Thus ε → unit → useless is the safest order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other orders can require repeating an earlier cleanup after a later transformation creates new unit or useless productions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Order grammar transformations so later steps clean artifacts introduced by earlier ones.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Bottom-up OSI order is physical transmission of bits (B), data-link framing (A), network-layer subnet control (D), transport connection/datagram service (E), and application/user interface (C). Hence B–A–D–E–C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other sequences place framing before physical signaling or user-interface handling below network/transport functions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → upper/user layers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: ANNs are machine-learning models inspired by nervous systems, scale to large data, and multilayer networks can learn useful features rather than requiring every feature to be hand-programmed. A–D are all correct at the intended level.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 incorrectly reject genuine ANN characteristics.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Representation learning is a central advantage of modern neural networks.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Assume Q is guilty. P's ‘Q did it’ is true; Q's ‘S did it’ is false; R's ‘I did not do it’ is true; and S's ‘Q lied’ is true because Q's statement is false. Exactly one statement is false, satisfying the condition.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Testing P, R, or S as guilty produces more than one false statement or otherwise violates the condition.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: For finite truth puzzles, test each candidate and count false statements explicitly.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Raster-scan CRT systems form pictures as dot samples traced line by line, the same scanning principle used by television displays. Both A and R are true, and R directly explains why a television monitor can serve as a raster display.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The remaining choices deny either a true statement or the explanatory connection.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Raster displays refresh a pixel grid in horizontal scan lines.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A process at the back of a queue of 20 waits while the other 19 processes each receive one 4 ms quantum. Maximum wait before its first CPU turn is 19×4=76 ms, ignoring context-switch overhead as the question does.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: 80 ms counts the waiting process's own quantum; 84 ms adds an extra quantum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Initial round-robin wait with N ready processes is at most (N−1)q.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The LP relaxation of weighted vertex cover gives a lower bound on the integer optimum, so Statement I is correct. But it minimizes Σw(v)x(v) subject to x(u)+x(v)≥1 and 0≤x(v)≤1. Statement II says maximize, which is wrong.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Any choice accepting Statement II uses the wrong optimization direction; option 2 also rejects the valid relaxation principle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: A minimization problem's relaxation provides a lower bound by minimizing over a larger feasible set.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Without keepalive, an idle client blocked in receive may not learn that the reboot erased server state, so it can wait indefinitely (I true). If it later sends, the rebooted host has no matching TCP connection and responds with RST, not FIN (II false).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options accepting II confuse abrupt loss of state with an orderly TCP close, which uses FIN.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Unknown post-reboot TCP segments elicit reset; FIN represents graceful shutdown.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A /16 block contains 2^(32−16)=65,536 addresses. Dividing it into 1024=2^10 equal subnets leaves 2^(16−10)=2^6=64 addresses per subnet.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: 32 and 16 borrow too many host bits; 128 borrows too few.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Addresses per equal subnet = original block size ÷ number of subnets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The supplied block 130.56.0.0/16 has the fixed network prefix represented by its first two octets, 130.56. Creating 2^10 subnets extends the CIDR prefix from /16 to /26, but among the printed textual-prefix choices only 130.56 is meaningful.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: 136.0, 136.255, and 136.56.255 are outside the granted 130.56.0.0/16 block.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: The original address prefix is 130.56; the subnetted CIDR prefix length is /26.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Each subnet contains 64 consecutive addresses. The first begins at 130.56.0.0 and spans offsets 0 through 63, so its last address is 130.56.0.63.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Other choices extend far beyond a 64-address /26 subnet or leave the granted block.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: A 64-address subnet advances in blocks of 64: .0–.63, .64–.127, .128–.191, .192–.255.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The last /24-sized octet region is 130.56.255.x, and its last /26 block begins at the final multiple of 64, 192. It therefore runs from 130.56.255.192 through 130.56.255.255.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The other ranges either begin at the first subnet, cross the block boundary, or do not contain exactly 64 addresses.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: The final /26 within any /24 is .192–.255.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.answerText: No listed option — the subnet mask is 255.255.255.192 (/26)
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Creating 1024=2^10 subnets from /16 borrows ten bits, yielding /26. A /26 mask has 26 leading ones: 255.255.255.192. Every printed option incorrectly begins with part of the network address rather than a valid mask.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: A subnet mask must be contiguous one bits followed by zero bits; none of the displayed 130.56... values is the /26 mask.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: New prefix = old prefix + log2(number of equal subnets).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: State q1 scans right after marking an a as X1. On the first unmatched c it must mark that c as X2 and move left into q2 so the head can return toward the corresponding X1. Therefore M1=(q2,X2,L).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Moving right fails to return for the next phase; entering q3 is premature; the transition is required and is not an error.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: The machine pairs each a with a c by marking c as X2, then returning left.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: M2 is the q0 transition on X1. To search past already marked a symbols, the logical move is (q0,X1,R). None of options 1–3 gives that transition, so the table entry represented by the supplied alternatives is an error entry/no matching listed move.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The listed q1/q2 moves switch to a later matching phase instead of retaining q0 while skipping a processed marker.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: When scanning for the next unmarked symbol, remain in the scan state while passing its markers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: M3 is the q3 transition on an unmarked a. State q3 is scanning right to find the next b, so it should preserve a, remain in q3, and move right: (q3,a,R). None of options 1–3 states this, making option 4 the only applicable choice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The listed moves write X1 or jump to q1/q4, which would corrupt or prematurely change the scan phase.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: A scan state normally preserves irrelevant symbols and continues in the same direction/state.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: After q4 marks a d as Y2, q5 moves left across processed symbols until it reaches the Y1 that marks the paired b. It must then enter q3 and continue left so q3 can locate the next unmarked b. Thus M4=(q3,Y1,L).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Remaining in q5 or q4 does not restart the b-search; moving right returns toward the already processed d side.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: The transition at the left marker changes from the return state to the next-symbol search state.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: M5 is q4 reading X2. While seeking an unmatched d, q4 must pass the X2 markers used for c symbols without changing them, so it remains q4 and moves right: (q4,X2,R).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Switching to q5 starts the leftward return before a d has been marked; moving left likewise reverses too early.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Processed markers between the current head position and target are skipped in the active scan state.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: P→Q is equivalent to ¬P∨Q, to its contrapositive ¬Q→¬P, and to ¬(P∧¬Q). Option 4 is ¬Q→P, equivalent to Q∨P, and is not generally equivalent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 are standard implication identities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: P→Q fails only when P is true and Q false.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Assign each of seven labeled people one of three floors, requiring every floor nonempty. Inclusion-exclusion gives 3^7−3·2^7+3·1^7=2187−384+3=1806.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: 2187 includes assignments leaving floors empty; 343 uses only two choices per person incorrectly; 1844 is not the surjection count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Onto functions from n people to k floors: Σ(−1)^i C(k,i)(k−i)^n.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The multinomial coefficient of x²y³z² in (x+y+z)^7 is 7!/(2!3!2!). Choose the two x positions and then three y positions: C(7,2)C(5,3)=210.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other products choose the wrong number of remaining positions and do not equal the multinomial coefficient.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Coefficient of x^a y^b z^c is (a+b+c)!/(a!b!c!).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: To avoid six equal grades as long as possible, place five students in each of seven grade categories: 7·5=35. The 36th student forces some category to contain at least six by the pigeonhole principle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: 7, 13, and 14 do not exhaust the possible distribution of five per grade.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: To force r objects in one of k boxes, need k(r−1)+1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The listed patterns all have sign bit 1, so none represents positive 28. The apparent intended value is −28: +28 is 0000 0000 0001 1100; invert and add one to get 1111 1111 1110 0100, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Taken literally, positive 28 is absent. Options 1,2,4 encode other negative values.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: For −x in fixed-width two's complement, invert +x and add one.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Minterms 4,5,6,7 form the implicant A′B. Minterms 3,7,11,15 form CD. Their union is exactly the given set, so F=A′B+CD.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other products cover minterms outside the set or fail to cover required minterms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: A four-cell K-map group eliminates the two variables that change within it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: For a T flip-flop, Q+=T⊕Q. From the diagram Tp=R, Tq=¬P, and Tr=¬Q. Starting 000 gives 011; applying the equations again gives 101; once more gives 000. Thus the next states are 011,101,000.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other sequences do not satisfy the simultaneous next-state equations from the feedback wiring.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Evaluate all synchronous flip-flop next states from the old state, not sequentially.

sources.title: Official GATE 2021 Computer Science paper

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Four-bit two's complement spans −8 to +7. Option 2 adds 1100(−4)+1010(−6)=−10, outside the range, so overflow occurs. The stored four-bit result wraps to +6, revealing the sign error.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Option 1 gives −7, option 3 gives +7, and option 4 gives −8; all are representable.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Signed overflow occurs when same-sign operands produce an opposite-sign result.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Static binding may be decided at compile time; relocation makes absolute addresses known at load time; dynamic dispatch may choose a target at runtime. Calls to internal functions are normally resolvable within the compilation unit rather than waiting for external symbol resolution at link time, making statement 3 the incorrect one.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Statements 1,2,4 correspond to recognized compile/load/runtime binding stages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Link time primarily resolves references between separately compiled modules.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The final initializer value 1 occupies arr[2][3][1] using zero-based indices. Pointer form *(*(*(arr+2)+3)+1) dereferences exactly that element.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 use out-of-range indices 3/4/2 for dimensions [3][4][2]; option 3 adds pointer expressions instead of indexing one scalar.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: a[i][j][k] equals *(*(*(a+i)+j)+k).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Using different derived objects through a common base interface relies on virtual dispatch: the actual overridden method is selected from the runtime object type. This is runtime polymorphism.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Compile-time/static polymorphism refers to overloading or templates; ‘derived polymorphism’ is not the standard category.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Base reference + virtual method + derived object = runtime polymorphism.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A Bézier curve interpolates its first and last control points. Intermediate control points generally do not lie on the curve; they determine endpoint derivatives and overall order/shape.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Ordinary B-splines generally do not interpolate both endpoint control vertices without special knot choices; the other names do not describe this control-polygon property.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Bézier endpoints lie on the curve; inner control points pull its shape.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Relational union returns every tuple occurring in either compatible input relation, eliminating duplicates under set semantics. It therefore combines the rows of both relations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Difference removes shared/right-side rows, Cartesian product combines columns across tuple pairs, and projection selects columns.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Union combines rows; projection selects columns.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: P,Q,R,S never occur on any FD right side, so every key must contain them. Their closure derives T from PQS, V from PQ, W and X from V, Y from R, and U from Q—every attribute. Removing any of P,Q,R,S loses that attribute, so {P,Q,R,S} is a candidate key.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other sets omit P or S, which cannot be derived; hence their closures are incomplete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Attributes absent from all FD right sides must appear in every candidate key.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A transaction log is critical recovery data and is written frequently. RAID 1 mirrors every log write, providing redundancy with no parity-update penalty and allowing recovery if one disk fails.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: RAID 0 has no redundancy; RAID 2/3 use specialized striping/parity and are unsuitable for small frequent log writes compared with mirroring.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: For critical write-heavy logs, simple mirroring prioritizes reliability and write behavior.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: All tests along one root-to-leaf branch must hold together, so they are joined by AND. Different branches offer alternative sufficient paths to a class, so branch rules are joined by OR.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other choices reverse conjunction within a path or disjunction among alternative paths.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Decision-tree path = conjunction; set of paths to a class = disjunction.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The protection model is represented by an access matrix whose rows are protection domains, columns are objects, and cells contain permitted operations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Owner/domain/global matrix are not the standard general model.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Access matrix unifies ACL (columns) and capability-list (rows) views.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The heap stores memory obtained and released dynamically while the process runs, such as objects created by malloc/new.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Temporary automatic data normally uses the stack; priority, queue pointers, and CPU accounting belong in the process control block.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Stack: automatic call data; heap: runtime dynamic allocation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: With four empty frames, 1,2,3,4 cause four faults. 5 causes a fifth, optimally evicting 2 because it is never reused. Then 3,4,1 hit. References 6,7,8 each introduce a new page and cause faults 6,7,8; the final 7,8 hit. Total: 8.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Counts 5–7 omit one or more compulsory/new-page faults.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: OPT evicts the page whose next use is farthest away, or never occurs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: One-to-one maps each user thread to a kernel thread and permits the greatest parallelism. Many-to-many multiplexes user threads over multiple kernel threads and gives intermediate concurrency. Many-to-one maps all user threads to one kernel thread, so one blocking call blocks all and no multicore parallelism occurs. Order: one-to-one > many-to-many > many-to-one.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Option 1 ranks many-to-one above many-to-many; option 3 reverses the ordering.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Kernel-visible schedulable entities determine achievable thread concurrency.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Unit tests before implementation (test-first development), readable visual layout, and refactoring after an initial pass are recognized coding practices. Self-documenting code reduces comment burden but does not eliminate necessary program/API/design documentation, so statement 4 is not a sound principle as worded.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 improve correctness, readability, or maintainability.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Clear code complements documentation; it does not replace all documentation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Risk exposure is expected loss: multiply the probability P that the risk occurs by the loss L if it occurs. Therefore RE=P×L.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Division, addition, or adding an arbitrary constant does not compute an expected monetary/impact value.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Expected impact = probability × consequence.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Black-box wrapping places an adapter around a reusable/legacy component using only its externally visible interface, without modifying its internal design. This is the standard component-wrapping technique intended here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The alternative color terms are not the named wrapping technique used in the source's component-reuse taxonomy.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: A wrapper adapts an existing component's interface while treating its implementation as a black box.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Cyclomatic complexity V(G)=E−N+2P measures the number of linearly independent control-flow paths. It guides basis-path testing by indicating how many independent paths require tests.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: It is not a direct count of source statements, defects, or literal loop cycles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Cyclomatic complexity = independent paths through the control-flow graph.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The intended sentence is that left and right subtree heights do not differ by more than one. That is the defining balance condition of a height-balanced (AVL-style) tree.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: A generic BST need not be balanced; ‘balanced binary tree’ is less precise, and weight balance compares subtree sizes/weights rather than heights.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Height balance requires |height(left)−height(right)|≤1 at every node.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Compression techniques encode digital audio/video efficiently. FFTs decompose signals into frequency components, so compression analysis such as transform coding operates in the frequency domain.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Encryption protects confidentiality rather than reducing representation size; time-domain is inconsistent with the stated FFT clue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: FFT converts time-domain samples into a frequency-domain representation useful for transform compression.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Huffman's 2-tree algorithm repeatedly combines the two smallest weights: 4+5=9, 8+9=17, 15+16=31, 17+25=42, 31+42=73. The minimum weighted path length equals the sum of merge weights: 9+17+31+42+73=172.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: 73 is only the root weight (sum of leaves), not the weighted external path length; 77 and 231 do not follow the optimal merges.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: For a Huffman tree, WPL equals the sum of all pairwise merge totals.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: There are C(n,2) key pairs. Universality bounds each pair's collision probability by 1/m=1/n². By the union bound, Pr(any collision)≤C(n,2)/n²=(n−1)/(2n)<1/2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The calculation gives a strict upper bound below one-half, not above it; it is not always below one-fifth.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Union-bound all pair-collision events: C(n,2)/m.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: For the CFL pumping decomposition uvwxy, the two pumped pieces cannot both be empty: |vx|≥1. Therefore their minimum combined length is 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Zero is forbidden; neither 2 nor pumping length n is a required minimum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: CFL pumping lemma requires |vx|≥1 and |vwx|≤p.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A context-sensitive (Type-1) grammar uses noncontracting productions, so it is ε-free except for the conventional optional S→ε case when S never appears on a right-hand side.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Unrestricted and context-free grammars may contain ε-productions; regular grammars may also represent ε under standard conventions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Type-1 productions do not shorten strings, hence no ordinary ε-productions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The L in LR means the input is scanned from left to right. The R describes constructing a rightmost derivation in reverse, not reading from the right.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1,3,4 contradict the definition of LR parsing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: LR = Left-to-right scan, Rightmost derivation in reverse.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A valid n-state automaton has exactly one initial state. With a nonempty accepted language it needs at least one final state; dead and unreachable states may be zero. Maxima are one initial, n−1 dead, n−1 unreachable, and n final states. This matches option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other choices require dead/unreachable states unnecessarily or allow zero final states despite a nonempty language.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Initial count is exactly one; reachability/deadness are optional; all states may be final.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: ARP broadcasts a request for a known IPv4 address on the local link; the owner replies with its MAC address.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: RARP performs the historical reverse mapping, DHCP assigns configuration, and UDP is a transport protocol.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Known IP → local MAC uses ARP.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Append the stated three-bit CRC remainder 110 to dataword 111111, producing codeword 111111110.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other strings do not consist of the unchanged dataword followed by the given remainder.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: CRC codeword = dataword || remainder.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: AckNo 6 is cumulative and announces 6 as the next expected sequence number, so packets through 5 are acknowledged. The first outstanding pointer Sf advances from 3 to 6; Sn remains 7 because no new send is described.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: 7 is the unchanged next-to-send pointer, while 4 and 5 under-advance the cumulative acknowledgement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: In Go-Back-N, a cumulative ACK moves the send base to its AckNo.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A code with minimum distance d_min can detect up to d_min−1 bit errors. To detect S errors, require d_min≥S+1; the minimum is S+1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Distances S or smaller cannot guarantee detecting all S-error patterns.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Detection capability = d_min−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: With a consistent heuristic, A* graph search is complete (under standard positive-cost/finite-branching conditions) and optimal. Consistency ensures f-values do not decrease along paths and closed nodes need not be reopened.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Greedy best-first and DFS are not generally optimal; BFS is optimal only for equal step costs and does not use h.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Consistent h implies admissibility and monotone A* f-values.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Generalized modus ponens packages matching/unification and implication application into one inference rule for first-order Horn-style knowledge bases.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: ‘Ponens’ alone names the propositional rule; clauses and variables are representations, not the requested inference-capturing process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Generalized modus ponens extends modus ponens with substitutions/unification.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Fuzzy logic permits truth degrees across an interval such as [0,1], rather than restricting every proposition to exactly true or false. It is therefore many-valued logic.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Two-valued, crisp, and binary logics allow only two membership/truth states.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Fuzzy truth is graded, not merely 0 or 1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The 4Ws canvas—Who, What, Where, Why—is used at the start of an AI/problem-solving project to define stakeholders, context, and the problem's boundaries. It belongs to problem scoping.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Data acquisition, modeling, and evaluation occur after the problem has been scoped.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: 4Ws clarifies the problem before data or model work begins.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A group cannot be empty because it must contain an identity, so A is false. If g²=g, cancellation gives g=e, so only the identity is idempotent (B). An isomorphism from a group to itself is an automorphism (C). If every element is self-inverse, (ab)⁻¹=ab but also b⁻¹a⁻¹=ba, hence ab=ba (D).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 4 accept false A; option 3 omits true D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: A group has an identity; exponent-two groups are abelian.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Ordered binary-tree shapes and BSTs are counted by Catalan C_n=(2n)!/((n+1)!n!). Labeling every node multiplies by n!, giving (2n)!/(n+1)! for A. Thus A and B are true. C's extra factorial is wrong. A simple labeled graph independently chooses any of C(n,2) edges, giving 2^[n(n−1)/2], so D is true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Choices containing C use an incorrect unlabeled-tree formula or omit one of A,B,D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Catalan counts binary-tree shapes/BSTs; arbitrary simple graphs count edge subsets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: R lacks (2,2), so it is not reflexive. It contains (1,2) without (2,1), so it is not symmetric. Since (3,1) and (1,2) are present but (3,2) is absent, transitivity fails.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 claim at least one property contradicted by these explicit counterexamples.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Disprove a relation property with one missing required pair.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: De Morgan gives F'=X'Y'Z'+XY'+YZ' (D). This simplifies to Z'+XY' (B): when Z'=1 the three terms cover both Y values, while for Z=1 only XY' remains. C=(X+Z')(Y'+Z') also simplifies to Z'+XY'. A incorrectly complements literals without changing the product of sums into a sum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options containing A accept an invalid De Morgan transformation; B,C,D are equivalent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Complement a product into a sum of complemented factors, then use absorption/consensus.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The CPU directly accesses primary memory, not disk/secondary storage, so A is false. Primary memory holds active instructions/data (B), and secondary storage provides persistent/backup storage (C). Memory is hierarchical, not single-level, so D is false. B and C only are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other options include false A or D or omit B/C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: CPU-addressable primary memory and I/O-accessed secondary storage are distinct hierarchy levels.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Inheritance directly supplies a shared interface and inherited implementation, reducing redundant declarations/implementation (A,C), and supports runtime polymorphism through substitutable derived objects (B). Dynamic binding is the dispatch mechanism used to realize runtime polymorphism rather than an independent inheritance benefit in this classification. Hence A,B,C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 omit A, B, or C while treating mechanism D as a separate benefit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Inheritance promotes reuse and subtype polymorphism; dynamic binding implements virtual dispatch.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.answerText: No listed option — A, B, and E are valid C++ string constructions
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: `string s1;` default-constructs and `string s2(s1);` copy-constructs. A single integer or character in braces does not match a valid `std::string` constructor, so C and D fail. `string s5{7,'a'};` matches the count-and-character constructor and creates seven a characters. The valid set A,B,E is absent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Each listed combination either includes invalid C/D or omits valid E.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: The repeated-character string constructor requires both count and character.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: CSS is cascading: multiple sheets can stack, and later/more-specific rules can override inherited corporate rules. HR can therefore load the corporate sheet and a small department-specific override sheet. B and C are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: A and D deny the two cascade behaviors the scenario relies on.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: The CSS cascade combines style sources and resolves conflicts by origin, importance, specificity, and order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: After Write(A), account A is reduced by 50, but B has not yet been increased. The invariant A+B is temporarily 50 too small. Atomic transaction execution permits such an intermediate state internally, provided it is not exposed/committed and is restored on failure.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The transaction need not abort or roll back during normal progress, and the intermediate database is not always consistent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Consistency is required at transaction boundaries; intermediate steps may temporarily violate an invariant.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: FD2 (dept→stuid) and FD3 (stuid→clsroll) are jointly recoverable after removing them because dept→clsroll (FD5), clsroll→stuid (FD4), and stuid→dept (FD1) form the needed cycle. FD5 alone is also redundant since dept→stuid→clsroll via FD2 and FD3. Thus proposals A and C identify removable dependencies; B does not.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Removing FD1 and FD5 loses the ability to derive stuid→dept, so B is not a valid redundancy removal.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Test an FD for redundancy by computing its left-side closure using the other dependencies.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A tiny quantum creates many context switches (A). Turnaround is not monotonic in quantum, so increasing it need not improve the average (B). Choosing a quantum large enough for most next CPU bursts lets those processes finish without another wait and can improve turnaround (C). All are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one correct scheduling observation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Quantum size trades responsiveness/context-switch overhead against completion per turn.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: SRTF schedule: A runs 0–3; B 3–5 and finishes; A 5–8 finishes (D's arrival at 7 leaves A with 1); C 8–12; E 12–15; D 15–21. Turnarounds are A=8, B=2, C=7, D=14, E=5. Average=(8+2+7+14+5)/5=7.2 ms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other averages result from ignoring preemption/arrival times or using waiting time instead of turnaround.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Turnaround = completion−arrival; SRTF always runs the least remaining arrived job.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: On a page fault, locate the desired page on disk (C). If no free frame exists, invoke replacement to free one (A). Read the page into the frame and update tables (D), then restart the trapped instruction (B). Thus C–A–D–B.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other sequences restart before loading or try to load before choosing/freeing a frame.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Locate → select/free frame → load/update → restart.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Testing is a substantial lifecycle activity and should be planned from the beginning. Its labor, environments, automation, and defect-repair work contribute materially to total development cost. Both statements are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 incorrectly deny early planning or testing's cost impact.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Test planning begins early even though execution spans later stages.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.answerText: No listed option — only B (number of lines of code) need not be assessed during unit testing
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Unit testing should examine algorithmic behavior/performance where relevant, error-handling paths, and independent execution paths. Raw line count is a size metric, not a unit-test correctness assessment. The printed choices all contain three items and none gives B alone.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Each listed set incorrectly excludes two genuine unit-testing concerns along with B.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Unit tests target behavior, paths, boundaries, and error handling—not source-line count.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.answerText: No listed option — A, B, C, and D all correctly describe the four AVL imbalance cases
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: LL is insertion in left-of-left; RR right-of-right; LR right-of-left; RL left-of-right. These are precisely the four standard positional cases. Every statement is correct, but each option lists only two.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: All offered subsets omit two correct case descriptions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Outer cases LL/RR need one rotation; inner cases LR/RL need two.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.questionText: For Hamiltonian problems, assess: A. Hamiltonian cycle is NP-complete. B. Every odd-order undirected bipartite graph is Hamiltonian. C. Hamiltonian path cannot be solved in polynomial time on a DAG. D. The cube G³ of every connected graph G with at least three vertices is Hamiltonian. E. If any NP-complete problem is polynomial-time solvable, then P=NP.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A is true. B is false because a bipartite Hamiltonian cycle alternates partitions and therefore has even order. C is false because longest/Hamiltonian path in a DAG is solvable by dynamic programming in topological order. D is the graph-cube Hamiltonicity theorem, and E follows from NP-completeness. Thus A,D,E.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1,2,4 include false B/C or omit true D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Hamiltonian path is hard generally but polynomial on DAGs; bipartite Hamilton cycles require equal partitions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Classical divide-and-conquer matrix multiplication performs eight n/2 multiplications plus Θ(n²) additions: T(n)=8T(n/2)+Θ(n²) (C). Master theorem has a=8,b=2, n^(log_b a)=n³, so T(n)=Θ(n³) (A), and the theorem applies (E).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: B gives the wrong asymptotic time; D is not the correct subproblem recurrence.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Eight half-size products preserve cubic time; Strassen reduces the number to seven.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A's sole accepting state loops on both symbols. B and C always retain an accepting q0 path for every prefix. D also has accepting q0 looping on both symbols; q1,q2 are unreachable and irrelevant. Hence all four accept every string over {a,b}, including ε.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one automaton that still has an accepting path for every input.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: For an NFA, one accepting computation suffices; unreachable states do not change the language.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Syntax-directed translation attaches semantic actions/attributes to grammar productions and supports type checking, syntax-tree construction, and intermediate three-address-code generation. Token recognition is the lexical analyzer's job, normally specified by regular expressions/finite automata, not SDT. Thus A,B,C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options containing D confuse lexical analysis with syntax-directed semantic processing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Lexer recognizes tokens; parser plus semantic actions performs syntax-directed translation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: For P, routes through X,Y,Z cost 3+7=10, 2+6=8, 5+5=10, so R stores 8 via Y: C true, A false. For Q the costs are 3+4=7, 2+6=8, 5+8=13, so R stores 7 via X: B true, D false. Thus B and C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The alternatives use nonminimum costs or the wrong next hop.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Distance-vector update takes min_neighbor[cost to neighbor + neighbor's advertised cost].

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: 12.20.164.0/22 covers third octets 164–167 (B). The /23 routes 168–169 and 170–171 combine as 12.20.168.0/22 (D). Together B and D exactly cover all listed subnets without unrelated ranges.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: 164.0 is not aligned for /21 or /20 and those summaries include addresses outside the given set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Aggregate only equal adjacent aligned blocks; /22 advances by four in the third octet.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Fragment payloads are 800 and 600 bytes, so the second datagram is 620 bytes: A true. Routers do not retransmit fragments, so B false. TCP at P retransmits after loss, so C true. Only the first fragment contains the TCP header/port, so D false. The not-true statements are B,D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other options mark A or C false even though their fragmentation/TCP behavior is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: IP routers fragment but do not recover loss; TCP endpoints do.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Neural networks are machine-learning models inspired by nervous systems, can use large datasets, and learn internal features instead of requiring all features to be hand-coded. All A–D are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit genuine ANN properties.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Neural networks combine brain-inspired connectivity with data-driven representation learning.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A fixed rule base learns statically, does not automatically incorporate later data changes, and does not improve from feedback after construction unless rules are explicitly revised. A,B,C are all drawbacks.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 deny one or more characteristic limitations of hand-authored rule systems.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Rule systems execute explicit knowledge; adaptive learning requires a separate update mechanism.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Linear programs use the simplex algorithm (A–III); predicate logic is first-order logic (B–IV); inorder is a tree traversal (C–II); Prim solves MST (D–I).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings swap algorithms with unrelated problem classes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Simplex→LP, Prim→MST, inorder→tree, predicate→first-order logic.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: 8085 DAA is arithmetic (A–IV), LXI moves immediate 16-bit data (B–III), RST is a restart/interrupt instruction (C–II), and JMP controls program flow (D–I).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other options misclassify data movement, interrupts, or jumps.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Classify instructions by their primary architectural effect.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A camera supplies a 2-D array input (A–III). LCD is transmissive (B–I). A flat-bed scanner produces a binary/digital image (C–II). Ink-jet printing consumes a 1-D stream/array input as listed (D–IV).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings interchange capture, display, scanning, and printing devices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Camera/scanner are image inputs; LCD/inkjet are display/output technologies.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.answerText: No listed option — correct matching is A–III, B–IV, C–I, D–II
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Timestamp ordering ensures deadlock freedom (A–III). Strict 2PL holds write locks to commit and prevents cascading rollback (B–IV). Ordinary 2PL obtains locks during its growing first phase (C–I). The lock point is the transaction's final lock acquisition (D–II). This exact mapping is absent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Each offered option swaps the 2PL growing-phase description and lock-point definition or misassigns timestamp/strict 2PL.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: 2PL lock point = last lock acquired; strictness prevents cascading aborts.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: In enhanced clock replacement, (0,0) is best (A–III); (0,1) is unused but dirty and must be written (B–I); (1,0) is likely reused soon but clean (C–IV); (1,1) is likely reused and also dirty (D–II).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other options confuse reference and modify-bit meanings.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Prefer unreferenced clean pages; dirty pages incur write-back.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Condition coverage is white-box (A–III); equivalence partitioning is black-box (B–I); volume testing is performance testing (C–IV); alpha testing is a system-level acceptance activity (D–II).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other mappings cross structural, functional, performance, and system testing categories.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Condition coverage sees code; equivalence partitions see input behavior.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: 8-Queens uses backtracking (A–IV); closest pair uses divide-and-conquer (B–I); string edit distance uses dynamic programming (C–II); fractional knapsack uses a greedy method (D–III).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other options assign the standard problems to mismatched paradigms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Recognize canonical examples for each algorithm-design paradigm.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: LL begins with stack `$S` and input `w$` (A–III), ending `$,$` (B–II). LR begins with stack `$` and input `w$` (C–I), and after acceptance has `$S,$` (D–IV).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings confuse whether the start symbol is initially pushed (LL) or produced by reductions (LR).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Predictive LL starts with S; bottom-up LR starts with only the end marker.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Physical transmits raw bits (A–III); Data Link frames them (B–I); Network controls/routes the subnet (C–II); Transport supplies connection-oriented or connectionless end-to-end service (D–IV).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other options shift functions to adjacent incorrect OSI layers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Bits→Physical, frames→Data Link, packets/routing→Network, end-to-end delivery→Transport.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Regression, KNN, and decision trees learn from labeled targets and are supervised (A,B,D→II). Clustering discovers unlabeled groups and is unsupervised (C→I).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other mappings classify regression/decision trees as unsupervised or clustering as supervised.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Labels distinguish supervised prediction from unsupervised structure discovery.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Each connected component with v_i vertices needs at least v_i−1 edges. Summing across k components gives Σ(v_i−1)=n−k. A forest attains this bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other formulas exceed or do not match the spanning-forest lower bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: A forest with n vertices and k components has exactly n−k edges.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A fetch copies PC to MAR (D,C), reads Memory (A), places the word in MBR (B), then transfers it to IR (E). Order D,C,A,B,E.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other sequences access memory before establishing MAR or load IR before MBR.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: PC→MAR; memory→MBR; MBR→IR.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.answerText: No listed option — correct ascending order is D, B, A, C
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Worst cases are binary search D=Θ(log n), linear search B=Θ(n), merge sort A=Θ(n log n), bubble sort C=Θ(n²). Ascending order is D,B,A,C, but no option states it.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Every choice places linear search before binary search or otherwise misorders the growth rates.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: log n < n < n log n < n².

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Conflict edges are T2→T4 and T2→T3 on X; T4→T3 on X; and T3→T1 on Y. The precedence order forced by this acyclic graph is T2,T4,T3,T1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other serial orders violate at least one observed conflicting-operation order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Build a precedence edge Ti→Tj when Ti's conflicting operation occurs first.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: C-LOOK moving upward serves 123,197,198,250,280, then wraps to45 and serves87. Movement is (280−101)+(280−45)+(87−45)=179+235+42=456 cylinders.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Other totals omit or mismeasure the circular wrap or an ordered segment.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: C-LOOK reverses logically by wrapping from highest request directly to lowest request.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The incremental model delivers the system in a sequence of usable releases. A small but functional core can therefore be designed, implemented, and delivered first; later increments add features. This makes it particularly suitable when a working core product is needed quickly.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Option 1 better characterizes a straightforward plan-driven approach and is not the distinctive advantage asked for. Team size alone does not make the model best, so option 3 is too absolute. Incremental development is widely used commercially, making option 4 false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Incremental development supplies useful software early and grows it release by release.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Using the least available edge cost as the best-first choice: from A choose C (2 rather than F at 7); from C choose F (3 rather than B at 10); from F choose D (1 rather than E at 6); and from D choose E (2 rather than Z at 7). Thus the listed node order before reaching Z is A→C→F→D→E.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other sequences skip a currently better frontier choice: E is not selected immediately after C, F should precede E, and D cannot be preferred to F at the first relevant comparison.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Best-first search repeatedly expands the most promising available node according to its evaluation value.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: In conventional regular-expression notation, union (| or +) has the lowest precedence, concatenation has the next higher precedence, and Kleene star (*) has the highest precedence. The required low-to-high order is therefore Union, Concatenation, Star.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Every other option places star too low or places union above concatenation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Regular-expression precedence is union < concatenation < closure/star.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: RSA key generation proceeds as follows: choose distinct primes p and q (D); compute n=pq (B); compute φ(n)=(p−1)(q−1) (E); select e relatively prime to φ(n) (A); and compute d such that ed≡1 mod φ(n) (C). Hence D-B-E-A-C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The alternatives try to compute n, φ(n), or the modular inverse before the values on which those operations depend have been chosen.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: RSA builds the modulus and totient before selecting e and finding its modular inverse d.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: A feed-forward neural network accepts information at its input layer, transforms it through one or more hidden layers, and ultimately produces an output. Sequence A correctly begins this flow as Information → Input layer → Hidden layer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Bias is a parameter applied within neurons, not an input flowing from output to hidden layers. An algorithm is not a network layer, and a normal feed-forward path does not put the output layer before the processing that produces it.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Data flows input → hidden processing → output in a standard feed-forward ANN.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Virtual memory uses secondary storage and address translation to give a process an address space that can exceed the currently installed physical RAM. It therefore increases the effective memory capacity visible to programs (Statement I), and its virtual address space may be larger than main memory (Statement II). Both are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 incorrectly deny one or both defining properties of virtual memory.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Virtual address space is an abstraction whose size is not limited to the amount of physical RAM currently resident.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Codd's expressive-equivalence result states that relational algebra and safe (domain-independent) relational calculus express the same class of database queries. Thus an algebra query can be written in calculus, and a safe calculus query can be written in algebra. Both statements are true under the standard database interpretation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The other choices deny one or both directions of this equivalence. Unrestricted, unsafe calculus expressions are excluded in the standard theorem.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Relational algebra and safe relational calculus are relationally complete and expressively equivalent.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: An integrated software-engineering product team must cover the full technical path: elicit stakeholder needs (A), specify product and post-development requirements (B), build integrated technical plans (C), translate requirements into an architecture (D), and prepare sufficiently detailed technical data for implementation (E). These are complementary lifecycle objectives, so all five statements are correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 arbitrarily omit legitimate requirements, planning, architecture, or implementation-preparation responsibilities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: An SWE-IPT integrates stakeholder needs, requirements, planning, architecture, and implementation data.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Every regular language is accepted by a finite automaton and can also be decided by a Turing machine that simulates that automaton. Likewise, every context-free language has a pushdown automaton and a Turing machine can simulate it; membership in a CFL is decidable. Therefore both A and B are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Regular and context-free languages are both strict subsets of the decidable languages, so neither assertion is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: A Turing machine can simulate weaker automata such as finite automata and pushdown automata.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Artificial neural networks form the model family used by deep-learning systems, so Assertion A is true. Backpropagation is indeed a standard method for computing gradients used to train neural networks, so Reason R is also true. However, naming one training technique does not explain why ANNs constitute the backbone/model structure of deep learning. Thus R is not the correct explanation of A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Option 1 confuses a related fact with a causal explanation, while options 3 and 4 incorrectly mark one of the two true statements false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Two statements may both be true without the reason logically explaining the assertion.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The condition 'enrolled in all prerequisites' is relational division. It is expressed by double negation: there must not exist a prerequisite p of course 324 for which there does not exist a matching enrollment e by the student. Option 3 is the intended double-NOT-EXISTS query (despite missing punctuation in the printed/OCR text).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: ANY in option 1 tests only at least one prerequisite. A scalar enrollment row cannot equal ALL different prerequisite course IDs as option 2 suggests. Double EXISTS in option 4 again establishes only at least one matching prerequisite.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: For every required item = there does not exist a required item lacking a match.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The inner query refers to s.advisor from the outer student row, so it is correlated: S1 is true and S2 false. Because advisor references the professor primary key empid, the subquery returns at most one advisor-sex value for a student. Comparing with ALL or ANY over that one value gives the same result, so S4 is true and S3 false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 3 claim the result changes, while options 3 and 4 incorrectly call the subquery uncorrelated.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: ALL and ANY coincide for a singleton subquery result; an outer-row reference makes a subquery correlated.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: To obtain the total credits offered by each department, course rows must be joined to their department by deptid=deptno, grouped by department identity and name, and aggregated with SUM(credits). Option 1 performs exactly these operations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: COUNT counts course rows rather than credits. Option 3 lacks GROUP BY and cannot legally mix department columns with the aggregate. Option 4 puts a join condition improperly in HAVING and creates a Cartesian product before grouping.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Join first, GROUP BY each department, then SUM its course credits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The department-course join belongs in WHERE, rows are then grouped by department, and the condition on the aggregate total belongs in HAVING. Option 4 has this logical SQL order and filters groups whose SUM(credits), aliased totalcredits, is greater than 40.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 try to use an aggregate result in WHERE. Option 3 delays the ordinary join predicate until HAVING and groups a Cartesian product. Although alias use in HAVING varies by SQL dialect, option 4 is clearly the intended query.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: For each student, the outer EXISTS succeeds if there is some course c with c.deptno=5 for which the inner EXISTS finds that student's enrollment in c. The query therefore selects students enrolled in at least one course offered by department 5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Universal 'all courses' logic requires double NOT EXISTS, not nested positive EXISTS. The query contains no NOT condition, so options 1 and 4 describe logic it does not perform. Option 2 also incorrectly changes existential qualification into universal qualification.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Nested positive EXISTS expresses 'there exists at least one matching course and enrollment.'

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Compute profit/weight ratios: item 5=6, item 1=4.5, item 7=3, item 6=16/9≈1.78, item 2=1, item 4≈0.64, item 3≈0.33. With capacity 15, take items 5,1,7,6 completely. Their weights are 1+2+3+9=15 and profits are 6+9+9+16=40. Therefore the optimum is 40.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The greedy ratio order exactly fills the capacity at value 40; 43, 44, and 45 cannot be obtained by replacing any selected weight with lower-ratio material.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Fractional knapsack takes items in decreasing profit-to-weight ratio.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: For capacity 25, first take items 5,1,7,6: weight 15, profit 40. Take item 2 fully: weight becomes 20 and profit 45. The remaining capacity 5 takes 5/11 of item 4, adding (5/11)×7=35/11≈3.1818. Total profit is 48.1818, represented by option 48.1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: 49 and 47.9 do not equal the ratio-greedy calculation, while 40 ignores the additional ten units of capacity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: After all higher-ratio items, fill the remaining capacity with the required fraction of the next item.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: The standard fractional-knapsack procedure orders n items by profit/weight ratio, which takes Θ(n log n), and then scans them in Θ(n). For n=8, n log₂n=8×3=24, so the listed form is O(24). The added item's numerical profit and weight do not change this asymptotic step count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: O(8) counts only the final scan and ignores ordering; O(64) is quadratic; O(56) corresponds to n(n−1) comparisons rather than the standard sorting bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Fractional knapsack is dominated by sorting ratios: O(n log n).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: Fractional knapsack has the greedy-choice property: choosing the greatest available profit per unit weight can never be improved by first taking a lower-ratio portion. Sorting by that ratio and filling capacity greedily therefore produces an optimum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: Dynamic programming is standard for 0/1 knapsack, while divide-and-conquer and backtracking are unnecessary for the divisible-item version.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: Divisibility makes the locally best profit/weight choice globally optimal.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.explanation: In 0/1 knapsack each item is indivisible: it is either selected completely (1) or excluded (0). Fractional knapsack permits taking any required fraction of an item. Option 4 states this defining distinction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.whyOthersFail: The two models do not always yield the same optimum. Their usual algorithms are reversed in option 3: 0/1 commonly uses dynamic programming, whereas fractional uses greedy selection. Option 1 is vague and does not identify the mathematical difference.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-15-shift-1-d.keyIdea: 0/1 decisions are indivisible; fractional decisions allow partial items.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Write the statement with quantifiers: ‘Some students like hockey’ means ∃x(Student(x) ∧ LikesHockey(x)). Negating an existential quantifier changes it to a universal quantifier and negates its predicate: ¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x). Therefore every student does not like hockey, expressed in the options as ‘Every student dislike hockey.’
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Option 1 still asserts that at least one such student exists, so it can be true at the same time as the original statement. Options 3 and 4 assert that all students like hockey, which is much stronger than the original statement rather than its negation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Negation rules: ¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x), and ¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The relation uses strict inequalities in both coordinates: (x,y) R (u,v) when x<u and y<v. It is irreflexive because x<x is never true. A partial order, under the standard definition, must be reflexive; an equivalence relation must also be reflexive (as well as symmetric and transitive). Hence this strict relation is neither a partial order nor an equivalence relation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 3 call it an order even though reflexivity fails. Option 4 fails because the relation is neither reflexive nor symmetric: if x<u, the reverse inequality u<x cannot hold simultaneously.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Before testing the harder properties of a relation, test reflexivity. A strict ‘less-than’ relation is irreflexive.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: There are eight people (four couples). The seven people other than the questioner give seven distinct feasible handshake counts, so their answers must be 0,1,2,3,4,5,6. The person reporting 6 shook everyone except their spouse, so that spouse must be the person reporting 0. Remove that couple. The same pairing argument then matches 5 with 1 and 4 with 2. The only unpaired answer is 3, and it must belong to the questioner’s spouse.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Counts 1, 2, and 4 are forced into complementary spouse-pairs (5,1) and (4,2). None can be the questioner’s spouse because their complementary partner is also among the seven respondents.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: In the couples-handshake puzzle with n couples, the distinct answers pair as 0 with 2n−2, 1 with 2n−3, and so on; the questioner’s spouse has n−1 handshakes.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Evaluate the four combinations of the two comparisons. The code returns false for (x>25,y>100), true for (x≤25,y≤100), false for (x>25,y≤100), and true for the remaining case (x≤25,y>100). Thus y does not affect the final result: both cases with x≤25 return true and both cases with x>25 return false. The entire conditional is therefore equivalent to returning x≤25.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Option 1 is the exact opposite of the required result. Options 3 and 4 depend only on y, but the original conditional gives different results for the same y value when x crosses 25.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: For a small conditional with Boolean inputs, make a truth table and look for a variable that becomes irrelevant.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: CPU registers are located inside the processor and are accessed directly by instructions. They have the smallest capacity but the lowest access latency in the usual memory hierarchy. Cache is also fast, but it sits below registers; main memory and secondary storage are progressively slower.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Cache memory is designed to reduce average memory-access time but is not faster than the processor’s registers. Main memory requires an external memory access, while secondary memory is slower still.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Typical speed order: registers → cache → main memory → secondary storage.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: With n address lines, the circuit can select 2^n distinct addresses. Each selected address stores or supplies m data bits because there are m data lines. Hence the organization is 2^n words × m bits, and the total capacity is 2^n·m bits. This is option 3 (whose exponent is faint in the OCR text).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Multiplying n by m treats address lines as though each line selected only one word. The address lines encode binary combinations, so the number of addressable words grows exponentially as 2^n, not linearly as n.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: n binary address lines select 2^n locations; m data lines determine the word width.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The minterms split into two groups of four. Minterms 0,2,8,10 all have B=0 and D=0 while A and C vary, giving B′D′. Minterms 5,7,13,15 all have B=1 and D=1 while A and C vary, giving BD. Combining the two implicants gives F=B′D′+BD.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The minterm membership does not depend on A or C, so expressions built from A,C cannot be correct. The AD expression also changes when A changes, whereas paired minterms show A is eliminated.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: In a K-map group, every variable that changes within the group disappears from the implicant.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: The unpipelined average instruction time is [0.40(4)+0.20(4)+0.40(5)]×10 ns = 4.4×10 ns = 44 ns. With pipelining, the clock period is the original 10 ns stage time plus 1 ns pipeline overhead, or 11 ns. In steady state the pipeline completes about one instruction per clock, so the speedup in instruction rate is 44/11=4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: The alternatives 2, 6, and 8 do not follow from the weighted CPI and the 11 ns pipeline clock. Ignoring the one-nanosecond overhead would give 4.4, which is also not any of those alternatives.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Pipeline throughput speedup = unpipelined average instruction time ÷ pipelined clock period, after the pipeline fills.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Machine language consists of binary-encoded instructions executed directly by a processor. It is hardware-dependent and offers almost no abstraction from the instruction-set architecture, so it is classified as a low-level language.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Assembly language is another low-level language but uses mnemonic symbols and requires an assembler, so it is not identical to machine language. A high-level language provides greater abstraction, and ‘translating language’ is not the relevant language category.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Machine code and assembly are low-level; machine code is the binary form the CPU executes directly.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: For the same bit width, an unsigned integer uses every bit for magnitude and therefore represents 2^n nonnegative values. A typical n-bit signed representation uses one bit’s worth of encoding to distinguish negative from nonnegative values, so its maximum positive magnitude is smaller. Thus the claim that the signed type has a greater range of values than the unsigned type is wrong.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Unsigned values are nonnegative, and signed values may be positive or negative. In an unsigned representation the most significant bit contributes to the numeric value rather than serving as a sign bit, so statements 1, 2, and 4 express the intended distinctions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: For n bits, unsigned commonly spans 0 to 2^n−1; two’s-complement signed spans −2^(n−1) to 2^(n−1)−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: A constructor defined outside its class uses the class scope-resolution qualifier: Date::Date(int dd) { ... }. The explicit specifier belongs on the constructor declaration inside the class; it is not repeated on the out-of-class definition. Therefore the form shown in option 1 is the correct definition syntax.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Option 2 incorrectly places explicit on the out-of-class definition. Options 3 and 4 are false because constructors may be declared in the class and defined outside it just like other member functions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: Declare `explicit Date(int)` in the class; define it later as `Date::Date(int) { ... }`.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.explanation: Refreshing a browser normally sends the same persistent session cookie back to the server, so the server can associate the new request with the existing session and continue it. The other events explicitly end or invalidate the client/server session association.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.whyOthersFail: Closing the browser can discard a nonpersistent session cookie. Logging out commonly invalidates the server-side session, and an inactivity timeout expires it. A refresh with a persistent cookie does none of those things.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2023-mar-11-shift-2-d.keyIdea: HTTP is stateless; a session continues across requests only while its identifier remains valid on both client and server.
