sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Space division does not require the same store-and-read time-slot operation. Virtual and packet switching are not the two circuit-switching implementation categories being contrasted in this question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Time switching rearranges time slots with memory; space switching connects physical paths through a matrix.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: In a link-state routing protocol, each router floods descriptions of its local links. After reliable flooding converges, all routers in the routing area have the same link-state database representing the topology. Each independently runs a shortest-path algorithm, commonly Dijkstra's algorithm, on that common database.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Distance-vector routers exchange distance estimates with neighbours and do not maintain a complete common topology database. 'Link vector' is not the standard method here. Dijkstra is the computation performed on the database, not the routing information-distribution method.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Link-state routing floods topology information so every router can compute paths from the same database.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: A 16-level phase-shift-keying signal carries 9600 bit/s. What is its modulation rate?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: An M-ary signal carries log2(M) bits per symbol. With M=16, each phase symbol represents log2(16)=4 bits. Symbol rate (baud)=bit rate/bits per symbol=9600/4=2400 symbols per second, so the modulation rate is 2400 baud.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 1200 baud would imply 8 bits per symbol (256 levels); 4800 would imply 2 bits per symbol (4 levels); 9600 would be binary signalling with 1 bit per symbol.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: For M-ary modulation, baud=bit rate/log2(M).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: Which Fast Ethernet physical-layer variant supports a station-to-hub/link distance of about 2000 metres?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: 100BASE-FX carries Fast Ethernet over optical fibre. In the commonly quoted full-duplex point-to-point configuration, multimode fibre supports a link of about 2 km. The copper twisted-pair variants are normally limited to roughly 100 m, so the 2000 m figure identifies 100BASE-FX.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 100BASE-TX and 100BASE-T4 are copper variants with much shorter segment limits. 100BASE-T1 is a later single-pair copper technology and is not the 2 km legacy Fast Ethernet option.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: The F in 100BASE-FX signals fibre, enabling much longer Fast Ethernet links than twisted-pair T variants.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Software engineering aims to deliver software that satisfies required quality and functionality while respecting project constraints. Among the options, 'software within budget in the given schedule' is the only answer that includes the product plus the two central management constraints of cost and time.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A program may be only one component and says nothing about quality or constraints. 'Software' alone omits delivery feasibility. 'Within budget' alone does not name the product and omits schedule.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Successful software engineering balances scope/quality, cost and schedule.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: The key process areas of CMM Level 4 are also satisfied by a process at which level?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: CMM maturity levels are cumulative. An organization assessed at Level 5 must institutionalize the lower-level practices and also add Level 5's optimizing practices. It therefore satisfies the quantitative process-management and software quality-management key process areas associated with Level 4. A Level 2 or Level 3 process has not yet demonstrated Level 4 control.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Levels 2 and 3 are below Level 4 and need not satisfy its quantitative KPAs, so A and B are false. Because those lower levels are not all correct, 'all of the above' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: CMM levels are staged and cumulative: a Level 5 process includes the capabilities required at Levels 2-4.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Validation checks whether the completed or specified system is the product stakeholders actually need: 'Are we building the right product?' It compares behaviour with user needs, intended use and acceptance criteria.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 'Are we building the product right?' is verification—checking conformance to specifications and correct construction. Field verification is not the definition, so C and D are also incorrect.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Verification checks conformance; validation checks fitness for user need.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A statistically controlled process varies only within established control limits because special causes have been removed or understood. Its future performance is therefore stable enough to predict within a statistical range. Predictability is what makes quantitative process management useful.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Maintainability is a product/process-change quality, measurability is a prerequisite for statistical analysis, and verifiability concerns checking results. None is the direct consequence expressed by statistical control.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Statistical control means stable variation, which makes process outcomes predictable within limits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Function-oriented design decomposes a system into modules that perform coherent functions. Good modules have high cohesion—their elements work toward one focused purpose—and low coupling—modules depend on each other through small, clear interfaces. This localizes change and simplifies testing and reuse.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Low cohesion mixes unrelated responsibilities. High coupling spreads the effect of changes across modules. Any option containing either undesirable property fails the design goal.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Good modular design maximizes cohesion within modules and minimizes coupling between them.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: Which software-size metric does not depend on the programming language used?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Function-oriented size measures count externally visible inputs, outputs, inquiries, logical files and interfaces, then adjust by prescribed weights. They describe delivered functionality before a language is chosen. The same requirements can therefore be estimated across different implementation languages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Lines of code vary greatly by language expressiveness and style. Token counts depend directly on a language's lexical and syntactic forms. Since A and C are language-dependent, 'all of the above' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Function points measure user-visible functionality; LOC and token counts measure language-specific implementation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: If an internal B+ tree node has p child pointers, it has p-1 search keys. Each pointer uses 4 bytes and each name key uses 8 bytes, so node size is 4p+8(p-1)=12p-8. It must fit in 512 bytes: 12p-8<=512, hence 12p<=520 and p<=43.33. The largest integer degree is therefore 43.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 16 and 42 fit but waste usable fan-out and produce a taller index than necessary. Degree 44 needs 4(44)+8(43)=520 bytes and does not fit in a 512-byte block.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: For p pointers and p-1 keys, solve p(pointer size)+(p-1)(key size)<=block size.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Inorder traversal visits left subtree, node, then right subtree. In a binary search tree every left key is smaller and every right key is larger according to the duplicate convention. Recursive inorder traversal therefore emits keys in nondecreasing sorted order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: An arbitrary binary tree has no ordering invariant. A heap guarantees only parent-child priority, not a sorted inorder sequence. Therefore 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: BST ordering plus left-root-right traversal yields sorted keys.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Mobile IP first performs agent discovery: a mobile node learns whether it is on its home or a foreign network and discovers mobility agents through advertisements/solicitations. It then registers its care-of address with its home agent so packets can be tunnelled to its current location. These are the two basic control functions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Route discovery belongs to routing protocols, not the named Mobile IP pair. 'IP binding' describes an association but is not paired as the standard basic function here. Hence 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Mobile IP operation begins with agent discovery and care-of-address registration.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: Pre-emptive scheduling is the strategy of temporarily suspending a running process:
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A pre-emptive scheduler can take the CPU from a running process before that process voluntarily blocks or completes its allowed execution interval, for example when a higher-priority process becomes ready. The saved context lets the suspended process resume later. Option A best expresses this early forced suspension.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Helping a starving process can be one policy reason but is not the definition. A process that requests I/O blocks voluntarily. Collision avoidance is unrelated to CPU scheduling.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Pre-emption is involuntary CPU revocation while a process is still runnable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Increasing the round-robin quantum reduces context switches and makes the policy approach FCFS, but average turnaround time is not monotonic. A larger quantum may let a long process delay several short ones, increasing their completion times; in another workload it may reduce repeated waiting and context-switch effects. As the quantum crosses different CPU-burst lengths, the completion order can change discontinuously. Thus the average varies irregularly with the workload and chosen quantum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: There is no universal always-increasing or always-decreasing rule, and the value is not constant. Those claims can be disproved by choosing different burst-time sets.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: RR turnaround depends on burst lengths and completion order; changing quantum can help or hurt non-monotonically.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Mutual exclusion means at least one resource is non-shareable: only one process may use an instance at a time. Another process requesting that occupied resource must wait. This is the Coffman condition described by the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Hold and wait concerns retaining resources while requesting more. No pre-emption means resources cannot be forcibly taken. Circular wait describes a cycle of processes waiting on one another.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Non-shareable allocation is the mutual-exclusion condition for deadlock.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A small loop repeatedly executes the same instructions and often revisits the same data, giving strong temporal and spatial locality; once the working set enters cache, most accesses hit. Large sequential code offers little temporal reuse but generates a stream of consecutive addresses. Interleaved memory maps consecutive words to different banks, allowing overlapped or parallel accesses and higher bandwidth. Therefore (i) and (iii) are true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Interleaving gives little special benefit to a tiny repeatedly reused loop compared with a cache. A large one-pass sequential stream can exceed cache capacity and has limited reuse, so caching is not the best of the two models for statement (iv).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Cache exploits locality and reuse; bank interleaving accelerates sustained sequential access.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: For page trace 4,3,2,1,4,3,5,4,3,2,1,5 and four frames, how many FIFO page faults occur?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Fill the four frames with 4,3,2,1: four faults. The next references 4 and 3 are hits. Reference 5 faults and evicts the oldest page 4. Then 4, 3, 2, 1 and 5 each fault in turn, evicting 3, 2, 1, 5 and 4 respectively. Total faults=4+1+5=10.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Counts 8 or 9 miss one or more cascading FIFO evictions after page 5 enters. Twelve incorrectly treats both hits at positions 5 and 6 as faults.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: FIFO evicts by arrival order, not recent use; maintain a queue of loaded pages while tracing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.additionalInformation: The source says 'percentage', but the options are counts. Ten faults out of twelve references would be about 83.33%.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A packet checksum groups the data bits into fixed-width words and treats each word as an integer. For the Internet checksum, these are 16-bit integers added with one's-complement arithmetic; the complemented sum is transmitted. The receiver repeats the operation to detect corruption.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Characters and real numbers are not the general checksum interpretation. Although the words consist of bits, the algorithm does not simply add individual bit values; it adds multi-bit integer words with carry handling.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Checksums add fixed-width integer words; CRCs instead treat bits as coefficients of a polynomial.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: If a signed integer occupies two bytes, what is its maximum value?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Two bytes contain 16 bits. In the usual two's-complement signed representation, one bit position determines the negative range, leaving the maximum positive bit pattern 0 followed by fifteen 1s. Its value is 2^15-1=32767.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 2^16-1=65535 is the maximum unsigned 16-bit value. 2^16 is outside the 16-bit unsigned range. 2^15=32768 is one greater than the maximum signed positive value.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: An n-bit two's-complement integer ranges from -2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1)-1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Emitter-Coupled Logic keeps its bipolar transistors out of saturation and steers current between branches. Avoiding saturation storage delay gives ECL very small propagation delay, making it the traditional logic family best known for high-speed operation, though at high power consumption and with smaller noise margins.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: TTL is fast but generally slower than ECL. Traditional MOS is slower, while CMOS is valued especially for low static power and density; modern CMOS can be extremely fast, but the classic logic-family comparison asked here selects ECL.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Classic ECL gains speed by using nonsaturating current-steering transistor operation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A software interrupt is generated by executing a specific instruction such as INT, trap or a system-call instruction. It deliberately transfers control to an interrupt/trap handler using the processor's interrupt mechanism. Therefore an interrupt initiated by an instruction is classified as software.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: External/hardware interrupts originate from devices, timers or external pins. 'Internal' is often used for exceptions caused by execution conditions, such as divide-by-zero, but the explicit interrupt-instruction case is conventionally called a software interrupt.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Hardware interrupts arrive from outside the instruction stream; software interrupts are explicitly invoked by instructions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The %c conversion tells printf to interpret the integer argument as an unsigned-character code and output the corresponding character. In ASCII, decimal 100 is the lowercase letter d, so the statement prints d rather than the digits '100'.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Printing 100 numerically would require %d. The integer argument is valid for %c after the normal variadic promotion, so the result is not garbage or 'none'.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: printf format controls interpretation: %d prints an integer's digits; %c prints the character with that code.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Classic Bluetooth divides communication into time slots and alternates transmission direction between the master/central and slave/peripheral on the same frequency-hopping channel. Using different time slots for the two directions is time-division duplex (TDD). Frequency hopping changes channel over time but does not make the link frequency-division duplex.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: TDM describes sharing capacity among streams but not specifically the two directions. FDM and FDD use simultaneous distinct frequency bands, unlike Bluetooth's alternating slot directions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Bluetooth combines frequency hopping with time-slotted time-division duplex.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: I is true: a decider halts on all inputs, so swapping its accept and reject outcomes decides the complement. II is true: two recognizers can be dovetailed in parallel and accept when either accepts, recognizing the union. III is false: recursively enumerable languages are not generally closed under complement; if both a language and its complement are RE, the language is recursive.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A omits the valid union closure. C accepts the false complement closure and omits II. D also accepts III while rejecting the true recursive-complement statement I.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Recursive languages are complement-closed; RE languages are union-closed but not complement-closed.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: Which routing algorithm is used by RIP and IGRP?
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: RIP and Cisco's historical IGRP are distance-vector routing protocols. Routers periodically advertise a vector of destination metrics to neighbours and update routes using Bellman-Ford-style reasoning. RIP uses hop count; IGRP used a composite metric. The source's 'Dijkstra vector' is a printing error for the intended distance-vector choice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: OSPF is a specific link-state protocol. Link-state routing floods topology and runs a shortest-path computation rather than exchanging only neighbour distance vectors. 'Dynamic' is true as a broad classification but does not name the algorithm family asked for.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: RIP and IGRP are distance-vector; OSPF and IS-IS are link-state.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: E-commerce produces clickstream events, rapidly changing products, customer interactions, external campaign data and often near-real-time analytical needs. A warehouse strategy must adapt its sources, refresh, scalability, identity resolution and security to this environment. Therefore saying it should be done in a purely classical manner is the incorrect statement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Overall business strategy should drive warehouse strategy. E-commerce creates new web-server and interaction data to analyse. Its security threats can reasonably be grouped into broad categories such as client, communication-channel and server threats. Those statements are not the incorrect one sought.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: An e-commerce warehouse extends classical warehousing for web-scale, behavioural and faster-changing data.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Software reliability is the probability of failure-free operation for a specified time and environment. Failures arise when residual faults/errors are activated by inputs and operational conditions. Consequently reliability is most directly related to how many defects remain and how likely the operational profile is to encounter them; more residual errors generally mean lower reliability.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Design quality and engineer experience influence how many faults are introduced or removed, but they are indirect factors. User requirements define expected behaviour and acceptance but do not themselves determine the realized failure rate.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Reliability falls as residual fault content and fault exposure rise; design and experience affect reliability through that fault content.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Home banking, online stock trading and online auctions all conduct commercial or financial transactions over electronic networks, so they are standard e-commerce applications. Evaluating an employee is an internal human-resource management activity and is not inherently an e-commerce transaction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options 1-3 exchange money, financial instruments or goods/services electronically. They fit the scope of e-commerce, unlike the HR appraisal task.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: E-commerce concerns electronically mediated market or financial transactions, not every activity performed with software.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The Global Positioning System uses signals from a constellation of satellites. A receiver measures signal travel-time differences from multiple satellites and solves for its three-dimensional position and clock offset. It is therefore the satellite-based positioning/tracking system described.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Bluetooth is a short-range local wireless technology. WAP is an application protocol framework for mobile web access. SMS carries short text messages over cellular networks. None independently performs satellite positioning.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: GPS determines location by ranging to multiple navigation satellites.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A usable microcomputer system needs a microprocessor to execute instructions, memory to hold programs and data, and peripheral/I/O equipment so it can communicate with users, storage and external devices. The processor alone is only the CPU component, not the complete system.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Each of A-C names one necessary component but omits the other two. Therefore only 'all of the above' describes the complete system.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Computer system=processor+memory+input/output/peripheral subsystem.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Arithmetic addition and data comparison are carried out by the arithmetic and logic unit inside the CPU chip. The control unit selects the operation and registers supply operands; the ALU produces the arithmetic result or condition flags used by comparisons.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Hard and floppy disks store data persistently. Memory chips store instructions/data and return them to the CPU; ordinary memory does not perform the requested addition and comparison.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: The CPU's ALU performs arithmetic and logical comparisons.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: The pipelining strategy is used to implement:
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Instruction prefetch overlaps fetching later instructions with decoding or executing earlier ones, forming the first/simple stages of an instruction pipeline. While one instruction proceeds downstream, the fetch unit keeps the pipeline supplied. This overlap increases throughput, so 'instruction prefetch' is the intended named strategy among the choices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Execution, decoding and manipulation are individual activities, not the specific overlapping fetch strategy named by the item. A full instruction pipeline includes them, but the option that captures the cited strategy is prefetch.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Pipelining overlaps stages of successive instructions; prefetch keeps later instructions ready before current execution finishes.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A linear data structure arranges elements in a logical sequence so, except at ends, each element has a predecessor and successor. Strings are sequences of characters, lists are sequences of elements, and queues are ordered sequences with insertion/removal rules. All three are linear structures.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A, B and C are each correct examples, but selecting only one omits the others. Trees and graphs would be standard nonlinear contrasts.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Strings, lists and queues all organize data sequentially.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A tree represents hierarchy directly: one root has children, those children have descendants, and every non-root node has a parent in the basic tree model. File directories, organization charts and syntax structures are common examples.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A deque is a linear sequence allowing insertion/removal at both ends. A priority queue orders access by priority but does not represent parent-child hierarchy. Therefore 'all' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Use a tree when relationships are naturally parent-child and multi-level.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Options A and D both satisfy the stated steady-state condition
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Let x be the output of the first NAND gate and z the output of the second NAND gate. From the circuit, x = NOT(A AND f), z = NOT(x AND B), and f = z AND C. The question fixes f=1, so C must be 1 and z must be 1. With f=1, x=NOT A; therefore z=1 exactly when (NOT A) AND B is 0, or equivalently when A=1 or B=0. Option A has B=0 and C=1, so it works. Option D has A=1 and C=1, so it also works. Direct substitution confirms that both reproduce f=1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: B gives A=0 and B=1, hence x=1 and z=0, making f=0. C has C=0, so the final AND gate forces f=0. The printed item is defective because it offers both A and D as separate choices even though both are steady-state solutions. If an unstated initial condition f=0 is assumed, A reaches 1 whereas D can remain at 0; that extra assumption may explain keys that select only A, but it is not in the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For feedback logic, write one Boolean equation per gate and require the assumed output to reproduce itself. Here f=1 implies C=1 and (A=1 or B=0).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An AVL tree maintains a balance factor of -1, 0 or +1 at every node, so its height is O(log n). Search follows a root-to-leaf path, while insertion and deletion first follow such a path and then perform only a bounded amount of rebalancing work per visited level. Their worst-case times are therefore O(log n). An ordinary binary search tree can become a chain of height n, making all three operations O(n) in the worst case.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2 and 3 each omit one operation that also receives the same worst-case improvement. AVL balancing benefits search, insertion and deletion, so the complete choice is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: AVL height is O(log n); an unbalanced BST can have height O(n). Since the three basic operations depend on tree height, AVL improves all three worst-case bounds.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The standard high-level GSM architecture has three major subsystems: the Switching System (SS), which performs switching and subscriber/mobility functions; the Base Station System (BSS), which provides the radio access path; and the Operation and Support System (OSS), which supervises and maintains the network. Individual elements such as the MSC and BSC live inside these larger systems.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: B mixes the BSS with two components, BSC and MSC, instead of naming the three major systems. C uses a cell and a BSC, which are radio concepts/components rather than all three subsystems. D likewise mixes systems and a cell while omitting BSS and OSS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Remember GSM at the top level as SS + BSS + OSS: switching, radio access, and operations/support.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The set {empty set} is not empty: it contains exactly one element, namely the empty set. A one-element set has 2^1=2 subsets. They are the empty subset and the whole set {empty set}. Hence its power set is {empty set, {empty set}}.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 1 lists only the empty subset and misses the whole set. Option 3 replaces the empty set with the number 0; these are different objects. Option 4 wrongly introduces 0 and consequently has the wrong members and cardinality.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Distinguish the empty set from a set containing it: |empty set|=0, but |{empty set}|=1, so the latter has two subsets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: FCFS services requests in their arrival order. Starting at 32, the head movements are |98-32|=66, |37-98|=61, |14-37|=23, |124-14|=110, |65-124|=59, and |67-65|=2. Adding them gives 66+61+23+110+59+2=321 cylinder moves.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other totals do not equal the sum of all six consecutive movements. A common error is to measure every request from the initial cylinder or to omit one transition; FCFS requires measuring from the head's new position after each service.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For FCFS disk scheduling, preserve queue order and sum the absolute difference between every pair of consecutive head positions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Component-level design translates architectural components into implementable internal designs. In a conventional or structured system it can refine flow-oriented analysis models into procedural components. In an object-oriented system it refines class-based analysis and design classes into methods, interfaces and collaborations. Thus component-level design applies to both perspectives.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 1 is incomplete because component design is not limited to conventional flow-oriented models. Option 2 is incomplete because it is not limited to object-oriented classes. Since both are valid contexts, 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Component-level design is the detailed design of implementable units and can be derived from either flow-oriented or class-based models.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In compiler theory, the lexical tokens of C are recognized by regular techniques, while the grammatical structure of declarations, expressions, statements and functions is described with a context-free grammar and parsed with context-free parsing methods. Under the standard exam convention, this makes C a context-free language at the syntax level.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Regular languages are not expressive enough for arbitrary recursive nesting such as balanced parentheses and blocks. 'Context-sensitive' is not the intended syntactic classification, although a complete C implementation must enforce context-dependent semantic constraints such as declarations and types. Therefore 'none' is unnecessary.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Separate syntax from semantics: C syntax is modeled by a context-free grammar; name, type and declaration checks are handled during semantic analysis.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Mobile Application Part (MAP) is a telecommunications application-layer protocol used for GSM/UMTS mobility, authentication, roaming and short-message-related signaling between network databases and switches. It belongs to the Signalling System No. 7 protocol family and is carried through the SS7 transaction/signaling stack, traditionally over TCAP.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: SNMP manages IP-network devices, SMTP transfers electronic mail, and HTTP transfers web resources. None provides the telecom signaling services on which GSM MAP operates.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: GSM MAP is part of the SS7 signaling environment; associate it with mobility and subscriber-database transactions, not Internet application protocols.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In IPv4 fragmentation, a fragmentation offset of 0 means the fragment begins with the first byte of the original datagram's payload. An M/MF bit of 1 means more fragments follow. Together, offset=0 and MF=1 identify the first fragment of a fragmented datagram.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A middle fragment has a nonzero offset and MF=1. The last fragment has MF=0 and normally a nonzero offset. The same header combination therefore cannot describe all fragment positions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: IPv4 fragment test: offset 0 means first position; MF 1 means not last. Both together mean first fragment with more to come.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Let A be the length-8 strings that start with 1 and B those that end with 00. For A, the remaining seven bits are free, so |A|=2^7=128. For B, the first six bits are free, so |B|=2^6=64. Strings in both sets start with 1 and end with 00; five middle bits remain free, giving |A intersection B|=2^5=32. Inclusion-exclusion gives 128+64-32=160.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 32 counts only the overlap, 64 counts only strings ending in 00, and 128 counts only strings starting in 1. Simply adding 128 and 64 would also be wrong because it double-counts the 32 strings satisfying both conditions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For an 'either ... or ...' count, use |A union B|=|A|+|B|-|A intersection B|.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Strength reduction is a code-optimization transformation that replaces an expensive operation with an equivalent cheaper one. Examples include replacing multiplication by a power of two with a shift, or replacing repeated index multiplication inside a loop with incremental addition. The program's meaning is preserved while execution cost is reduced.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: It does not restrict the permitted values of input variables and certainly does not aim to reduce program efficiency. Since option 2 states the standard definition, 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Strength reduction changes operation cost, not program meaning: expensive arithmetic is replaced by cheaper equivalent instructions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The described calculation is EA = contents of register + constant displacement. This is indexed addressing (also called displacement or base-plus-offset addressing in many architectures): the instruction supplies the constant and a register supplies the variable/base part of the address.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Absolute addressing uses an address stated directly in the instruction. Indirect addressing obtains the effective address through another memory/register reference rather than the stated register-plus-constant calculation. Immediate addressing places the operand value itself in the instruction, so no effective memory address is formed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Recognize addressing modes from the EA formula: register + constant offset indicates indexed/displacement addressing.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: BCNF requires every nontrivial functional dependency X -> Y to have X as a superkey. Third normal form permits one additional case: when X is not a superkey, the dependency may still be accepted if each attribute of Y is prime. BCNF therefore imposes at least all the restrictions of 3NF, so every BCNF relation is in 3NF.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A 3NF relation need not be in BCNF because 3NF's prime-attribute exception can allow a determinant that is not a superkey. Consequently the two forms are not identical, and a BCNF relation cannot fail 3NF.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: BCNF is stricter than 3NF: BCNF implies 3NF, but 3NF does not always imply BCNF.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: The 426 K process cannot be allocated; none of the listed partitions is correct
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Apply first fit from the beginning for every process. The 212 K process goes into the 500 K hole, leaving 288 K. The 417 K process goes into the 600 K hole, leaving 183 K. The 112 K process then goes into the first sufficient remaining hole, the 288 K remainder, leaving 176 K. The free holes are now 100 K, 176 K, 200 K, 300 K and 183 K. None is large enough for 426 K, so that process must wait or allocation fails.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The original 500 K and 600 K holes have already been used and their remainders are too small. The 200 K and 300 K holes were never large enough. Thus A-D are all false. This printed multiple-choice item is defective unless some unstated deallocation or different allocation rule is assumed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: First fit rescans holes in address order for each process and splits the selected hole. Track the remainder after every allocation; total free memory is irrelevant when no single hole is large enough.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Windows historically defines its wide-character APIs and wchar_t representation around 16-bit UTF-16 code units. Therefore the intended answer to this exam-era question is 16 bits per Windows Unicode character/code unit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 8 bits is the width of a byte and is insufficient for a UTF-16 code unit. 32 bits corresponds to UTF-32 or to a full supplementary Unicode code point represented as two UTF-16 code units, not one Windows wide code unit. 64 bits is not a standard Unicode encoding unit. Strictly, a supplementary character uses a surrogate pair totaling 32 bits, so 'character' here should be read as code unit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For Windows API questions, remember UTF-16: one wchar_t/code unit is 16 bits, while some Unicode code points require two such units.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An AVL tree is a self-balancing binary search tree. For every node it maintains |height(left)-height(right)| <= 1. After insertion or deletion, rotations restore this condition, keeping the overall height logarithmic.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A general binary search tree has no height-balance guarantee. Threading replaces null links with traversal links and does not impose this height condition. A complete binary tree has a level-filling shape property, which is different from the local AVL balance-factor rule.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: AVL means a binary search tree with balance factor -1, 0 or +1 at every node.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The data-link layer groups the physical layer's raw bit stream into identifiable frames. It also commonly handles link-level addressing, error control, flow control and medium access. Therefore framing is a central data-link design issue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Representation and synchronization of individual bits are physical-layer concerns: they deal with signals, encoding and clocking. Connection control is normally associated with establishing and managing end-to-end transport connections in this layer-oriented comparison, not with the defining data-link task asked here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Layer cue: physical layer handles bits; data-link layer packages those bits into frames.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The nonterminal hierarchy determines precedence. Subtraction is generated inside F by F -> F-F, while multiplication and addition are generated at the E level. Whenever an E-level operator needs an F operand, a subtraction expression can already be formed as that F. Thus '-' binds more tightly than '*', making statement 2 true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The grammar does not support the usual arithmetic assumption that '*' must outrank '+': precedence must be read from these productions, not imported from convention. '+' and '-' are on different nonterminal levels, so they do not have equal precedence. The productions likewise do not establish '+' as having higher precedence than '*'.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: In an expression grammar, operators introduced in a lower/deeper nonterminal generally have higher precedence. Here '-' is inside F, below the E-level operators.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The original Software Capability Maturity Model describes five ordered process-maturity levels: 1 Initial, 2 Repeatable, 3 Defined, 4 Managed, and 5 Optimizing (often written 'Optimized' in exam options). They progress from ad hoc work to disciplined, measured and continuously improving processes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 'Primary' and 'Secondary' are not CMM maturity levels, and neither is 'Stating'. Option 1 is the only list that preserves the recognized five-level sequence, so 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Memorize the classic CMM sequence as I-R-D-M-O: Initial, Repeatable, Defined, Managed, Optimizing.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Indefinite blockage in priority scheduling is starvation: a low-priority job may wait forever while higher-priority jobs keep arriving. Aging prevents this by gradually increasing the priority of a process the longer it waits. Eventually its priority becomes high enough for the scheduler to run it.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A parity bit detects certain transmission errors. Compaction combines scattered free memory to reduce external fragmentation. A timer supports preemption and time accounting but, by itself, does not guarantee that a low-priority job's priority will ever overtake newly arriving jobs.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Starvation is cured by aging: waiting time is converted into progressively higher scheduling priority.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the Windows Graphics Device Interface (GDI), Ellipse() draws an ellipse inside a supplied bounding rectangle. If that bounding rectangle has equal width and height, the ellipse has equal radii and is therefore a circle. There is no standard GDI Circle() function in this API set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Circle() is the tempting name but is not the relevant Windows GDI function. RoundRect() draws a rectangle with rounded corners. Pie() draws a pie-shaped wedge bounded by an ellipse and radial lines, not a complete circle by itself.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A circle is a special ellipse. In Windows GDI, call Ellipse() with a square bounding box.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the CODASYL network model, a FIXED retention constraint permanently associates a member record with the set occurrence into which it is inserted. Because the membership cannot be changed to another owner, the DML RECONNECT operation is not permitted for a FIXED set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: An OPTIONAL member may exist without set membership and can be connected or disconnected as required. A MANDATORY member must remain in some set occurrence, so RECONNECT is precisely the one-step operation used to move it from one occurrence to another without leaving it unattached. Therefore the restriction is not common to all three.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: CODASYL retention: OPTIONAL can detach, MANDATORY can reconnect, FIXED cannot change set occurrence.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: RG-59 is a 75-ohm coaxial cable traditionally used for baseband video and cable-television distribution. Its characteristic impedance matches common video/RF television systems.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Classic thick Ethernet used 50-ohm RG-8-type coax, and thin Ethernet used 50-ohm RG-58. 'Ethernet' alone does not identify the 75-ohm RG-59 application asked here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Cable association: RG-59 = 75 ohms = video/cable TV; legacy coaxial Ethernet uses 50-ohm cable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: RAD stands for Rapid Application Development. It is an iterative, time-boxed software-development approach that emphasizes quick prototyping, strong user feedback, reusable components and frequent delivery instead of a long single-pass lifecycle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 'Rapid and Design', 'Rapid Aided Development' and 'Rapid Application Design' are not the expansion of the established software-engineering acronym.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: RAD = Rapid Application Development; connect it with prototypes, user involvement and short development cycles.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Model the chain as a full rooted 4-ary tree. A person who sends the letter is an internal vertex with exactly four children; a person who does not send it is a leaf. If I people send and L=100 do not, the number of edges is both 4I and (I+L)-1. Thus 4I=I+100-1, so 3I=99 and I=33. The total number who saw the letter is I+L=33+100=133.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other pairs do not satisfy the full m-ary tree relation L=(m-1)I+1. For m=4 and L=100, the only possible sender count is 33 and the first person is already included as the root.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For a full m-ary tree, L=(m-1)I+1 and total vertices are I+L. Here 100 leaves imply 33 internal senders and 133 readers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Location 5; none of the listed options is correct
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: With table locations 0 through 12, insert in order using linear probing: 55 hashes to 3; 58 to 6; 68 hashes to occupied 3 and goes to 4; 91 goes to 0; 27 goes to 1; and 145 goes to 2. Now 79 mod 13=1. Locations 1, 2, 3 and 4 are occupied, so probing stops at the first free location, 5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 through 4 are exactly the occupied probe sequence, not the first available location. Starting table numbering at 1 instead of 0 does not repair the item: the same consecutive collision chain still leads to location 5. The printed option set is therefore defective.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Linear probing checks h(k), h(k)+1, h(k)+2, ... modulo the table size until the first empty slot. Never stop at an occupied option just because it is the original hash value.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A predictive LL(1) parser must choose a production using one lookahead symbol. Immediate or indirect left recursion prevents such top-down parsing and must be eliminated. Common left prefixes also delay the choice between alternatives, so the grammar is left-factored to expose the decision point. Hence both transformations are used when preparing a suitable grammar.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Removing only left recursion can leave alternatives with indistinguishable prefixes. Left factoring alone can leave recursive nonterminals that make a top-down parser loop. Also, these transformations are necessary preparation but do not guarantee that every context-free grammar has an equivalent LL(1) grammar.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: LL(1) preparation normally requires both elimination of left recursion and left factoring, followed by FIRST/FOLLOW conflict checks.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: All three activities create exposure in an electronic-payment system. Stolen or fabricated card credentials can be used fraudulently; card data can be intercepted or redirected while transmitted; and remotely stored credentials can be breached later. Secure systems reduce these risks with authenticated payment flows, encrypted transport, tokenization, restricted storage and monitoring.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2 and 3 each identify a real risk but each alone is incomplete. The question asks for the risk factors collectively, so 'all of the above' is the complete choice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Payment-card risk exists across the credential lifecycle: use, transmission and storage all need protection.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the C++ exception model used when this question was written, terminate() is invoked when exception handling cannot continue, while unexpected() handles violation of an old dynamic exception specification. Both functions have void return type, so the intended declarations are void terminate() and void unexpected().
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 2, 3 and 4 assign a non-void return type to at least one function, which contradicts the historical library interface. Modern C++ has removed unexpected() and dynamic exception specifications, so this is an exam-era language-history question rather than current coding advice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Historical C++ exception hooks terminate() and unexpected() both return void; in modern C++, focus on std::terminate and noexcept.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Segmentation allocates variable-sized contiguous regions for logical segments such as code, stack and data. As segments are created and removed, free memory becomes separated into holes. The total free space may be sufficient while no single contiguous hole is large enough, which is external fragmentation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Paging and pure demand paging use fixed-size pages and frames, so any free frame can hold any page and external fragmentation is avoided, though internal fragmentation can occur. Swapping is a movement policy rather than the listed variable-sized allocation scheme whose defining problem is being tested; its fragmentation behavior depends on the underlying allocator.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Variable-size contiguous allocation, including segmentation, causes external fragmentation; fixed-size paging avoids it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Basis-path testing examines a program's control-flow graph. Cyclomatic complexity gives the number of linearly independent paths, and test cases are designed to execute each independent basis path. Because the technique uses internal control structure and code paths, it is a white-box testing technique.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Black-box testing derives cases from externally visible requirements without inspecting code. Unit and system testing describe testing levels, not structural-versus-functional technique classes; basis-path testing is often applied to units, but its defining category is white-box.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Basis-path testing = control-flow graph + cyclomatic complexity + independent paths, so it is white-box testing.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In a network DBMS, the User Work Area is a collection of host-program variables whose record layouts correspond to database record types. Retrieved record contents are delivered by the DBMS into this area, and updates prepared by the program are passed back through it. It is therefore the communication area between the host program and the DBMS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A 'host record' is not the second communicating software system, so options 1 and 2 misstate the interface. The host language provides the environment in which the variables are declared, but the data transfer is between the executing host program and the DBMS, not between a program and its language.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Treat the UWA as the record exchange buffer at the host program-DBMS boundary.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The center of a tree consists of the vertex or adjacent pair with minimum eccentricity, equivalently the middle of every diameter. Any path from a left leaf such as a to a right leaf such as j has 5 edges: a-b-c-h-i-j. Its two middle vertices are c and h. Each has maximum distance 3 to a leaf, while moving one step away raises the maximum distance to at least 4. Thus both c and h, and only those two, are centers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: d is farther than c from all right-side leaves, and k is farther than h from all left-side leaves. The six intermediate branch vertices do not share minimum eccentricity. The tree has an odd-diameter length in edges, so its center is an adjacent pair rather than a single vertex.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Find a longest leaf-to-leaf path; the middle vertex or middle adjacent pair is the tree center. A 5-edge diameter has two centers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: For a B-tree of order m, each node has at most m children and therefore at most m-1 keys. If the root is at depth 0 and leaves are at depth d, the maximum number of nodes is 1+m+m^2+...+m^d=(m^(d+1)-1)/(m-1). Multiplying by m-1 keys per node gives m^(d+1)-1 maximum keys.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 2 counts the maximum number of nodes, not keys. Option 3 multiplies by m-1 without first dividing the geometric-series sum and is too large. Option 4 is a truncated geometric expression and does not count all levels through depth d.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Maximum B-tree keys equal the maximum node count times (m-1): ((m^(d+1)-1)/(m-1)) times (m-1) = m^(d+1)-1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Canonical LR(1) constructs items with individual lookahead symbols and recognizes the largest grammar class among the listed deterministic parsing methods. Its detailed states avoid reductions that would conflict in coarser LR tables, so it is more powerful than SLR and LALR and far more powerful than LL(1).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: LL(1) is a restricted top-down method. SLR uses FOLLOW sets for reductions and can introduce conflicts. LALR merges LR(1) states with identical LR(0) cores, producing smaller tables but sometimes creating conflicts that canonical LR(1) avoids.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Power order for these choices: LL(1) is weakest; SLR and LALR cover more grammars; canonical LR(1) is the most powerful.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The UNIX nice command starts a process with an adjusted niceness value, which influences its scheduling priority. A larger nice value generally means the process is more willing to yield CPU time and therefore receives lower scheduling priority. Existing processes may be adjusted with renice on systems that provide it.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: init is the traditional system-initialization process, kill sends signals to processes, and ps displays process information. None of those commands is specifically used to set the task's niceness/priority value.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: UNIX scheduling cue: nice starts a command with adjusted niceness; higher niceness usually means lower CPU priority.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: AES always uses a 128-bit data block, but the number of rounds depends on key length. AES-128 uses 10 rounds, AES-192 uses 12 rounds, and AES-256 uses 14 rounds. The initial AddRoundKey step is separate from this standard round count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other triples do not match the AES specification and do not follow its two-round increase as key size rises from 128 to 192 to 256 bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Memorize AES key/round pairs: 128/10, 192/12, 256/14; block size remains 128 bits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The representative basic gate for TTL is the NAND gate, whose multi-emitter input structure naturally supports TTL logic. ECL is commonly built around the NOR/OR function using differential transistor stages. The fundamental CMOS building block is the inverter, made from complementary pMOS and nMOS transistors. Thus a-1, b-2, c-3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Each other code assigns at least one family to the wrong characteristic circuit. In particular, the CMOS inverter is the clearest anchor; once c-3 is fixed, only option 1 also gives the standard TTL-NAND and ECL-NOR associations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Logic-family anchors: TTL-NAND, ECL-NOR, CMOS-inverter.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A union is a user-defined type, so a-2. Void belongs to the built-in fundamental types, so b-3. A pointer is constructed from another type and is therefore derived, so c-4. In the terminology used by the question, long qualifies/modifies double, so d-1. The complete match is a-2, b-3, c-4, d-1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes classify void as user-defined or derived, treat a pointer as built-in/user-defined, or fail to associate union with user-defined type. Those assignments conflict with the standard introductory C++ type hierarchy.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: C++ type examples: union is user-defined, void is built-in, pointer is derived, and long modifies double in long double.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Track the stack from bottom to top. push(1), push(2) gives [1,2]; Pop leaves [1]. push(3), push(4) gives [1,3,4]; two Pops leave [1]. push(5) gives [1,5], and the final Pop again leaves [1]. Therefore the top element is 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 4 and 3 are removed by the two consecutive Pop operations, and 2 is removed by the first Pop. A stack is LIFO, so each Pop removes the most recently pushed element still present; no Pop removes the bottom 1 in this sequence.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For stack traces, write the stack after every operation and remember LIFO: push adds at the top, Pop removes only the current top.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An enumeration defines a finite set of named legal values for a variable. For example, an enum Day might permit only MON, TUE, WED and the other named days. The names improve readability while the language assigns or represents them with underlying integral values.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: An enumeration is not merely a declaration of arbitrary numbers, does not sort strings, and does not specify an execution sequence for operators. Its defining purpose is to restrict a value to one member of a declared named set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: An enumerated type lists the complete set of named values that variables of that type may take.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The C++ stream open mode ios::in requests input access, meaning the file is opened for reading. It can be supplied to a stream constructor or open() call, and ifstream uses input mode by default.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: ios::app opens for append-style output, with writes placed at the end. in::ios reverses the scope qualification and is not a valid mode name. ios::file is not a standard C++ open mode.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: C++ file modes: ios::in for reading, ios::out for writing, and ios::app for appending.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Encryption transforms readable plaintext into ciphertext using a key so that an unauthorized party intercepting communication cannot understand the data. Its primary security service is confidentiality, which is why encryption is especially important in data-communication systems.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Compression, not encryption, is intended to reduce storage space. Encryption alone does not guarantee integrity because an attacker may alter ciphertext; integrity normally requires a MAC, digital signature or authenticated-encryption mode. It is certainly not intended to decrease integrity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Encryption primarily provides confidentiality. Integrity and authenticity require an additional or authenticated cryptographic mechanism.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The subset construction represents each DFA state by a subset of the NFA's state set Q. A set with |Q| elements has 2^|Q| subsets, including the empty subset, so an equivalent DFA may require as many as 2^|Q| states in the worst case.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: |Q| and 2|Q| grow only linearly and cannot represent every possible subset. Subtracting one wrongly removes the empty subset, which may be needed as the dead DFA state when no NFA state is reachable.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: NFA-to-DFA conversion uses states that are subsets of Q; the power set has 2^|Q| members.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Write the operands in binary. 1 is 001 and 2 is 010, so 1 & 2 = 000 = 0. Likewise, 3 is 011 and 4 is 100, so 3 & 4 = 000 = 0. Therefore (1 & 2) + (3 & 4) = 0 + 0 = 0.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The nonzero choices result from confusing bitwise AND with logical truth or ordinary arithmetic. A bit survives AND only when that same bit position is 1 in both operands; neither pair shares a set bit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Evaluate bitwise operators position by position before applying the surrounding arithmetic operation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: During the forward pass, activations move from input/source neurons through hidden layers to output/sink neurons. The loss is then differentiated using the chain rule, and error gradients propagate backward from the output layer toward the input layer. Those gradients determine the weight updates. Hence the defining direction of backpropagation is from sink to source.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Forward source-to-sink propagation computes predictions rather than backpropagating error. Stopping at hidden nodes describes only part of a pass and does not propagate gradients through the complete dependency chain. 'Backward from sink to hidden nodes' is therefore incomplete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Neural-network cycle: activations flow forward; loss gradients flow backward from output/sink toward input/source.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: For the one-to-one textbook comparison intended here, ECL is matched with low propagation delay because its transistors avoid saturation and it is the fastest of the three families. CMOS is matched with high fan-out because its inputs draw extremely small steady-state current. The remaining property, high power dissipation, is assigned to TTL relative to CMOS. Thus a-3, b-2, c-1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 3 assign high power dissipation to CMOS, contradicting its major low-static-power advantage. Option 4 fails to give ECL its characteristic low propagation delay. In broader comparisons ECL also consumes substantial power, but the forced one-to-one matching uses speed as ECL's distinguishing property.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For this matching set: TTL-high power, ECL-low delay, CMOS-high fan-out. Use the forced one-to-one context when properties overlap in practice.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Software quality assurance (SQA) is called an umbrella activity because it spans the entire software process. It establishes standards, reviews processes and work products, audits compliance, tracks defects and supports continuous quality improvement across communication, planning, modeling, construction and deployment.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Debugging is performed when faults are located and corrected. Testing executes or evaluates software to reveal failures, and designing creates the solution structure. Each is important but occurs as a specific engineering activity; SQA oversees quality throughout all of them.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: An umbrella activity cuts across every framework phase; SQA provides process-wide quality oversight.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Both NOR (option 2) and NAND (option 4) are commutative but not associative
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: NAND and NOR are symmetric in their two operands, so x NAND y = y NAND x and x NOR y = y NOR x: both are commutative. Neither is associative. For x=0, y=0, z=1, (x NAND y) NAND z = 0 but x NAND (y NAND z) = 1. With the same values, (x NOR y) NOR z = 0 but x NOR (y NOR z) = 1. Thus both options 2 and 4 satisfy the wording.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: OR and XOR are both commutative and associative, so options 1 and 3 do not fit. The item is defective because it asks for one operation but separately lists two correct operations, NOR and NAND.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Do not infer associativity from commutativity. OR and XOR have both properties; NAND and NOR are commutative but fail associativity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The relation must be joined with itself: p1 and p2 represent two posting rows. The predicate p1.skill=p2.skill keeps pairs requiring the same skill. The additional condition p1.posting_No<p2.posting_No removes self-pairs and keeps only one orientation of each pair, so (10,20) appears without the duplicate reverse pair (20,10). This is exactly query 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Query 1 compares each row with itself and also requires a posting number to be less than itself, so it returns nothing. Query 2 includes self-pairs and both orientations of each pair. Query 4 deliberately selects only equal posting numbers, not distinct pairs.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2012-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: To find unordered distinct pairs with a self-join, match the shared attribute and impose p1.key < p2.key.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In bidirectional data transfer, a receiver can delay its acknowledgement briefly and attach it to a data frame travelling in the reverse direction. Sending the data and acknowledgement together avoids a separate acknowledgement-only frame. This efficiency technique is called piggybacking.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 'Piggy packing', 'back packing' and 'good packing' are not the data-link protocol term. The name comes from the acknowledgement riding on, or piggybacking on, reverse-direction data.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Piggybacking combines an acknowledgement with an outgoing data frame in the reverse direction to reduce protocol overhead.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Presentation layer; none of the listed options is correct
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the seven-layer OSI reference model, the presentation layer translates data representations and is conventionally responsible for encryption/decryption and compression. It prepares application data in a form suitable for transmission and reverses the transformations at the destination.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The physical layer transmits signals, the data-link layer handles frames on one link, and the network layer routes packets. Applications may themselves perform encryption in real systems, but 'Application' is not the standard OSI-layer answer to this textbook responsibility. The paper omits Presentation from its choices, so the item is defective.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: OSI mnemonic: presentation layer handles translation, encryption and compression.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Baud rate counts signal units (symbols) transmitted per second, so 1000 signal units per second means 1000 baud. Each signal unit represents 4 bits, making the bit rate 1000 symbols/s x 4 bits/symbol = 4000 bps.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other choices either interchange baud and bit rate or fail to multiply symbols per second by bits per symbol. Baud equals bit rate only when every symbol carries one bit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Bit rate = baud rate x bits per signal unit. Here 1000 x 4 = 4000 bps.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Very-low-frequency and low-frequency radio waves have long wavelengths and can follow the curvature of Earth as ground waves. This propagation supports communication beyond the optical horizon and is used for applications such as navigation and long-range low-rate links.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Sky-wave propagation is mainly associated with ionospheric reflection at higher HF frequencies. Line-of-sight and space-wave propagation dominate VHF and above, where waves do not follow Earth's surface as effectively.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Frequency-propagation cue: VLF/LF use ground waves; HF commonly uses sky waves; VHF and above mainly use line of sight.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: RSA uses n=pq and phi(n)=(p-1)(q-1)=12 x 30=360. The public and private exponents satisfy ed congruent to 1 modulo 360. With d=7, test e=103: 7 x 103=721=2 x 360+1. Therefore e=103 is the modular inverse of 7 modulo 360.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Multiplying 7 by 101, 105 or 107 gives remainders 347, 15 and 29 modulo 360, not 1. They therefore cannot be the matching RSA exponent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Given RSA private exponent d, find e from ed mod phi(n)=1, with phi(n)=(p-1)(q-1).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Fan-in of module A is the number of other modules that directly call A or transfer control/data into it. It measures how many incoming dependencies A has. A high fan-in often indicates a reused service, although its interface must remain stable.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The number of modules called by A is fan-out, not fan-in. 'Related to' is too vague, while 'dependent on A' may describe transitive or conceptual dependence rather than the direct incoming call/control definition.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Fan-in counts incoming callers; fan-out counts modules called by the component.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Cohesion describes how strongly the data elements and operations inside one module belong together in performing a focused purpose. Good design seeks high cohesion so a module has a clear responsibility and changes for one concern remain localized.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Coupling measures dependence between different modules. Modularity is the overall division of a system into modules, and granularity describes their relative size or level of detail. Neither names the internal relationship asked here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Cohesion is within a module; coupling is between modules. Prefer high cohesion and low coupling.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Software configuration management identifies work products, establishes baselines, controls and records changes, reports configuration status and audits consistency. Its control covers normal product evolution, corrections made after defects are discovered, and approved requirement changes. Therefore all three listed sources of change are within SCM.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each describe only one reason a configuration item may change. SCM is not limited to one cause; it manages the integrity and traceability of all controlled changes throughout the lifecycle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: SCM controls versions and baselines for every approved change, whether caused by evolution, bug fixes or changing requirements.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Requirements engineering includes eliciting stakeholder needs, analyzing and negotiating them, documenting/specifying the agreed requirements, validating them and managing changes. 'Requirement design' is not a standard requirements-engineering step; design begins deciding how the system will satisfy requirements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Elicitation discovers needs, analysis resolves and models them, and documentation records them in an agreed form. All three are recognized requirements activities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Requirements state what is needed; design decides how to build it. Do not mix the design phase into requirements engineering.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Beta testing places a near-final product with selected real users in their actual operating environment, using realistic or actual data and workflows. The goal is to reveal field issues that controlled in-house testing may miss before general release.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Alpha testing is performed at the developer's site or in a controlled environment, often with developer observation. Regression testing checks whether changes have broken previously working behavior and is not defined by real-world deployment.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Alpha is controlled and developer-side; beta is user-side testing in the real environment.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The rule marks <= 100 restricts the allowed values of the marks attribute and preserves valid database state. It is a domain/check integrity constraint and can be enforced with a CHECK condition, usually together with a lower bound such as marks >= 0.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Referential integrity concerns foreign-key values matching referenced keys. 'Over-defined' and 'feasible' are not the standard database constraint types that describe this upper-bound rule.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A range rule on one attribute is a domain integrity constraint; a relationship between table keys is referential integrity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: GO BOTTOM positions the record pointer on the last record, number 30. SKIP -3 then moves backward three record positions: 30 to 29 is one, to 28 is two, and to 27 is three. Control therefore reaches the 27th record.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 28 results from moving back only two positions. Records 3 and 4 would come from treating -3 as an absolute record number or wrapping around, neither of which is how the relative SKIP command works.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: GO BOTTOM selects the last record; SKIP n moves relatively from the current record, with negative n moving backward.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An ER model identifies entity types, their attributes and the relationship types connecting them. A complete conceptual data model also records semantic integrity constraints and business rules that the diagram's basic shapes may not fully express. Statements I, II, III and IV therefore all belong to the ER modeling result.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options B, C and D each omit at least one essential structural element, such as attributes or relationships. Excluding semantic rules would also leave important domain meaning undocumented.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A conceptual ER model combines entities, attributes, relationships and relevant semantic constraints.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Cardinality ratio and participation are structural constraints of a relationship type. They determine whether foreign keys can be placed on one side, whether a separate cross-reference relation is needed, and whether mutual references are appropriate when translating an ER model into a database design.
