sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Entities and keys are model components affected by the design, but the phrase 'cardinality ratio and participation' specifically names relationship constraints. 'Rules' is too general and is not the formal completion used here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: ER relationship constraints include cardinality (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and participation (total or partial); these guide relational mapping.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Data-integrity controls keep stored values valid and consistent. Upper and lower bounds validate a numeric value against its permitted domain, rejecting entries outside the range. This directly protects correctness of the data.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Passwords are access-security controls: they restrict who can reach data but do not ensure an authorized user enters valid values. A data dictionary stores metadata, not the data itself, and it is not primarily an access-history log.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Integrity controls validate values and relationships; security controls govern authorization and access.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Read the C declaration outward from ptr. Parentheses make *ptr bind first, so ptr is a pointer. After the closing parenthesis comes [10], meaning the pointed-to object is an array of ten elements. The leading int makes each array element an integer. Thus ptr points to an array of 10 integers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: An array of pointers would be written int *ptr[10] without parentheses around *ptr. The declaration does not allocate an array object named ptr; ptr itself is one pointer. Therefore 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: In C declarations, parentheses change binding: int (*p)[10] is pointer-to-array, while int *p[10] is array-of-pointers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: All three declarations are valid C. int n=32 initializes an integer. char ch=65 converts the representable integer value 65 to char, typically the code for 'A'. float f=(float)3.2 explicitly converts the double literal 3.2 to float. Hence none has a compilation error.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each point to a legal scalar initialization. A compiler may warn about conversions under strict warning settings, but a warning is not a required C compilation error for these values and casts.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: C permits scalar initialization with representable converted values; distinguish a diagnostic warning from a language constraint error.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: C++ permits overloading arithmetic operators such as *, compound assignment operators such as +=, and comparison operators such as ==, subject to the language's operator-overload rules. The scope-resolution operator :: is fixed by the language grammar and cannot be overloaded.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options A, B and C all name overloadable operators. Overloading cannot change their precedence, associativity or number of operands, but user-defined behavior may be supplied when at least one operand has class or enumeration type.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Non-overloadable C++ operators include ::, ., .*, ?: and sizeof; scope resolution is the answer here.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Inheritance defines a new derived class from an existing base class. The derived class receives accessible base-class data and behavior and may add or specialize members of its own. It supports reuse and models an appropriate 'is-a' relationship.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Overloading gives the same name to different operations, polymorphism allows one interface to select different implementations, and encapsulation bundles state with operations while controlling access. None specifically creates a class from a parent class.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Inheritance creates derived classes from base classes; use it for genuine is-a relationships.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: For a nonnegative floating-point value x, adding 0.5 moves values with fractional part at least 0.5 into the next integer interval. Casting the entire sum to int then truncates the fractional part, producing the usual round-to-nearest result: y=(int)(x+0.5).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option B uses function-call syntax for int rather than the C-style cast expected by the question. C casts only x before adding 0.5, so assigning to an integer then truncates and fails to round up. D casts x too early and has the same problem. For negative values, the simple +0.5 formula is not symmetric; use a standard rounding function in real code.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Classic positive-number rounding uses cast after addition: (int)(x+0.5). The placement of parentheses is essential.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Evaluate postfix with a stack. Push 8, 4, 2 and 5. The + operator combines 2 and 5 to give 7, leaving 8, 4, 7. The - operator pops 7 then 4 and computes 4-7=-3. Finally * computes 8 x (-3)=-24.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The fractional options result from reversing or changing the operators, while 24 loses the negative sign. For every binary postfix operator, the first popped value is the right operand and the second popped value is the left operand.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Postfix evaluation rule: push operands; on an operator, compute second-pop operator first-pop and push the result.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In a non-empty linked queue, front already points to the oldest node and remains unchanged during enqueue. The new node is linked after the current rear, and rear is updated to point to that new last node. Therefore only the rear pointer changes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Neither pointer changing would make the new last node inaccessible through rear. Changing only front would incorrectly alter the deletion end. Both pointers change only when inserting into an empty queue, where the first node is simultaneously front and rear.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Enqueue into non-empty linked queue updates rear; enqueue into empty queue initializes both front and rear.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A recursive function reduces a problem to smaller instances of the same form. Mathematical induction mirrors this structure: prove the base case, assume correctness for smaller input, and use that assumption to prove the next case. This makes induction the standard proof method for recursive algorithms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Diagonalization proves certain existence or non-computability results, commutativity is an algebraic property, and matrix multiplication is an operation. None supplies the base-case and inductive-step structure needed here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Recursion and induction are structural partners: base case corresponds to the induction base, and the recursive step corresponds to the inductive step.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: For a B-tree of minimum degree t, every non-root node contains at least t-1 keys. A node can contain at most 2t-1 keys; if another key must be inserted, the full node is split. Correspondingly, an internal node has between t and 2t children, except for the root's special lower bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The maximum 2t+1 is too high, and t+1 is not the minimum key count. The defining occupancy interval for non-root nodes is [t-1, 2t-1].
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Minimum-degree-t B-tree bounds: non-root keys t-1 through 2t-1; children t through 2t.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Merge compares the first unconsumed elements of the two sorted lists and removes the smaller. In the worst case, neither list becomes empty until only one of the total m+n elements remains. Selecting the first m+n-1 elements therefore needs m+n-1 comparisons; the last element is then copied without comparison.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: m x n would compare every cross-list pair and is unnecessary. max(m,n) and min(m,n) underestimate interleaved worst cases. The linear bound is what makes merging efficient.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Worst-case comparisons when merging sorted lists of lengths m and n equal m+n-1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: S1 is correct: SLR places a reduction using the FOLLOW set of the production's left-hand nonterminal, while LR(1) items carry explicit lookahead symbols and LALR retains merged LR(1) lookahead information. S2 is also correct: canonical LR(1) recognizes a strictly larger grammar class than LALR(1), which in turn contains SLR(1).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options A-C declare at least one true statement false. The practical trade-off is that canonical LR tables are more powerful but larger; LALR merges states for compact tables, and SLR uses coarser FOLLOW information.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Parser power hierarchy: SLR(1) is contained in LALR(1), which is contained in canonical LR(1).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: None exactly; option D needs its final λ alternative replaced by A
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Write n=m+k-r, where k is the number of extra leading a symbols (0 to 3) and r is the number of extra trailing b symbols. Let A -> aAb | B generate equal a/b pairs followed by B, and B -> Bb | λ generate any extra b symbols. The start rule must be S -> A | aA | aaA | aaaA. This generates every a^n b^m with n-m at most 3, including b^m when n=0.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A permits only surplus 3. B adds only the empty string, and C permits surpluses 2 or 3 plus empty. D is closest but uses λ instead of A; consequently it cannot generate b, bb and other required strings with n=0,m>0. The printed choices therefore omit the correct grammar.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For n<=m+3, allow 0, 1, 2 or 3 unpaired leading a symbols, then generate matched a/b pairs and arbitrary extra trailing b symbols.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: S1 is true. Regular languages are closed under reversal, so L^R is regular when L is regular; they are also closed under concatenation, so L L^R={uv | u in L, v in L^R} is regular. S2 is false: {ww^R} is the language of even-length palindromes, which requires unbounded comparison between the first half and its reverse and is not regular.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and B incorrectly reject the closure argument in S1. D incorrectly accepts the palindrome language as regular. A finite automaton cannot remember an arbitrarily long first half to verify the second half.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Regular languages are closed under reversal and concatenation, but the language of arbitrary palindromes is not regular.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Relocation assigns actual load addresses to program sections and adjusts address-sensitive instructions and data so they refer to the correct locations. A relocating linker or loader uses relocation entries emitted by the assembler to perform these changes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Symbol resolution matches references with definitions but does not by itself adjust every address after placement. Parsing analyzes grammatical structure, and assembly translates assembly language into object code. The address-adjustment process specifically is relocation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Relocation = place program parts at load addresses and patch address-dependent code/data to match those placements.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A top-down parser begins with the start symbol and repeatedly expands the leftmost unexpanded nonterminal to predict the input from left to right. It therefore constructs a leftmost derivation of the input string.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A bottom-up LR parser reconstructs a rightmost derivation in reverse. The reverse-derivation descriptions therefore belong to bottom-up parsing, while a rightmost derivation is not the standard expansion strategy of a left-to-right top-down parser.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Top-down parsing produces a leftmost derivation; bottom-up LR parsing produces a rightmost derivation in reverse.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The duality principle for Boolean algebra replaces every OR (+) by AND (·), every AND by OR, every 0 by 1 and every 1 by 0, while variables and complements remain unchanged. Both kinds of interchange must be performed together to obtain the dual expression.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A changes the operations but omits the constants. D changes the constants but omits the operations. B says either transformation is enough, whereas the dual requires both simultaneously.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Boolean dual: swap + with · and 0 with 1 everywhere; do not complement the variables merely to form the dual.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Interpret (1204) in base x: x^3+2x^2+0x+4=292. Testing x=6 gives 216+72+4=292, so the base is 6. The digit 4 is valid because a base must exceed its largest digit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Base 2 is impossible because digit 4 is not legal. Bases 8 and 10 give 1028 and 1204 respectively, not 292. Since the correct base 6 is not listed, 'none of the above' is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A numeral d3d2d1d0 in base x has value d3x^3+d2x^2+d1x+d0; also require x greater than every digit.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: F=(x+y)z̄ is 1 exactly when z=0 and at least one of x,y is 1. The satisfying assignments are (x,y,z)=(1,1,0),(1,0,0),(0,1,0). Their minterms are xyz̄, xȳz̄ and x̄yz̄, whose sum is option D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Any term containing uncomplemented z represents a row where z=1, but F must then be 0. Option C includes x̄ȳz̄, where x+y=0. The other options likewise include a false row or omit a required true row.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For canonical SOP, list every input row making F=1 and write one minterm containing all variables for each row.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Statement I says every positive integer m divides every positive integer n, which is false; for example, 2 does not divide 1. Statement II asks whether one positive integer divides every positive integer. Choosing m=1 makes P(1,n) true for every n, so II is true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and D incorrectly make the universal statement I true. B overlooks the witness m=1 for the existential statement II. One counterexample disproves a universal claim, while one valid witness proves an existential claim.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Quantifier strategy: refute ∀ with a counterexample; prove ∃ by giving a witness. Here 2∤1, but 1 divides all positive integers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: For large x, log(x²+1)=Θ(log x), so (x+1)log(x²+1)=Θ(x log x). The remaining term is 3x²=Θ(x²), which grows faster than x log x. The whole sum is therefore Θ(x²), and hence O(x²).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: O(x log x) is too small because of the quadratic term. O(x³) and O(x² log x) are valid loose upper bounds in a purely formal sense, but the question asks for the standard tight Big-O estimate among the choices; O(x²) is the tightest listed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For sums, keep the asymptotically dominant term. Polynomial x² dominates x log x.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Each tree with n_i vertices has n_i-1 edges. A forest of t disjoint trees with total vertices n has Σ(n_i-1)=Σn_i-t=n-t edges.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Adding t has the wrong sign, multiplying n and t ignores tree structure, and n^t has no connection to edge counting. Each connected tree component contributes exactly one fewer edge than vertices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A forest with n vertices and t connected components has exactly n-t edges.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Composition order matters. (f∘g)(x)=f(3x+2)=2(3x+2)+3=6x+7. Conversely, (g∘f)(x)=g(2x+3)=3(2x+3)+2=6x+11. Thus the requested pair is 6x+7, 6x+11.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: B reverses the two compositions. C incorrectly adds the functions instead of substituting one into the other. Since A matches both substitutions, 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Read f∘g as f after g: substitute the complete formula for g(x) into f.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: P(n,r)=n!/(n-r)!. Therefore P(n+1,r)=(n+1)!/(n+1-r)!. Divide by P(n,r): the ratio is (n+1)/(n+1-r). Hence P(n+1,r)=P(n,r)(n+1)/(n+1-r).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other denominators or numerator n-1 do not arise when cancelling the two factorial formulas. A quick check with r=1 also works: P(n+1,1)=n+1, which only option A reproduces from P(n,1)=n.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Use P(n,r)=n!/(n-r)! and simplify factorial ratios before selecting an identity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Kuratowski's theorem states that a finite graph is non-planar exactly when it contains a subdivision, or a subgraph homeomorphic to, K5 or K3,3. Subdividing edges adds degree-2 vertices without changing the underlying obstruction to planarity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: K3,2 and K2,3 are planar complete bipartite graphs. K6 is non-planar but is not one of the two minimal homeomorphic obstructions named by the theorem. The condition uses 'or', because either obstruction suffices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: The two Kuratowski obstructions are K5 and K3,3.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: I is true: a half adder adds two input bits and produces sum and carry. II is false because that description is a half adder, not a full adder. III is true: a full adder accepts two operand bits plus carry-in and produces sum and carry-out. IV is true because an inverter implements Boolean NOT.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A includes false statement II and omits true III and IV. B includes II and omits I and IV. C also includes II and omits IV. Only D contains exactly I, III and IV.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Half adder has two inputs; full adder has three inputs including carry-in; inverter outputs the complement.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: ActiveX controls were compiled Windows binary components that Internet Explorer and related container applications could embed in web pages to provide interactive functionality. They executed native code on the client machine, which also created substantial security risk.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A URL is an address, not a page container, and 'script page' is not the defining host. Word can host some embedded objects, but the exam statement specifically describes the historical use of ActiveX controls in web pages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: ActiveX was native Windows code embedded in web pages, unlike portable sandboxed web scripting; it is now obsolete and unsafe for modern web design.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: WAE contains WML/WMLScript facilities, so it matches Scripts (iii). WTP supplies transaction services analogous to web request handling and is matched with HTTP (i) in this comparison. WDP provides a datagram service analogous to UDP, so c-iv. The remaining printed 'Wireless' item maps to IP (ii). The code is therefore iii, i, iv, ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes fail at least the two strongest anchors: WAE must map to scripts and WDP to UDP. The source itself truncates list item d to 'Wireless', so the last pairing is obtained by the one-to-one matching after those protocol correspondences are fixed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: WAP stack cues: WAE hosts WMLScript; WTP handles transactions; WDP offers UDP-like datagrams over wireless bearers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: ATM, or Asynchronous Transfer Mode, was designed for high-speed integrated voice, video and data transport using fixed 53-byte cells. Telephone carriers deployed it in backbone and long-haul networks because fixed-size cells support predictable switching and quality-of-service classes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: ISDN provides digital subscriber access rather than the principal long-haul backbone technology described. Frame Relay is a variable-length data service without ATM's cell-based integrated traffic design. ISTN is not the relevant standard.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Historical telecom association: ATM used fixed-size cells for carrier backbones and long-haul mixed traffic.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the United States, cross-industry EDI document standards are maintained through ANSI's Accredited Standards Committee X12. The X12 transaction sets standardize business documents such as purchase orders, invoices and shipping notices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: ISO publishes the international EDIFACT family through UN-related standardization work but is not the organization intended by this history of the early U.S. business standards. ITU-T focuses on telecommunications, and IEEE is best known for electrical, computing and networking standards.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: EDI standards cue: ANSI ASC X12 is the major U.S. business-document standard; UN/EDIFACT is the international counterpart.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An EDI software path begins with the business application, converts its internal proprietary format, translates the result into the agreed EDI standard format, and wraps it in an EDI envelope for interchange. Thus the four named layers are business application, internal format conversion, EDI translator and EDI envelope.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A incorrectly calls the standards-conversion component a network translator. C and D substitute generic Internet/OSI transport layers for the EDI application-processing stages. IP transport may carry messages but is not one of these four EDI software layers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: EDI flow: business application → internal-format conversion → EDI-standard translation → EDI envelope.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: At each one-unit quantum, a process returning to the ready queue competes with new and waiting processes; waiting priority grows faster (rate 2) than running priority (rate 1), and ties favor the least recently executed. The resulting CPU sequence is P1, P2, P1, P3, P4, P1, P3, P1. P2 completes at time 2, P4 at 5, P3 at 7 and P1 at 8. In the requested P1,P2,P3,P4 order, the finish times are 8,2,7,5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A is simple arrival-order completion and ignores dynamic priority/preemption. C gives an impossible finish time 2 for P1, which needs four CPU units. D swaps the completion order of P3 and P4; P4's single quantum completes at time 5, while P3 needs its second quantum at time 7.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For dynamic-priority scheduling, build a one-quantum timeline and update ready-queue priorities at every boundary; report completion times in process-ID order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A 4 KB page uses 12 offset bits, leaving a 20-bit virtual page number in a 32-bit address. A 64-entry, 4-way TLB has 64/4=16 sets, requiring 4 set-index bits. The remaining virtual-page-number bits form the tag: 20-4=16 bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 12 bits is the page offset, not the tag. 20 bits would be the tag only for a fully associative TLB with no set index. 15 bits does not follow from either the page size or 16-set organization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Set-associative TLB tag bits = virtual-address bits - page-offset bits - set-index bits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: SSTF services the nearest pending cylinder in the order 53→65→67→37→14→98→122→124→183. The movements 12+2+30+23+84+24+2+59 total 236. For SCAN moving downward, the head goes from 53 to cylinder 0, then reverses and continues to the far end cylinder 199; total movement is 53+199=252 cylinders while servicing requests along the path.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 235 is an arithmetic error in the SSTF path. 640 is far too large and typically results from repeatedly measuring from the initial position. A SCAN total of 236 would stop at the last request 183, which is LOOK behavior; standard SCAN continues to the physical end of the disk.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: SSTF repeatedly chooses the closest request. SCAN travels to a disk end before reversing; LOOK reverses at the last pending request.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: With binary storage units, the number of 512-byte blocks is (1.3×2^30)/2^9=1.3×2^21. A bitmap needs one bit per block, so its size is (1.3×2^21)/8=1.3×2^18 bytes. Dividing by 2^10 bytes per KB gives 1.3×2^8=332.8 KB.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other values result from losing a factor of 4, using the wrong block count, or mixing bits and bytes. The sequence must be disk bytes ÷ bytes per block ÷ 8 bits per bitmap byte.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Bitmap size in bytes = disk size in bytes / block size in bytes / 8.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Modern Linux uses the Completely Fair Scheduler for ordinary tasks. CFS is preemptive and attempts to distribute CPU time fairly by tracking each runnable task's virtual runtime and favoring the task that has received the least weighted CPU service.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Processor affinity constrains where a task may run but is not the overall scheduling policy. Handshaking is a synchronization/communication concept. Highest Penalty Ratio Next is a different non-preemptive response-ratio policy, not Linux's general scheduler.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Linux CFS is a fair preemptive scheduler based on weighted virtual runtime.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Weak duality says every feasible dual objective bounds every feasible primal objective. If the primal objective can improve without bound while the dual had a feasible solution with a finite objective, that dual value would provide a bound—a contradiction. Therefore an unbounded primal implies an infeasible dual.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A feasible dual cannot coexist with an unbounded primal under the usual primal-dual pair. Alternative optima concern multiple finite optimal points. 'No bounded solution' is imprecise; the definite conclusion is that no dual feasible solution exists.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Primal unbounded ⇒ dual infeasible. Dual unbounded ⇒ primal infeasible; an infeasible problem does not alone determine the other problem's status.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The Karush-Kuhn-Tucker necessary conditions include stationarity of the Lagrangian with respect to every decision variable, primal feasibility gi(x̄)≤bi, dual feasibility for the multipliers/slacks under the chosen sign convention, and complementary slackness λ̄i[gi(x̄)-bi]=0. The three displayed statements are members of this set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each state only one necessary part: stationarity, complementary slackness or primal feasibility. Selecting one alone omits the others, so the complete answer is all of these.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: KKT checklist: stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility and complementary slackness.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: From x1-x2≥0 and x1,x2≥0, we have x2≤x1. Therefore 3x1-x2≥3x1-x1=2x1≥0. The second constraint demands the same expression be at most -3, which is impossible. The feasible region is empty.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: With no point satisfying both constraints, the program cannot have a feasible, unbounded or single-point solution. Objective maximization is irrelevant once constraint feasibility fails.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Test LP feasibility before optimizing; combine inequalities to expose contradictions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: For a connected flow graph, cyclomatic complexity is V=E-N+2=13-10+2=5. This equals the number of regions, including the outside region. For a connected structured flow graph V=P+1, where P is the number of predicate nodes, so P=4. The pair is 5 regions and 4 predicates.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other pairs fail either the edge-node formula or V=P+1. A reverses the correct quantities; C and D do not match the graph's cyclomatic complexity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Connected flow graph: V=E-N+2=regions=predicate nodes+1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Unadjusted Function Points count weighted external inputs, outputs, inquiries, internal logical files and external interface files. The adjusted function-point value is FP=UFP×CAF, where CAF is the complexity adjustment factor derived from general system characteristics.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: FAC reverses the accepted acronym. Cost and productivity may later be estimated using function points, but they are not multipliers in the function-point calculation itself.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Adjusted function points = UFP × complexity adjustment factor (CAF).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Data coupling passes only the needed data (ii). Stamp coupling passes an entire composite structure (iii). Common coupling occurs when modules share global data (i). Content coupling is the strongest form listed, where one module transfers control into or depends on the internals of another (iv). Thus a-ii,b-iii,c-i,d-iv.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes swap the distinguishing definitions. Use 'stamp' for a whole record/structure, 'common' for shared globals and 'content' for intrusion into another module's implementation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Coupling quality generally worsens from data to stamp to common to content coupling.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Software configuration management identifies configuration items, establishes baselines, controls and records changes, constructs and tracks versions, and audits evolving configurations for integrity and quality. Those are exactly the four objectives described.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Quality, version and change management each cover part of the list, but SCM is the umbrella discipline integrating identification, change control, version construction and configuration auditing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: SCM unifies configuration identification, version control, change control, status accounting and audits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Equivalence partitioning selects representatives from input classes, and boundary-value analysis targets values at class edges. Applied one variable at a time, neither systematically covers interactions among several input conditions. Combination-oriented methods such as decision tables, pairwise testing or cause-effect graphs address that gap.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The techniques are effective for their intended purpose, so A is too broad. C states the opposite of the weakness. Therefore 'none' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Equivalence and boundary tests cover individual input domains well but may miss multi-input interactions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: D is a realistic management principle: an organization that cannot govern internal software work will not automatically gain control by outsourcing it. Contracts do not replace requirements clarity, monitoring and technical governance. The other three are classic software myths.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A ignores maintenance, B underestimates the cost and ripple effects of change, and C conflicts with Brooks's law: adding people to a late project can make it later because of training and communication overhead.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Outsourcing transfers implementation work, not the need for competent project governance.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A state diagram describes the behavior of one object class over time (iv). An object diagram is useful for abstract modeling and concrete program design (i). A class diagram describes object classes and their static relationships (ii). An instance diagram can document concrete test-case configurations (iii). The mapping is iv,i,ii,iii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes confuse static class descriptions with behavioral state models or swap general object models with concrete instance snapshots. Fixing state→iv and class→ii leaves only option A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: UML cue: class diagram is static type structure; object/instance diagram is a concrete snapshot; state diagram is lifecycle behavior.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A coherent method performs one function or closely related functions (iv). Keeping methods small means breaking a large method into smaller parts (iii). Consistency means maintaining parallel structure and conventions (ii). Uniform coverage means providing corresponding operations, such as a method to get the last list element (i). Thus iv,iii,ii,i.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Each alternative mismatches at least one direct definition. 'Small' anchors to breaking apart, while 'coherent' anchors to a single focused responsibility.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Reusable methods should be focused, small, stylistically consistent and provide a uniform family of operations.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: SOAP is an XML-based messaging protocol designed for structured information exchange between applications independent of implementation language and operating system. Web-service operations can use SOAP messages to implement remote procedure calls, commonly transported over HTTP.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: HTTP transports resources/messages but does not itself define language-neutral RPC envelopes. SNMP manages network devices, and SMTP transfers email. Neither is the requested application RPC protocol.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: SOAP supplies the structured, language-neutral message envelope; HTTP is often only its transport.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: XSLT transformations are defined in an XSLT stylesheet. The stylesheet contains templates that match XML nodes and specifies the result structure, which may be XML, HTML or text.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: An HTML page can be an output but does not direct XSLT. A document type declaration describes allowed XML structure, and a stored procedure is database code. Neither contains XSLT transformation templates.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: XML data + XSLT stylesheet → transformed XML, HTML or text output.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Dynamic loading brings a code module or class into a running program only when it is needed. This lets the application acquire functionality without linking and loading every component at startup.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Data hiding restricts access to representation, dynamic typing determines/checks types at runtime, and dynamic binding selects an overridden method at runtime. None specifically adds program code during execution.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Dynamic loading adds code at runtime; dynamic binding chooses behavior at runtime; dynamic typing determines types at runtime.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Function overloading provides several implementations under one function name, with selection based on the compile-time argument types and arity. This is ad-hoc polymorphism because behavior is defined separately for a finite set of type signatures.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Virtual polymorphism refers to subtype/dynamic dispatch, not overload resolution. 'Transient' and 'pseudo' are not the standard classification of function overloading.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Overloading is compile-time ad-hoc polymorphism; overriding through virtual methods is runtime subtype polymorphism.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Pascal is the structured-language example (a-iii). Traditional FORTRAN is the non-structured example (b-iv). Java is object-oriented (c-i), and classic BASIC is commonly interpreted (d-ii). The complete code is iii,iv,i,ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes assign at least one language to the wrong paradigm. Java→object-oriented and BASIC→interpreted are strong anchors; Pascal and early FORTRAN then distinguish structured from non-structured programming.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Exam-era associations: Pascal-structured, FORTRAN-non-structured, Java-object-oriented, BASIC-interpreted.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: When an expression contains operands of different ranks, the language's usual arithmetic rules convert narrower operands toward a wider common type so the operation can be performed consistently. This automatic upward conversion is type promotion.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Type conversion is a broad term covering many explicit and implicit changes, but promotion specifically means conversion to a higher-ranked type. Evaluation computes an expression, and declaration introduces a typed name.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Promotion is an implicit widening conversion used to bring operands to a common higher-ranked type.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: C++ retains C's dynamic allocation system, malloc/calloc/realloc with free, and adds its own type-aware new/delete system. new allocates storage and constructs objects; delete destroys objects and releases storage.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The two systems are not both unique to C++, nor are both merely unspecified or generic improvements. One is inherited from the C library and the other is native C++ syntax and semantics.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Do not mix allocation families: pair malloc with free and new with delete.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: new obtains dynamic storage (and initializes/constructs the object), while delete destroys the object and releases storage previously obtained with new. Together they provide the allocation and deallocation lifecycle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and B each name only half of the pair's purpose. C notes an advantage of new over raw byte allocation but omits the central allocation/deallocation combination asked by the item.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Use new/delete as a matched pair; in modern C++, prefer RAII containers and smart pointers to manual ownership.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Forward movement selects a subgoal that has a possible unifier (iv). Unification binds a variable to a constant, variable or term (i). Deep backtracking restarts/reconsiders the conjunctive goal more broadly (ii), whereas shallow backtracking returns to a previous subgoal to try another solution (iii). Thus iv,i,ii,iii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes confuse unification with search movement or interchange deep and shallow backtracking. Variable binding is the direct anchor for unification.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Prolog uses unification for bindings and backtracking to explore alternative clauses/subgoals.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: S1 is true: adding S→SS to the first grammar permits concatenations, such as strings with multiple central a contributions, that the grammar without SS cannot generate. S2 is also true: S→SS already makes SSS derivable by expanding one of the two S symbols again, so explicitly adding S→SSS does not change the language.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and C incorrectly reject one true statement, while D rejects both. A production is redundant when its effect can already be obtained through repeated existing productions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: To compare grammars, test whether an added production generates new terminal strings or is derivable from existing recursive rules.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Both T inputs are tied to 1, so each T flip-flop toggles on its active clock edge. Q0 toggles on every external clock. The second flip-flop is clocked by Q0 and toggles on each Q0 rising edge. Starting Q1Q0=00, the states after successive external clocks are 11, 10, 01 and 00. Thus after four cycles the state is 00.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 11, 10 and 01 are intermediate states after the first, second and third cycles. Counting only Q0 transitions or toggling Q1 on both edges produces the wrong sequence.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: For a ripple counter, record which edge clocks each stage and trace the state one external clock at a time.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Pragmatics studies how context, speaker intention and situation affect the interpretation of an utterance. The same sentence can convey different meanings in different conversational settings, which is exactly the knowledge described.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Morphology studies word structure, syntax studies grammatical arrangement, and semantics studies conventional literal meaning. Context-dependent intended meaning is pragmatic.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: NLP levels: morphology-word form, syntax-structure, semantics-literal meaning, pragmatics-contextual meaning.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Unification attempts to find a substitution for variables that makes two literals or terms identical. If one exists, a most general unifier is preferred because it imposes no unnecessary bindings.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Validation is not the object returned by unification. Option C reverses the procedure and its result. Minimax is an adversarial-search method unrelated to matching logical literals.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Unification procedure → substitution (ideally an MGU) that makes terms match.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A* is optimal when its heuristic is admissible: h*(N)≤h(N) for every node, so it never overestimates the true remaining cost. The intended option is that the estimate underestimates the actual cost; equality is also allowed as the limiting perfect-heuristic case.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Overestimation can cause A* to ignore the optimal path. A states only exact equality, which is sufficient but unnecessarily stronger than the general admissibility condition. The complete rule is non-overestimation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: A* tree-search optimality uses an admissible heuristic: 0≤h*≤true remaining cost; consistency is the stronger graph-search condition.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Means-ends analysis chooses an operator that would reduce the current-goal difference. If its preconditions are not satisfied, establishing those preconditions becomes a new subgoal. This backward-chaining pattern is called operator subgoaling.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Backward planning and goal-stack planning are broader planning approaches. Operator overloading is a programming-language feature and has nothing to do with planning preconditions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Operator subgoaling turns an unavailable operator's preconditions into intermediate goals.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: At a maximizing node, a beta cutoff occurs once the node's value is at least the best upper bound beta already available to an ancestor MIN node. At a minimizing node, an alpha cutoff occurs once the value is at most alpha. Therefore the pair is beta, alpha.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A reverses the levels; C and D use one bound for both node types. Alpha records MAX's best lower bound, while beta records MIN's best upper bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: MAX level: beta cutoff. MIN level: alpha cutoff.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The standard fuzzy intersection uses the componentwise minimum. Taking min for each pair gives {min(0.2,0.1),min(0.5,0.5),min(0.6,0.2),min(0.1,0.7),min(0.9,0.8)}={0.1,0.5,0.2,0.1,0.8}.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A resembles componentwise maximum (union). B and C use the larger membership in at least one position. Intersection cannot exceed either input membership at the same element.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Standard fuzzy union uses max; standard fuzzy intersection uses min; complement uses 1-μ.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The height of a fuzzy set is the supremum of its membership grades. A fuzzy set is normal when at least one element has full membership grade 1, so its height equals 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Membership grades lie between 0 and 1, making a negative height impossible. Height 0 is the empty fuzzy set, while height below 1 describes a subnormal fuzzy set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Normal fuzzy set: max/sup membership is 1. Subnormal: every membership is below 1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A neuron first forms the weighted input sum net=Σ(wi·xi), usually with an added bias. The activation function φ then maps this net value to the neuron's output.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Summing weights alone ignores inputs, summing inputs alone ignores learned importance, and adding the two separate sums is not a dot product. Neural computation multiplies each input by its corresponding weight before summation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Artificial neuron: output=φ(Σwixi+b).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The compression ratio is CR=n1/n2, where n1 is the size of the original representation and n2 is the size after compression. Therefore 1/CR=n2/n1 is the fraction of the original data that remains. Subtracting it from 1 gives RD=1-n2/n1, the fraction removed because it was redundant. RD consequently means the relative data redundancy of the first dataset.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Data compression is the process, while CR measures its ratio; RD measures the redundant fraction eliminated. Data relation and data representation are not the quantities defined by this standard formula.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: CR tells how much smaller the compressed data is; RD=1-1/CR tells what fraction of the original representation was redundant.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: AES supports three standardized key lengths: 128, 192 and 256 bits. It does not use 56-bit or 124-bit keys, so statement C is false. The distinction is easy to remember: 56 bits is associated with the effective key length of DES, not AES.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A correctly describes symmetric cryptography: the same secret key is used for encryption and decryption. B is historically correct because DES was widely deployed. D correctly describes public-key cryptography, which uses a related public/private key pair rather than one shared secret.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Memorize the standard AES key sizes as 128/192/256 bits; DES uses a 56-bit effective key.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The generator polynomial x^3+1 corresponds to the bit pattern 1001 and has degree 3. Append three zeros to the data: 11001001 000. Modulo-2 long division of 11001001000 by 1001 gives remainder 011. Replace the appended zeros with this remainder, producing the transmitted codeword 11001001 011 = 11001001011. At the receiver, dividing that codeword by 1001 gives zero remainder when no error occurred.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: B sends the original message without a CRC. A and C append bit strings of the wrong construction or length; a degree-3 generator requires exactly three CRC bits, so the transmitted word must contain 8+3=11 bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: CRC steps: convert the polynomial to bits, append r zeros for degree r, divide with XOR, and append the r-bit remainder.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.answerText: For even n, option C: 3n/2-2 comparisons. For odd n, the exact bound is 3(n-1)/2, so the printed question has no single formula valid for every n.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Use the pairwise method. First compare the two elements in each pair; compare the larger one only with the current maximum and the smaller one only with the current minimum. For even n, initialization uses one comparison and the remaining (n-2)/2 pairs use three each: 1+3(n-2)/2=3n/2-2. For odd n, initialize both extrema with the unpaired element and process (n-1)/2 pairs, giving 3(n-1)/2 comparisons. Thus option C is the familiar intended answer under the usual even-n assumption, but its floor formula is one comparison too small when n is odd.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, 2n-2, is the cost of finding minimum and maximum in two independent scans and is not optimal. B and D are not attainable comparison bounds for arbitrary unsorted inputs. C is exact for even n only, despite being the intended choice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Compare elements in pairs: one comparison separates a minimum candidate from a maximum candidate, saving about n/2 comparisons.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Strassen's divide-and-conquer method partitions each matrix into four blocks but computes the block products using seven recursive multiplications instead of eight. Its recurrence is T(n)=7T(n/2)+O(n^2). By the Master theorem, T(n)=Theta(n^(log2 7)); since log2 7 is about 2.807, this is conventionally written O(n^2.81).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: O(n^3) is the classical three-loop algorithm, not the more efficient algorithm intended here. The listed O(n^2.67) is not Strassen's bound. O(n^2) would match the input/output size scale but is not achieved by Strassen and was not a known general matrix-multiplication bound in this exam context.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Strassen: seven half-size multiplications lead to n^(log2 7) ≈ n^2.81.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.answerText: The printed question is under-specified: Theta(n^2) if m<4, Theta(n^2 log n) if m=4, and Theta(n^(log2 m)) if m>4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Write the recurrence in Master-theorem form T(n)=mT(n/2)+Theta(n^2). The critical exponent is log2 m, so compare n^2 with n^(log2 m). If m<4, then log2 m<2 and the nonrecursive n^2 work dominates, giving Theta(n^2). If m=4, the two terms balance, giving Theta(n^2 log n). If m>4, the recursive leaves dominate, giving Theta(n^(log2 m)). Because the paper supplies no value or range for m, none of A, B or C is the unique answer for all m.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A is valid only when m<4, C only when m=4, and B is the tight bound only when m>4. D does not arise for positive constant m with an n^2 combine term. Choosing one option without a condition on m hides the missing information.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: For T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n), compare f(n) with n^(log_b a); here the threshold is m=4.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A common subsequence of length 4 exists: B-C-B-A occurs in X at positions 2,3,4,6 and in Y at positions 1,3,5,6. Dynamic programming confirms no length-5 common subsequence exists. Using L[i,j]=L[i-1,j-1]+1 when the current symbols match and max(L[i-1,j],L[i,j-1]) otherwise, the final table entry is L[7,6]=4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Lengths 2 and 3 are possible but not longest because the explicit subsequence BCBA has length 4. Length 5 is ruled out by the LCS dynamic-programming result.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: To establish an LCS answer, exhibit a subsequence of that length and use the DP recurrence to rule out a longer one.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: For integer keys in [0,m-1], create m buckets or counters, scan the n input keys to place/count them, and then scan the m bucket positions to emit them in order. These two phases cost Theta(n) and Theta(m), so the total running time is Theta(n+m).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Theta(n) ignores the need to initialize or scan m possible key positions unless m is assumed proportional to n. Theta(n log n) is a comparison-sort bound and is unnecessary when keys can index buckets directly. Theta(n log m) does not describe the counting/bucket procedure.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Direct-address sorting pays once for the n records and once for the m-value key universe: n+m.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: In the terminology intended by this question, a clique is a maximal complete subgraph: it is complete and cannot be enlarged by adding another adjacent vertex. A minimal vertex cover covers every edge, but removing any one of its vertices would leave some edge uncovered. Hence the blanks are maximal and minimal.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and B incorrectly call the complete subgraph minimal. C incorrectly calls the vertex cover maximal; adding vertices preserves the cover property but does not express the economical irredundancy normally required here. Note that some modern texts call every complete vertex subset a clique and reserve 'maximal clique' for the inclusion-maximal case; the exam is using the stricter wording in its blank.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Maximal means cannot be enlarged; minimal means no element can be removed while preserving the property.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The context-free pumping lemma says that every sufficiently long w in L can be written w=uvxyz so that |vxy|≤m, |vy|≥1, and uv^i xy^i z belongs to L for every i≥0. The first inequality keeps both pumped pieces within a bounded window; the second ensures that at least one of v or y is nonempty, so pumping actually changes the string.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Any option with |vxy|≥m reverses the bounded-window condition. Any option with |vy|≤1 permits both pumped portions to be empty and does not state the required non-emptiness. Therefore only B contains both standard inequalities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: CFL pumping lemma memory aid: the middle window is at most m, but the total pumped material is at least 1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The grammar S→aSB | b and B→b generates exactly a^n b^(n+1). The base rule S→b gives n=0. Each application of S→aSB adds one a at the front and one B at the end; converting every B to b adds one matching trailing b, while the base b supplies the one extra b. It is in Greibach normal form because every production begins with a terminal followed only by zero or more variables.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A allows B→lambda and therefore does not force one trailing b per recursive a. B has no terminating production for S, so it cannot derive a terminal string. D describes the language but is not in GNF because its right side contains terminal b after variable S.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: In GNF every rule has the form A→aA1...Ak: one leading terminal, then variables only.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Every context-sensitive language is decidable: a linear-bounded automaton has only finitely many configurations on a fixed input, so its acceptance can be decided by exploring the configuration graph without looping forever. Thus every context-sensitive language is recursive. The containment is proper; the recursive languages include languages that require more than linear space and are not context-sensitive. Therefore both S1 and S2 are correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and B deny the valid inclusion CSL⊆Recursive. C incorrectly treats that inclusion as equality and denies that recursive languages exist outside CSL.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Chomsky/decidability containment: regular ⊂ context-free ⊂ context-sensitive ⊂ recursive ⊂ recursively enumerable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: One frame contains 800×600=480,000 pixels. At 8 bits per pixel, that is 3,840,000 bits per frame. At 40 frames per second, the stream requires 3,840,000×40=153,600,000 bits/s=153.6 megabits/s, using the decimal network-rate unit 1 Mbps=10^6 bits/s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 15.36 Mbps misses a factor of 10, 2.4 Mbps omits major factors from colour depth or frame rate, and 1536 Mbps adds an extra factor of 10.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Uncompressed video rate = width × height × bits per pixel × frames per second.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Under historical classful IPv4 addressing, the first octet determines the class. Class C addresses begin with bit prefix 110 and have first-octet values 192 through 223. The address 200.200.200.200 begins with 200, which lies in that interval, so it is Class C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Class A uses first octets 1-126, Class B uses 128-191, and Class D multicast addresses use 224-239. None includes 200.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Classful first-octet ranges: A 1-126, B 128-191, C 192-223, D 224-239.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The transport layer provides end-to-end delivery between processes. It segments a large message into smaller transport units, numbers or otherwise tracks them, and reassembles them in the correct order at the destination. These are exactly the decomposition, sequencing and end-to-end recomposition functions in the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The physical layer transmits raw bits. The data-link layer frames data for a single link rather than providing end-to-end message reassembly. The application layer supplies network services to applications but delegates segmentation and sequencing to transport.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Transport is the end-to-end layer for segmentation, sequencing, reliability and reassembly.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A request travels from the ground client up 40,000 km to the satellite and down 40,000 km to the ground server: 80,000 km. The response follows the same two-hop route back, so the best-case propagation distance is 4×40,000=160,000 km. Ignoring transmission, processing and queuing delays as best-case assumptions, delay=distance/speed=160,000/300,000 second=0.53333 second=533.33 ms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 133.33 ms counts only one ground-to-satellite leg. 266.67 ms counts a one-way client-to-server route through the satellite. 400 ms omits one of the four 40,000 km legs. A response time must include both the request and its reply.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Satellite request-response propagation uses four altitude legs: up, down, up, down.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: In asynchronous serial communication there is no continuously shared clock. A start bit announces the beginning of a character so the receiver can align its sampling times, and one or more stop bits mark the return to the idle state and provide separation before the next character. Their primary purpose is therefore character-level synchronization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Start and stop bits do not calculate a parity or checksum, so they are not an error-detection code. They cannot reconstruct corrupted data, so they do not perform error correction. They add overhead and may reduce efficiency, but slowing communication is a consequence rather than their purpose.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Asynchronous serial frames use start/stop bits to synchronize each character without a shared clock line.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Attenuation is the gradual loss of signal amplitude or power as a signal propagates through a medium. Resistance converts some electrical energy to heat, so a received signal is weaker than the transmitted signal. Amplifiers or repeaters may be used to compensate over long distances.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Distortion changes the signal's shape because components experience different delays or attenuation. Noise is unwanted added energy or interference. A decibel is a logarithmic unit used to express gain or loss; it is not itself a transmission impairment.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Attenuation means loss of strength; distortion means change of shape; noise means an unwanted added signal.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Match each mode by how it obtains the operand. Indexed addressing uses a register plus a constant/displacement to address memory, so a→iii. Direct addressing embeds a fixed memory address and is suitable for globals known at compile time, so b→iv. Register addressing names a register and does not use a memory address for the operand, so c→i. Base-indexed addressing combines two registers, optionally with an offset, so d→ii. The order is iii, iv, i, ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and B treat indexed addressing as the two-register form, which belongs to base-indexed addressing. D gets the first mapping right but swaps direct/register or base-indexed behavior. Checking all four positions leaves only C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Direct=fixed address; register=operand in register; indexed=register+displacement; base-indexed=base+index(+displacement).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: An instruction format must balance several linked constraints. Its total size affects memory use and fetch bandwidth; the number of address bits determines the directly addressable register or memory space; and the opcode/function fields must provide enough distinct encodings for every required operation. Since A, B and C are all genuine design criteria, the inclusive answer is all of these.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each identify only one part of the design problem. Selecting any one alone ignores the trade-off among instruction length, operand-address capacity and opcode capacity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: An instruction format divides a finite bit budget among opcode, operand/address and mode fields.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Synchronous digital components coordinate state changes with a master clock generator. That timing device produces a periodic train of clock pulses; the active edge or level of each pulse tells registers and control circuits when an operation may occur. Therefore the complete pair is master clock generator, clock pulse.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A omits the standard qualifier master, B uses the nonstandard term master generator, and C reverses the roles into an imprecise generator/clock pair. D supplies both standard names.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: The master clock generator is the timing source; its periodic clock pulses synchronize digital operations.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Serial-access memory is appropriate when records form an ordered stream and are processed sequentially, usually in the same manner. Its access time depends on how far the required item is from the current position, so it is not chosen for arbitrary fast access. None of the offered descriptions states the actual sequential-stream use case, making 'None of these' correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Whether data consists of numbers is irrelevant to the access method. Short access time is a strength of random-access memory, not serial access. Processing each word differently does not create a reason for sequential access; the usual application processes an ordered sequence uniformly or in turn.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Serial access follows storage order; use it for sequential streams, not rapid arbitrary lookup.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Trace the two product terms in the diagram. The upper AND gate receives x and NOT y, producing x·y'. The lower AND gate receives NOT x and y, producing x'·y. The final OR gate therefore produces x·y' + x'·y. This expression is 1 exactly when the inputs differ, which is the XOR function.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: OR is also 1 for x=y=1, but this circuit's two terms both become 0 there. AND is 1 only at 11. XNOR is 1 when the inputs are equal, the complement of the expression produced here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: XOR's sum-of-products form is x·y' + x'·y: one input true and the other false.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A trap is caused by the currently executing instruction or by a deliberately executed system-call instruction, so it occurs at a reproducible point in the program and is synchronous with program execution. A hardware interrupt is raised by an external event such as I/O completion or a timer, whose arrival is independent of the current instruction, so it is asynchronous with the program.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A reverses the definitions. C compares interrupts with I/O devices rather than with program execution; an I/O device may itself generate an interrupt synchronously with its own event, but that event is asynchronous to the CPU program. Hence D is unnecessary because B states the distinction correctly.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Trap=internal and synchronous; hardware interrupt=external and asynchronous to the instruction stream.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The diagram has three entity types: M, P and N, so begin with three tables. R1 links M to P and R2 links N to P; each has an arrow toward P, indicating a many-to-one key constraint, and neither relationship has its own attributes. Each relationship can therefore be represented by storing P's key as a foreign key in the corresponding M or N table. The total-participation double lines do not require separate relationship tables. Minimum table count: 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Two tables cannot represent all three entity sets. Four or five tables result from creating separate tables for R1 and/or R2, but those tables are unnecessary for attribute-free many-to-one relationships because foreign keys capture them without loss.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: An attribute-free many-to-one relationship can usually be absorbed into the many-side table as a foreign key.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Balance and Customer-name are attributes of Deposit, so that is the required relation. First select tuples satisfying Balance>10000: sigma_(Balance>10000)(Deposit). Then project Customer-name from the selected tuples: pi_(Customer-name)(sigma_(Balance>10000)(Deposit)). This is exactly the operation sequence represented by option A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: B incorrectly uses selection with Customer-name as though it were a Boolean condition; retrieving columns requires projection. C and D use Borrow, which does not contain Balance in the stated schema. D also reverses/misuses selection and projection.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Selection filters rows; projection chooses columns. Filter Deposit by balance, then project customer names.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A conceptual database model is intended to give an accurate, implementation-independent representation of the relevant universe of interest (also called the universe of discourse), while abstracting away irrelevant detail. Statement C denies that defining purpose and is therefore the false statement. The weak-relationship construct and the treatment of higher-arity relationships in the other statements refer to the relationship-analysis framework described by Dey, Storey and Barron.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A correctly attributes the weak relationship type to the 1999 Dey-Storey-Barron work. B describes the event-precedent and condition-precedent links used in that framework. D is consistent with its use of scenarios and vignettes to illustrate ternary and higher-arity relationships.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: A conceptual model should faithfully represent the relevant concepts and relationships in its universe of discourse, without committing to storage details.

sources.title: Dey, Storey and Barron - Improving Database Design Through the Analysis of Relationships
OGC Points of Interest Conceptual Model Standard - definitions

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Neither query finds the requested row as written. Q1 rejects any candidate S whenever a course-5 row e has e.marks>=S.marks. A maximum course-5 student rejects itself because equality satisfies >=; a student outside course 5 with marks above every course-5 mark could instead pass. Q2 uses >ANY, which means greater than at least one value, not greater than or equal to every value, and it also does not restrict the outer S row to course 5. A correct pattern is SELECT stuid FROM Student WHERE course=5 AND marks=(SELECT MAX(marks) FROM Student WHERE course=5).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A is wrong because Q1's >= makes even the maximum fail and its outer query is unfiltered. B is wrong because >ANY can return many nonmaximum students and the outer query is unfiltered. Since neither is correct, C is also false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: For a group maximum, compare with MAX(...) or use NOT EXISTS with a strictly greater competitor and the same group condition.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Decomposition splits a combined right side: X→YZ implies X→Y and X→Z, so a→iv. Union combines dependencies with the same determinant: X→Y and X→Z imply X→YZ, so b→iii. Composition combines two dependencies: X→Y and Z→W imply XZ→YW, so c→i. Pseudo-transitivity says X→Y and YW→Z imply XW→Z, so d→ii. The order is iv, iii, i, ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C attach at least one rule name to the wrong symbolic pattern. In particular, union and decomposition are converse-looking operations, while composition and pseudo-transitivity have different left-side constructions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Recognize FD rules by shape: union joins right sides, decomposition splits them, composition joins two dependencies, and pseudo-transitivity carries an added W.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A secondary index is commonly implemented with a B-tree/B+ tree, so a→ii. Domain relational calculus is a declarative, nonprocedural query language, so b→iv. Attribute closure is computed from a set of functional dependencies, so c→i. Natural join is a relational-algebra operation, so d→iii. The resulting order is ii, iv, i, iii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each mismatch one or more basic database concepts: functional dependency governs closure rather than the secondary index, domain calculus is the nonprocedural language, and natural join belongs to relational algebra.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Index→B-tree; declarative query→domain calculus; closure→functional dependencies; natural join→relational algebra.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Statement I is true: an ordinary trackball controls two screen coordinates, while a spaceball senses three translational and three rotational components, giving six degrees of freedom. Statement II is also true in the intended device description: the trackball's ball is physically rotated, whereas a spaceball is essentially stationary and senses applied force/torque. Statement III is false because it calls the trackball a three-dimensional positioning device. Therefore only III is not true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A incorrectly rejects both true statements I and II. B incorrectly rejects II along with III. C rejects only II, although II describes the stationary force-sensing nature of a spaceball. D isolates the sole false statement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Trackball: 2-D pointing by rolling a ball. Spaceball: fixed 3-D input controller sensing six degrees of freedom.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: For the standard 2-D transformations intended here, two translations by vectors t1 and t2 combine to translation by t1+t2, so I is true. Two rotations about the same origin/pivot by angles theta1 and theta2 combine to theta1+theta2, so II is true. Successive scale factors multiply componentwise: scaling by (sx1,sy1) and then (sx2,sy2) gives (sx1sx2,sy1sy2), so III is true. Hence all three statements hold.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each omit one valid composition rule. The rotation statement assumes the usual 2-D same-pivot setting; arbitrary 3-D rotations about different axes cannot be reduced merely by adding angles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Composite 2-D transforms: translation vectors add, same-pivot rotation angles add, and scale factors multiply.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Squash and stretch conveys weight and flexibility; slow-in/slow-out models acceleration and deceleration by changing frame spacing; staging presents an action so its meaning is unmistakable. These are classic principles for creating convincing animation, not algorithms that define rendering or image morphing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Rendering converts a scene description into pixels and may be used to produce animation frames, but these are not rendering rules. Morphing interpolates one shape or image into another. Since the three named rules specifically belong to animation, 'all the above' is too broad.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Squash-and-stretch, slow-in/slow-out and staging are animation principles that communicate motion and intent.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Put the line in signed form F(x,y)=3x+7y-2. Points are on the same side when their F values have the same sign. At the origin, F(0,0)=-2, which is negative. For (0.5,0), F=3(0.5)+7(0)-2=-0.5, also negative, so this point is on the origin's side.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: F(3,0)=7, F(1,0)=1 and F(0.5,0.5)=3; all three are positive, opposite to the origin's negative value. None lies on the line because none gives F=0.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: For ax+by+c=0, evaluate the left side: equal nonzero signs mean the same half-plane.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: In the normalized CMY model, each subtractive component is the complement of its additive RGB component: C=1-R, M=1-G and Y=1-B. Solving gives R=1-C, G=1-M and B=1-Y. In vector form, [R,G,B]^T=[1,1,1]^T-[C,M,Y]^T, which is option C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A reverses the subtraction and would give negative RGB values for ordinary CMY inputs. B subtracts unrelated constants 1,2,3. D subtracts 0.5 from each CMY component rather than taking the complement about 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Normalized RGB and CMY are componentwise complements: RGB=1-CMY.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A transformation about an arbitrary pivot begins by translating the pivot P1 to the origin (I). With the pivot fixed at the origin, scale the object to the reduced size (II) and rotate it to the desired orientation (III). Finally translate the origin/pivot to its required final location P2 (IV). Omitting either translation would apply the rotation/scale about the wrong point or leave the object in the wrong place, so all four operations are required.
