sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A never moves the transformed object from the origin to P2. B performs scale/rotation before bringing P1 to the origin, so the transformation uses the wrong pivot. C omits the required reduction/scaling. Only D contains the complete sequence.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Arbitrary-pivot composite transform: translate pivot to origin, transform, then translate to the destination.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The POSIX test utility uses the binary operator = for string equality, so the safe form is test "$a" = "$b". Quoting preserves each expanded value as one argument and handles empty strings. The command exits with status 0 when the strings are identical and 1 when they differ.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: -eq performs integer comparison, not string comparison. -equal is not a test operator. The final command uses a nonportable/incorrect construction and also starts a new shell without guaranteeing that a and b are exported into it.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Shell test: = compares strings, -eq compares integers; quote variable expansions.

sources.title: The Open Group - POSIX test utility

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Windows isolates low-level, platform- and processor-dependent operations behind the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL). In the Windows 2000 architecture this layer is supplied as HAL.DLL, allowing the kernel and device drivers to use a stable interface across different hardware configurations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: NTFS is a file system, not the processor-abstraction library. The microkernel contains core scheduling and interrupt mechanisms but is not the named DLL boundary in the question. Process Manager is an executive component concerned with processes, not processor-dependent hardware access.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: HAL hides hardware-specific details from the rest of Windows; historically its binary is HAL.DLL.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn - Windows kernel-mode HAL library

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Object Linking and Embedding (OLE) lets one Windows application place an object created or managed by another application inside its document. An audio object can therefore be embedded in a Word document and activated through its associated server/application.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The Clipboard transfers copied data but does not define a persistent linked or embedded compound-document object. Task switching merely changes the active application. 'C Win App' is not the Windows compound-document feature described.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: OLE enables compound documents containing linked or embedded objects such as audio, spreadsheets or drawings.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A TLB stores recently used virtual-page to physical-frame translations. It is implemented as associative, or content-addressable, memory: the incoming virtual page number is presented to all stored tags, and comparison hardware checks the tags in parallel. A match can therefore return the frame number without a sequential one-by-one search.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A and C describe sequential comparison, which would defeat the TLB's speed. B recognizes parallel comparison but calls the organization only cache memory; D gives the precise associative-memory mechanism and the simultaneous tag comparison requested by the definitions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: A TLB is a small associative translation cache whose tags are searched in parallel.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The most direct way to break a deadlock cycle is to terminate one participating process. Its resources are released, allowing other blocked processes to proceed. A real recovery policy must choose a victim carefully and may need to terminate additional processes if one termination does not remove every cycle, but the mechanism itself is simple.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Rollback needs checkpoints and restoration logic. Resource preemption must identify a safe preemptible resource and handle rollback/starvation. Locking another process cannot release the resources forming the deadlock and can make blocking worse.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Deadlock recovery can break the wait-for cycle by terminating a victim process and releasing its resources.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.answerText: Intended answer: B, tree-structured directory. 'Hierarchical directory' in A is also a descriptive synonym, so the wording is not perfectly discriminating.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The traditional UNIX namespace starts at one root directory, '/', and every ordinary file or directory has a path descending through parent directories. In operating-system directory classifications this is called a tree-structured directory. The term hierarchical also describes that same parent-child organization, which is why option A sounds true; B is the formal category the question is asking for.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A is not conceptually false, but it is a general description rather than the conventional classification intended by the options. A directed-acyclic-graph directory permits shared files/subdirectories with multiple parents, and a general graph may contain cycles; neither is the basic tree model named here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Tree structure means one root and one parent path for each node; links can make the full UNIX namespace more graph-like in practice.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: In traditional UNIX boot terminology, process 0 is a special kernel-created process, not a child made by fork. It creates process 1 and then serves as the swapper/scheduler idle process. Process 1, not process 0, is the init process (or the modern init-system equivalent). Therefore statement A is the statement that is not true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: B is true because process 0 exists as part of kernel initialization rather than being produced by fork. C expresses its traditional post-boot role as swapper after process 1 is created. D correctly describes it as a special boot-created process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Traditional UNIX: PID 0=swapper/scheduler; PID 1=init; ordinary processes descend through fork.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The shell special parameter $! expands to the process ID of the most recent background pipeline. After starting sleep 1 in the background with &, echo $! prints that sleep job's PID. Thus the intended command is sleep 1 & echo $!.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: $? is the exit status of the most recent foreground pipeline, not a PID. $# is the number of positional parameters. $* expands the positional parameters. None identifies the latest background process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Shell special parameters: $!=last background PID, $$=shell PID, $?=last status, $#=argument count, $*=all arguments.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.answerText: Intended answer: B, the most complete listed set: ready, standby, running, waiting, transition and terminated. As written with 'include,' the question is not uniquely worded because the shorter options also list genuine states.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The Windows dispatcher distinguishes Ready (able to run), Standby (selected to run next), Running, Waiting (blocked for an event/resource), Transition (ready except that a needed resource such as the kernel stack is not resident), and Terminated. Option B is the only offered list containing all six of these named states. Microsoft's enumeration also includes Initialized, so B should be read as the intended most complete choice among the options, not a mathematically exhaustive list.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A omits standby, transition and terminated; C omits standby; D omits ready and waiting. However, because the stem says states 'include' rather than asking for the complete list, each shorter option contains only valid state names, creating a wording defect.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-dec-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Windows adds dispatcher states such as Standby and Transition beyond the basic ready-running-waiting model.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn - System.Diagnostics ThreadState

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: SMI=1-(Fa+Fc+Fd)/Mf. The numerator subtracted from 1 counts the relative amount of change in the current release: added, changed and deleted modules. As a product stabilizes, fewer modules require such changes, so (Fa+Fc+Fd)/Mf tends toward 0 and SMI tends toward 1. A value near 1 therefore indicates maturity/stability, while a lower value indicates substantial churn.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: SMI near 0 means the counted changes are roughly as large as the release's module count, which signals instability rather than stabilization. A negative value would indicate even heavier change under the formula. Hence B and C reverse the interpretation, and D is unnecessary.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Software Maturity Index is one minus the fraction of modules added, changed or deleted; stability pushes it toward 1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Walston-Felix is an empirical software cost/effort-estimation model, so a→ii. The Quick-Fix model describes an ad hoc maintenance approach, so b→iv. Putnam's resource-allocation model uses the Rayleigh staffing curve and software equation for project planning, so c→iii. The Musa-Okumoto logarithmic-Poisson reliability model describes changing failure intensity during testing, so d→i. The sequence is ii, iv, iii, i.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A and C incorrectly associate Quick-Fix with failure intensity, while B moves Walston-Felix away from cost estimation. Checking the distinctive endpoints-cost estimation for Walston-Felix and failure intensity for logarithmic-Poisson-leaves only D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Model cues: Walston-Felix=cost, Quick-Fix=maintenance, Putnam=resource/project planning, logarithmic-Poisson=reliability intensity.

sources.title: FIU study of software cost-estimation models
National Academies - Reliability growth and logarithmic-Poisson model

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Cleanroom software engineering aims to prevent defects rather than rely on finding and repairing them after execution. It combines formal specification/design, correctness verification and statistically based usage testing. Removing defects before they can trigger serious failures or hazards is therefore the Cleanroom philosophy described by the stem.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Incremental and Agile models organize development and delivery but do not inherently guarantee defect prevention. The spiral model is risk-driven and repeatedly evaluates risks, yet the specific defect-prevention process named in the question is Cleanroom.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Cleanroom emphasizes correctness by construction and statistical quality certification-defect prevention before failure.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Equivalence partitioning derives tests from the externally visible input domain, without requiring knowledge of the program's internal code. Inputs expected to behave similarly are grouped into valid and invalid equivalence classes, and one or a few representatives are tested from each class. That makes it a black-box test-design method.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: White-box testing derives cases from control flow or code structure. Orthogonal-array testing systematically samples combinations of factors and is a separate technique. Stress testing pushes a system beyond normal resource or workload limits rather than partitioning its input domain.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Equivalence partitioning reduces black-box tests by choosing representatives from behaviorally equivalent input classes.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The three rules are a compact framework for user-interface design: give users meaningful control and escape routes, minimize what they must remember between screens, and make commands/layout/feedback consistent. Together they guide the construction of a usable, predictable interface.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: User satisfaction is an outcome of good design rather than the name of the rules themselves. They do not primarily conserve CPU, memory or storage resources. Because B states their design purpose directly, 'none of these' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Interface golden rules: user control, low memory burden and consistency.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Software safety is treated as a software quality-assurance activity because it identifies hazards, estimates their severity/probability and imposes requirements or controls that prevent software behavior from causing system-level harm. Safety analysis is therefore part of assuring the product's quality and dependability throughout development.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Risk mitigation, monitoring and management is a broader project-risk framework; safety can feed that framework but the quoted definition classifies it as SQA. Cost estimation predicts effort/budget. Defect-removal efficiency is a metric comparing defects removed before release with total defects, not hazard analysis.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Software safety extends SQA from ordinary defects to software-induced hazards and system failure.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.answerText: No listed option is always true. Projection produces no more columns and no more rows than the original relation; duplicate elimination can reduce the row count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Relational-algebra projection pi_A1,...,Ak(R) retains only the named attributes, so its number of columns is k≤the original degree. Because relations are sets, duplicate projected tuples are eliminated; its number of rows is therefore at most the original cardinality and may be smaller. Example: projecting Department from two employees in the same department produces one department tuple, not two. Thus neither 'same rows' nor 'same columns' is guaranteed unless extra conditions are supplied.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A is impossible because projection selects existing attributes rather than adding columns. B is impossible because deleting attributes and duplicates cannot create more tuples. C fails when two original rows become the same projected tuple. D fails whenever a proper subset of columns is projected.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Projection can reduce both degree (columns) and cardinality (rows after duplicate removal); it never increases either.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: WHERE sex='M' first restricts rows to male employees. GROUP BY deptname then forms one male-employee group per department. HAVING compares each group's AVG(salary) with the scalar subquery's AVG(salary), which is computed over every employee in the organization. The query outputs department names whose male employees' average salary exceeds the overall organizational average.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A omits both the male filter and department grouping. B reverses the > condition. C changes > to equality. D alone includes all three essential details: male employees, per-department average and comparison with the organization-wide average.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: SQL evaluation cue: WHERE filters rows, GROUP BY forms groups, HAVING filters groups, and the uncorrelated subquery supplies one global average.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Atomic attribute values establish 1NF. From a→c and b→d, the pair {a,b} determines all attributes and is the candidate key under the stated dependencies. But c depends on only a and d depends on only b-each is a nonprime attribute depending on a proper subset of the composite key. These partial dependencies violate 2NF. Hence the relation is in 1NF but not 2NF.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B and C require 2NF, which the partial dependencies violate. D merely says 1NF and is less precise than A because the information is sufficient to identify the 2NF violation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: 2NF forbids a nonprime attribute from depending on only part of a composite candidate key.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.answerText: No listed option is correct. The standard mapping is RAID 0→ii, RAID 1→iii, RAID 2→iv, RAID 3→i.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: RAID 0 stripes data without redundancy, so a→ii. RAID 1 mirrors complete data copies, so b→iii. RAID 2 uses bit-level striping with Hamming-style error-correcting codes, so c→iv. RAID 3 uses bit/byte-interleaved data with a dedicated parity disk, matching bit-interleaved parity, so d→i. The required sequence ii, iii, iv, i does not appear among A-D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A incorrectly assigns error-correcting codes to RAID 0. B, C and D all call RAID 0 mirrored disks, which is RAID 1. Although D correctly maps RAID 2 and RAID 3, it swaps the first two levels.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: RAID mnemonic: 0=stripe, 1=mirror, 2=ECC, 3=dedicated interleaved parity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The golden ratio is phi=(1+sqrt(5))/2 and its algebraic conjugate is (1-sqrt(5))/2=1-phi. The quadratic formula shows that these are precisely the two roots of x^2-x-1=0. Equivalently, phi^2=phi+1, which rearranges to that polynomial.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: The two cubic equations have different roots and are not the minimal polynomial of phi. x^2+x-1=0 has roots (-1±sqrt(5))/2, so it includes phi-1 rather than phi itself.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Golden-ratio identity phi^2=phi+1 immediately gives phi^2-phi-1=0.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The unusual square-root subproblem becomes ordinary after a logarithmic substitution. Let m=log n and define S(m)=T(e^m). Since sqrt(n)=e^(m/2), the recurrence becomes S(m)=2S(m/2)+m. The Master theorem has a=2, b=2 and f(m)=m=Theta(m^(log_2 2)), so S(m)=Theta(m log m). Substituting m=log n gives T(n)=Theta(log n·log log n).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A and B incorrectly retain a factor n even though repeatedly taking square roots makes the recursion depth only log log n. C counts roughly the depth but ignores the work across levels. D contains both the per-level/logarithmic work and the log-log number of balanced levels.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: For recurrences on sqrt(n), set m=log n; square root becomes halving and Master-theorem analysis applies.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: An n-element binary heap is an almost-complete binary tree. A node of height h roots a subtree that, except near the final incomplete level, accounts for about 2^(h+1) positions. Counting the possible roots yields the standard bound: the number of nodes of height h is at most ceil(n/2^(h+1)). For h=0 this says the number of leaves is at most ceil(n/2), which matches heap structure.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A's denominator 2^h is too small and gives a weaker, nonstandard level bound; at h=0 it merely says at most n. B and C claim lower bounds ('greater than') that fail for many heaps and heights. D states the standard upper bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Heap height-count lemma: at most ceil(n/2^(h+1)) nodes have height h.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Build the Huffman tree by repeatedly merging the two smallest frequencies (in thousands): 5+9=14, 12+13=25, 14+16=30, 25+30=55, and 45+55=100. The resulting code lengths are 1 for frequency 45; 3 for 12, 13 and 16; and 4 for 5 and 9. Total bits, in thousands, are 45·1+12·3+13·3+16·3+9·4+5·4=224. Thus the file requires 224,000 bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: The other totals do not equal the weighted external path length of the optimal Huffman tree. A useful arithmetic check is that every merge adds its combined frequency to total cost: 14+25+30+55+100=224 thousand bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Huffman cost can be computed by summing every merge weight; here that sum is 224 thousand.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A vertex cover V1 must touch every edge. Formally, for each edge (u,v) in E, at least one endpoint belongs to V1: u∈V1 or v∈V1. The inclusive 'or' allows both endpoints to be selected but requires at least one.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A defines a clique-like property among selected vertices and says nothing about covering every graph edge. B unnecessarily requires both endpoints of every edge, which would usually force nearly all nonisolated vertices into the cover. D describes an independent-set-like condition, not edge coverage.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Vertex cover: every edge has at least one selected endpoint; independent set is its complementary viewpoint.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: In a full mesh, every unordered pair of distinct devices needs one direct physical channel. Device 1 can be paired with n-1 others, device 2 with n-2 new others, and so on, giving (n-1)+(n-2)+...+1=n(n-1)/2 links. Division by two avoids counting the same link once from each endpoint.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: n(n+1)/2 includes n extra self-pairs that are not links between different devices. 2n and 2n+1 grow only linearly, but a full mesh needs a link for every pair and therefore grows quadratically.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Full-mesh links equal the number of 2-device combinations: C(n,2)=n(n-1)/2.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Baud counts signal units (symbols) per second, while bit rate counts bits per second. If each of 600 symbols per second represents 6 bits, bit rate=600×6=3600 bits/s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: 100 results from dividing rather than multiplying. 6/600 has the wrong operation and units. Since 3600 is listed, 'none of the above' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Bit rate = baud rate × bits carried by each signal unit.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The data-link layer provides node-to-node delivery over one link, so a→ii. The network layer chooses routes across networks, so b→iv. The transport layer provides end-to-end flow control, so c→i. Application-layer protocols provide user services such as electronic mail, so d→iii. The sequence is ii, iv, i, iii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A assigns routing to transport, C assigns mail services to transport/routing to application, and D assigns flow control to application. Only B preserves all four standard layer responsibilities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Link=node-to-node; network=routing; transport=end-to-end flow control; application=mail and user services.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The image contains 1024×800 pixels. At 3 bytes per pixel and 8 bits per byte, its size is 1024×800×3×8=19,660,800 bits. Using the nominal Ethernet rate 10 Mbps=10,000,000 bits/s, transmission time=19,660,800/10,000,000=1.96608 seconds, approximately 1.966 seconds.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: 19.66 and 196.6 seconds introduce one or two extra factors of 10. 0.1966 seconds omits a factor of 10. The calculation deliberately ignores headers, contention and interframe overhead as the question requests the raw uncompressed transfer time.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Transmission time = width × height × bytes/pixel × 8 / link bit rate.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A decibel expresses a logarithmic ratio between two power levels: dB=10 log10(P2/P1). For voltage or current across the same impedance the corresponding form is 20 log10(V2/V1). It therefore measures the relative strength of two signals or the same signal at two locations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Frequency measures cycles per second. Attenuation is the loss itself and may be reported in decibels, but it is not the unit/measure named by the stem. Throughput is the achieved data-transfer rate.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Decibel is a logarithmic ratio, not an absolute signal-strength unit.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Thick coaxial cable was used by 10BASE5 Ethernet as a shared bus. Multiple stations attached to the same cable segment through transceiver taps, making the medium multidrop. Every attached station observed transmissions on the common coaxial segment.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Twisted-pair Ethernet links are normally point-to-point between a station and a hub/switch, whether shielded or unshielded. Fiber links are likewise ordinarily point-to-point. Thick coax is the listed medium historically designed for taps along one shared bus.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Multidrop means several devices share and tap one physical medium; classic thick-coax Ethernet is the model example.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Standard 10-Mbps Ethernet uses Manchester coding. Each data bit is represented by two half-bit signal intervals with a mandatory mid-bit transition, so the signal-element rate is twice the data rate: 2×10 Mbit/s=20 Mbaud.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: 10 Mbaud would apply to one signal element per bit, not Manchester's two. 30 and 40 Mbaud imply three or four signal elements per bit and do not match standard 10-Mbps Ethernet coding.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Manchester encoding needs two signal elements per bit, so baud rate is twice bit rate.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: In an optimal simplex tableau, a nonbasic variable normally has a strictly non-improving reduced cost. If its reduced cost Zj-Cj is exactly zero, that variable can enter the basis without changing the objective value. A pivot may therefore produce a different basic feasible solution with the same optimum-an alternative optimal solution.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Degeneracy concerns a basic variable equal to zero, not a zero reduced cost for a nonbasic variable. Unboundedness requires an improving entering column with no valid leaving row. Optimality of the current solution is established by the sign condition on all reduced costs; the extra zero specifically signals an alternative optimum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: At optimum, zero reduced cost for a nonbasic variable means another basis can achieve the same objective value.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A nondegenerate basic feasible solution of an m-by-n transportation problem has exactly m+n-1 occupied independent cells. If fewer than m+n-1 allocations are positive, one or more basic variables have value zero; the solution is called degenerate.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Nondegenerate requires the full m+n-1 positive basic allocations. Unbounded describes an objective that can improve without limit. Unbalanced means total supply differs from total demand and is fixed by a dummy row/column; it is unrelated to the number of positive allocations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Transportation degeneracy test: positive allocations < m+n-1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Total supply is 8+12+14=34 while total demand is 30, so first add a dummy destination of demand 4 and zero costs. Vogel's penalties first allocate F3→Dummy=4. A consistent continuation allocates F2→W5=8, F2→W4=4, F1→W4=4, F1→W2=4, F3→W1=4 and F3→W3=6. The real transportation cost is 8(1)+4(2)+4(2)+4(2)+4(6)+6(4)=8+8+8+8+24+24=80; the dummy allocation costs zero.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: 76, 90 and 96 do not equal the cost of the VAM allocation after balancing the problem. Omitting the dummy destination leaves supply and demand unequal and prevents a valid basic feasible solution.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Before VAM, balance supply and demand with a zero-cost dummy; then repeatedly use the largest row/column penalty.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: In this actor-based animation model, an actor is a behavior program associated with an object. The animation system invokes it once for each generated frame so it can update or determine that object's current position, appearance or other characteristics before the frame is rendered.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Twice per frame has no general role, and 30 or 60 are frame rates measured per second, not the number of times one actor runs within each frame. The per-frame update count is one.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Frame rate says how many frames occur per second; an actor update runs once within each frame.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Bresenham's line algorithm maintains an integer decision/error variable. At each pixel step it updates that variable using additions and subtractions to choose between the two candidate pixels. It avoids slopes, multiplication by fractions and floating-point rounding, which made it fast and predictable on raster hardware.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Real arithmetic and floating-point arithmetic are precisely what Bresenham avoids. It does not require a mixture of real and integer calculations because the recurrence can be expressed entirely with integers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Bresenham rasterizes lines using an integer error accumulator and incremental updates.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Critical fusion frequency (also called critical flicker-fusion frequency) is the threshold at which a periodically refreshed light or picture is perceived as continuous and steady rather than flickering. Displays must refresh above an appropriate threshold for comfortable viewing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Crucial fusion frequency is a plausible-sounding but nonstandard phrase. Current frequency fusion and critically diffused frequency are not the recognized visual-perception terms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Critical flicker fusion is the perceptual transition from separate flashes to a steady image.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A finite 2-D Cartesian point (X,Y) is represented homogeneously as (x,y,z) with X=x/z and Y=y/z for z≠0. When z=0, those divisions do not produce finite Cartesian coordinates; the homogeneous triple represents a direction, or a point at infinity, in the projective plane.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Cartesian points require a nonzero scale coordinate, commonly normalized to z=1. 'Parallel point' is not the standard projective term. The origin is represented by (0,0,z) with z≠0, such as (0,0,1), not by every point with z=0.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Homogeneous last coordinate nonzero=finite point; zero=point/direction at infinity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: One resolvable line pair consists of one black line plus one adjacent white line. Forty black lines interleaved with forty white lines therefore form forty black-white pairs across one inch, giving a spatial resolution of 40 line-pairs per inch.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: 80 counts individual lines but labels them incorrectly as 80 pairs. 1600 multiplies unrelated counts. 40 lines per inch counts only one color's lines and uses the wrong unit for the stated black-white resolving pattern.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Line-pair resolution counts black-white cycles, not individual lines.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The image has 1024×1024 = 2²⁰ pixels. At n bits per pixel, it needs n×2²⁰ bits. An 8-bit machine stores eight bits per byte, so the size is (n×2²⁰)/8 bytes = n/8 megabytes when the question uses 2²⁰ bytes per MB. For example, an 8-bit image occupies 1 MB and a 24-bit image occupies 3 MB.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A multiplies by eight where conversion from bits to bytes requires division by eight. C omits the n-bit color depth. D gives a fixed size independent of the number of bits per pixel.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Image storage = width × height × bits per pixel ÷ 8 bytes.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: C supports multidimensional arrays, conceptually as arrays whose elements are themselves arrays. An array may therefore be declared with one, two, three or more subscripts, subject to implementation limits. Its elements occupy contiguous storage, and C lays multidimensional arrays out in row-major order, so the rightmost subscript changes fastest.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Two and three subscripts are valid examples but are not exclusive limits. 'Only one subscript' ignores C declarations such as int a[3][4] and higher-dimensional arrays.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: A C array can have n dimensions; multidimensional arrays are contiguous arrays of arrays in row-major order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: All four questions affect the meaning of an arithmetic expression. Precedence decides which operator binds first; associativity resolves operators of equal precedence; operand-evaluation order decides which operand is computed first; and side-effect restrictions determine whether that order can change program state. A language specification must define or deliberately leave constrained behavior for every one of these issues.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A omits side effects, B omits associativity, and C omits operand-evaluation order. Each omitted issue can change an expression's result or portability, so none of those partial lists is complete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Expression semantics depend on precedence, associativity, evaluation order and the treatment of side effects.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A Horn clause contains at most one positive literal. In logic-programming notation it is written with one head atom on the left of the rule arrow and a conjunction of body atoms on the right; a goal clause may have no head at all. Thus the left side is either a single atomic proposition or empty, matching D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A excludes valid goal clauses with an empty head. B permits several head atoms, which would create several positive literals and cease to be Horn. C unnecessarily restricts the body to a single atom, whereas a Horn rule may have a conjunction of many body atoms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Horn means at most one positive literal: one rule head or an empty head for a goal clause.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Parameter passing can be classified by the direction in which information crosses the call boundary. In mode carries an initial value from caller to callee; out mode returns a value from callee to caller; and in-out mode does both. These are all fundamental semantic models, regardless of whether a language implements them by copying, references or another mechanism.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each name a genuine model but omit the other two. Because the question asks which models apply, the complete choice is 'all of the above.'
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Parameter modes describe information flow: in, out, or both directions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Treat a as an opening bracket and b as a closing bracket. The rule aSb wraps a balanced string in one matching pair, SS concatenates two balanced strings, and λ supplies the empty balanced string. Consequently every generated word has equally many a's and b's, and no prefix can contain more b's than a's: for every prefix v, n_a(v)≥n_b(v). This is the one-kind Dyck language described by A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B has the prefix inequality reversed; the word ab generated by aSb already violates B at its first symbol. C and D require unequal total counts, but every production either adds one a and one b or combines already balanced strings.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: S→aSb | SS | λ generates balanced-parenthesis strings with nonnegative prefix balance and final balance zero.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A deterministic PDA must have at most one possible move for a fixed state, input choice and stack top, which is statement s1. It must also avoid a choice between consuming input and taking an ε-move: if δ(q,ε,b) exists, every δ(q,c,b) for c in the input alphabet must be empty. That is statement s2. Both restrictions are needed to make the next move unique.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Using only s1 can still leave an ε-move and an input-consuming move simultaneously enabled. Using only s2 does not prevent multiple outcomes for the same transition argument. Therefore neither single statement alone is sufficient, and 'neither' is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: DPDA determinism requires a unique transition and no ε/input transition conflict for the same state and stack top.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A general context-free grammar can be converted to an equivalent Chomsky-normal-form grammar and parsed by the CYK dynamic-programming algorithm. For an input of length n=|w|, CYK considers O(n²) substrings and up to O(n) split positions per substring, giving O(n³) time for a fixed grammar. Hence the stated general parsing bound is proportional to |w|³.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Logarithmic and linear bounds are not available for arbitrary context-free grammars. A quadratic table has O(n²) cells, but filling each cell may examine O(n) splits, producing the cubic rather than quadratic bound.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: General CFG membership via CYK uses substring × substring × split-point dynamic programming: O(n³).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A regular grammar is recognized by a deterministic finite automaton, so b→i. A context-free grammar is recognized by a pushdown automaton, so c→iv. An unrestricted grammar generates a recursively enumerable language, so d→ii. Context-sensitive languages are decidable and therefore recursive, giving a→iii among the supplied choices. The sequence is iii, i, iv, ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A and D associate context-sensitive languages with recursively enumerable and unrestricted grammars with recursive, losing the intended Chomsky-hierarchy pairing. B incorrectly connects a regular grammar to a PDA and a context-free grammar to a DFA.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Regular↔finite automaton, context-free↔PDA, unrestricted↔recursively enumerable; context-sensitive languages are recursive.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Under the standard noncontracting conventions used for context-sensitive languages, they are closed under intersection, concatenation, substitution and inverse homomorphism, so s1 is true. Context-free languages are closed under substitution and homomorphism, but not under complementation. If CFLs were closed under complement, De Morgan's law together with closure under union would also make them closed under intersection, which they are not. Therefore s2 is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A incorrectly accepts the CFL complementation claim. C and D reject s1 even though the listed context-sensitive closure properties hold under the standard convention. Only B records s1 true and s2 false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: CFLs survive homomorphism and substitution, but complement is the decisive nonclosure in s2.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.answerText: Intended answer: D, Immediate operand. The standard mode name is immediate addressing, so the question relies on terminology and is not ideally worded.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Register indirect, autoincrement and relative indexed are all conventional names of effective-address calculation modes. 'Immediate operand' describes where the operand value appears—inside the instruction—rather than giving the conventional mode name, which is 'immediate addressing.' On that literal naming distinction, D is the intended choice. Be aware that architecture texts routinely do classify immediate addressing as an addressing mode, making the wording ambiguous if 'immediate operand' is treated as shorthand for it.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A obtains the effective address from a register's contents. B uses a register indirectly and then increments it. C combines PC-relative and indexed displacement ideas. All three are recognizably named addressing modes, whereas D is phrased as an operand type rather than a mode name.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Distinguish the immediate operand (value embedded in the instruction) from the conventional term immediate addressing mode.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Instruction formats are often classified by how many explicit operand addresses they contain. A stack machine uses zero-address instructions because operands are implicit on the stack; an accumulator machine often uses one address; many register machines use two addresses; and three-address instructions explicitly name two sources and one destination. Computer designs can therefore use all four formats.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A, B and C exclude at least one well-established format. In particular, any answer omitting zero-address stack instructions is incomplete, and three-address code is also a standard organization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Address count ranges from zero for implicit stack operands to three for two explicit sources plus a destination.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: BR (branch) and JMP (jump) directly change the program counter. TST examines a value and sets condition flags used to control a following conditional transfer, so it is commonly grouped with control-support instructions in this classification. SHL shifts operand bits left and is a logical/data-manipulation operation; it does not itself direct instruction sequencing. Therefore SHL is the non-control instruction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: BR and JMP are canonical transfer-of-control instructions. TST supports conditional control by establishing status flags. SHL instead transforms data, even though its flags may later be tested by a separate branch.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Control instructions alter or decide sequencing; SHL primarily transforms an operand's bit pattern.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: An illegal opcode, invalid operand use, divide-by-zero or similar execution fault is detected inside the CPU while it executes the current instruction. It therefore raises an internal interrupt, more commonly called an exception or trap. The saved machine state lets an operating-system handler diagnose, terminate or sometimes recover from the fault.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A software interrupt is deliberately requested by executing a designated trap/system-call instruction rather than arising from erroneous use. An external interrupt comes asynchronously from hardware such as a timer or I/O device. Since those causes are distinct, 'all of the above' is incorrect.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Execution-detected errors are synchronous internal interrupts (exceptions), not asynchronous external interrupts.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: For product-of-sums minimization, group the zero cells of the four-variable Karnaugh map. The zeros are minterms 3,4,6,7,11,12,13,14,15. A group with W=X=1 gives (W′+X′); a group with Y=Z=1 gives (Y′+Z′); and a group with X=1,Z=0 gives (X′+Z). Multiplying these maxterms yields (W′+X′)(Y′+Z′)(X′+Z), option A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B complements Z in the X=1,Z=0 group, which would make required minterms fail. C uses (Y′+Z), corresponding to Y=1,Z=0 rather than the actual Y=Z=1 zero group. D similarly uses (Y+Z′), describing the wrong zero pattern.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: For POS, group K-map zeros; a variable fixed at 1 appears complemented in the maxterm, while one fixed at 0 appears uncomplemented.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: TTL developed from diode-transistor logic, so a→iii. ECL avoids transistor saturation and is known for very high switching speed, so b→iv. MOS technology permits high component density, so c→i. CMOS draws very little static power because ideally only one transistor network conducts in a stable state, so d→ii. The resulting order is iii, iv, i, ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A assigns low power to ECL even though ECL is power-hungry. B assigns high density to TTL and the DTL lineage to MOS. D assigns low power to ECL and high speed to CMOS, missing the distinguishing textbook properties.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Logic-family cues: TTL←DTL, ECL→speed, MOS→density, CMOS→low static power.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.answerText: Intended answer: B (ii, i, iv, iii), but the printed pairing 'Private key → Domain constraint' is conceptually defective.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Three matches are direct: a foreign key enforces referential integrity (a→ii); an event-condition-action model is implemented by a trigger (c→iv); and encryption is a principal database-security mechanism (d→iii). These force sequence ii, i, iv, iii, which is option B. The remaining forced match is 'private key→domain constraint,' but a cryptographic private key belongs with encryption, not a database domain constraint. The source likely contains a wording error, so B is only the intended elimination-based answer, not a fully valid four-pair mapping.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A and C map foreign keys to encryption instead of referential integrity. D maps foreign keys to domain constraints and ECA to encryption. B alone contains the three defensible matches, although its leftover private-key pairing exposes the question defect.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Use certain pairs first in a matching question, but flag the item when elimination forces a conceptually false leftover pair.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: In a C function parameter, an array declaration is adjusted to a pointer to its first element. The function therefore accesses the caller's actual array storage through that pointer. Unless the parameter points to const-qualified elements, an assignment such as a[0]=value changes the original array element.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B would be true only if a separate copy were made or mutation were forbidden with const; ordinary array passing does neither. Passing an array is valid C and does not itself cause a compile-time or run-time error, so C and D are false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: C passes array access through a pointer to the original elements, not by copying the whole array.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Once the node containing 'Vivek' is located, a doubly linked list gives direct access to its predecessor through the prev link. The node before Vivek can then be unlinked in constant time by updating the surrounding next and prev links. This makes predecessor deletion natural and efficient.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A circular link only connects the end back to the beginning; it does not by itself give a backward link. A singly linked list cannot move directly from Vivek to its predecessor unless that predecessor was separately retained during traversal. A deque gives efficient deletion only at its two ends, not immediately before an arbitrary interior name.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Doubly linked lists support direct predecessor access and O(1) deletion once the target position is known.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: In printf, the precision on a %s conversion limits the maximum number of characters printed. In %.*s, the asterisk says to read that precision from the next int argument. Here i is 7, so printf outputs the first seven characters of 'hello world': h e l l o, a space, and w—namely 'hello w'. The following s argument supplies the string.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: There is no error in the %.*s format itself. 'hello' would require a precision of 5. 'o world' would require starting at s+4 rather than limiting the length from the beginning. The source uses an old-style main declaration; modern C should write int main(void), but the MCQ is testing dynamic string precision.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: %.*s means print at most the next integer's number of characters from the supplied string.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The fragment cannot be parsed as valid C. In the declaration int *b, &a;, the token & cannot introduce a C variable declarator. The printed statements also omit required semicolons after *b=20 and the printf call. These are syntax violations detected during compilation, before any execution occurs.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A ignores several invalid tokens. A logical error describes a compilable program that computes the wrong result. A semantic/runtime issue such as dereferencing an uninitialized pointer would matter only after the syntax errors were repaired; the source as printed fails earlier.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Classify the earliest failure: invalid declaration grammar and missing semicolons make this a syntax error.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: calloc allocates storage for an array of elements and initializes its bytes to zero, so a→iii. free releases previously allocated storage, b→i. malloc allocates a requested number of bytes, c→iv. realloc changes the size of a previously allocated block and may move it, d→ii. The sequence is iii, i, iv, ii.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B assigns 'modifies allocated space' to free and 'frees' to malloc. C swaps the roles of free, malloc and realloc. D describes calloc as a plain requested-size allocation and free as a resizing operation. Only A preserves all four standard library roles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: calloc=array allocation plus zeroing; malloc=raw allocation; realloc=resize; free=release.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A binary symmetric channel is an abstract discrete memoryless channel with binary input and output. Each transmitted bit is independently flipped with crossover probability p, with the same probability for 0→1 and 1→0. It is a probabilistic channel model, not a half-duplex protocol, full-duplex protocol or bit-oriented data-link protocol. Therefore none of A–C applies.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Half duplex and full duplex describe allowed transmission directions. Bit-oriented describes how a data-link protocol frames and controls bit sequences. None of these defines the binary symmetric channel's probabilistic error behavior.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: BSC means symmetric independent bit-flip probabilities, not a duplex or framing protocol.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Hamming distance is the number of positions at which equal-length binary strings differ. Comparing 100101000110 with 110111101101 gives mismatches at positions 2, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 12. There are six mismatches, so the distance is 6.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: The counts 3, 4 and 5 stop before all mismatching positions have been included. A position-by-position XOR gives 010010101011, whose six 1s confirm the result.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Hamming distance = population count of the XOR of the two codewords.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The data word 1110001010 contains five 1s, which is odd. For even parity, append a parity bit 1 so that the transmitted word contains six 1s. Appending that bit gives 11100010101.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B appends 0, leaving the count of 1s odd. C adds no parity bit. D is shorter than the original data word and therefore cannot be the encoded word.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Even parity chooses the added bit so the total number of 1s, including the parity bit, is even.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: An M×N image contains MN pixel positions. In a binary image each position has two independent choices, 0 or 1. By the multiplication principle, the number of possible assignments is 2×2×…×2 over MN positions, which equals 2^(MN).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: M+N and M×N count dimensions or positions rather than assignments of values to all positions. 2^(M+N) treats row and column counts as independent binary choices; the exponent must be the number of pixels, MN.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: For k possible values at each of P independent pixels, the number of images is k^P; here k=2 and P=MN.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A digital image is obtained by sampling spatial coordinates and quantizing amplitude. Thus x and y take values on a finite discrete pixel grid, while f(x,y) takes one of a finite number of intensity or color levels. Both the coordinate set and amplitude set are finite for a stored digital image.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Infinite or continuous coordinates/amplitudes describe an ideal analog image model. A digital array has a bounded number of samples and a bounded bit depth, so neither B nor C applies, and D is unnecessary.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Digital imaging discretizes both space (sampling) and value (quantization).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: With quantum 4, the round-robin timeline is A:0–4, B:4–8, C:8–12, D:12–16, A:16–20, C:20–24, D:24–25, C:25–26. Completion times are A=20, B=8, C=26 and D=25. Subtracting arrivals gives turnaround times 20, 7, 24 and 22 ms. Their average is (20+7+24+22)/4 = 73/4 = 18.25 ms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: The other numerical choices result from ignoring arrival times, using completion time directly for every process, or reordering the ready queue incorrectly after a quantum. Round robin appends an unfinished process behind all processes already waiting.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Turnaround time = completion time − arrival time; first build the exact round-robin timeline.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Trace ABACABDBACD with two empty frames. FIFO faults on A,B,C,A,B,D,A,C,D: 9 interrupts; hits occur only on the third A and the second B. LRU retains the most recently used page and faults on A,B,C,B,D,A,C,D: 8 interrupts; it also hits on the fifth A. Hence the FIFO/LRU pair is 9 and 8.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A undercounts LRU by one, B undercounts both algorithms, and D undercounts FIFO and LRU. Initial loads count as page interrupts because each brings a new page into memory, exactly as the question states.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: FIFO evicts the oldest loaded page; LRU evicts the page whose most recent reference is oldest—trace them separately.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: P1 acquires S before requesting Q, while P2 acquires Q before requesting S. An interleaving can let P1 hold S and P2 hold Q; P1 then waits for Q and P2 waits for S. Neither can reach its signal operations, creating a circular wait with no progress—a deadlock.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Starvation means a process is repeatedly denied service while others continue; here both can become permanently blocked. A race condition is an unsynchronized outcome dependency, but the semaphores do synchronize access—the problem is their opposite acquisition order. Multithreading is an execution model, not the failure shown.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Acquire multiple locks in one global order; opposite lock orders can create circular wait and deadlock.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: There are 10 devices. In state 1, allocations are (2,3,4), so only 1 device is available; remaining needs are (2,2,4). No process can satisfy its remaining need with 1, so no safe sequence starts and state 1 is unsafe. In state 2, allocations are (0,2,4), leaving 4 available; needs are (4,3,4). P2 can finish first and release its 2 allocated devices, raising the work count to 6, after which P1 and P3 can finish. Thus state 2 is safe.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: B reverses the two classifications. C overlooks that state 1 has no process with need≤available. D overlooks the safe sequence in state 2, such as P2→P1→P3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: A Banker state is safe only if repeated need≤work tests can produce a completion order for every process.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A user-to-kernel mode switch normally continues executing on behalf of the same process; it changes privilege level and transfers control to kernel code but does not replace the whole running process. A switch between user processes must save one process's CPU context, choose another runnable process, restore its context and often disturb caches or address-translation state. Therefore the usual cost relation is T1<T2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: T1>T2 contradicts the extra scheduling and context-management work in a process switch. Equality is not generally expected because the operations differ. Although exact timings depend on hardware and OS design, the standard architectural comparison is sufficiently clear to reject 'nothing can be said.'
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: A mode switch changes privilege within one execution context; a process switch changes the execution context itself.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The working set W(t,Δ) is the set of pages referenced during a recent window of execution. Programs tend to revisit a relatively small current locality, so keeping that working set resident implements the principle of locality. If the sum of active working sets exceeds physical memory, the OS can suspend processes instead of allowing continuous page replacement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Swapping and segmentation are different memory-management mechanisms. Thrashing is a harmful condition the working-set model helps prevent; it is not the underlying concept the model implements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: The recent-reference window estimates a process's current locality and its needed resident pages.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.answerText: C, approximately 16 GB (the exact binary capacity from all listed pointers is about 16.063 GiB).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A 1 KB indirect block holds 1024/4=256 disk addresses. The inode can therefore address 10 direct blocks, 256 through the single-indirect entry, 256² through the double-indirect entry and 256³ through the triple-indirect entry. Total size is (10+256+256²+256³)×1 KB = 16,843,018 KB, about 16.063 GiB. The dominant triple-indirect term is exactly 16 GiB, so the offered rounded answer is 16 GB.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: 1 GB is far too small because triple indirection alone reaches 256³ blocks. 30 GB and 64 GB overstate the pointer fan-out; a 1 KB block with 4-byte addresses has 256, not 512 or 1024, pointers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Indirect capacity grows by pointer fan-out: B/addr pointers per level, giving n, n² and n³ data blocks.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Threads in one process share the process's address space and therefore its virtual-memory mappings. The OS does not need a separate virtual-memory state for every thread. Each thread still needs its own program counter/register context, scheduling information and stack so that calls, local variables and execution position remain independent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A and B say 'only' and omit other essential per-thread state. C is false because each thread requires a separate stack. D correctly identifies major state shared at process level rather than duplicated per thread.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Per thread: registers, stack and scheduling state; per process: address space and resources shared by its threads.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Windows XP and Vista-era Windows can recognize and use FAT16, FAT32 and NTFS volumes, so all three listed file systems are within their support. NTFS is the native and normally preferred system-disk format because it adds permissions, journaling, compression, quotas and other features, but support is broader than the default recommendation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A, B and C each name a supported file system but exclude the other supported choices. If the question had asked for the usual installation/default system volume, NTFS would be the better wording; as printed, 'uses' is broad enough that all of the above is intended.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Distinguish a Windows version's supported file systems from its preferred/default system-volume format.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Negation as failure should fail when G succeeds and succeed when G cannot be proved. In option B, call(G) is tried first. If it succeeds, the cut commits to that clause and fail makes not(G) fail. If call(G) fails, execution never reaches the cut, so Prolog tries the second fact not(G), which succeeds. This implements the required operational behavior.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A cuts before trying G, so failure of G cannot fall through to the success clause. C places the cut after fail, where it can never be reached. D lets the second clause succeed even after the first clause proves G and then fails, so it makes not(G) succeed incorrectly.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Prolog negation pattern: call the goal, cut on success, force failure; otherwise fall through and succeed.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: An informed search uses problem-specific heuristic information to choose promising states. Hill climbing follows heuristic improvement, best-first search expands the most promising node according to an evaluation function, and A* uses f(n)=g(n)+h(n). Depth-first search uses only the search-tree structure, expanding the deepest available node without a heuristic, so it is uninformed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A, B and C all explicitly depend on a heuristic or evaluation estimate. D may be combined with heuristics in a hybrid algorithm, but plain DFS itself is not informed search.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Informed search consults h(n) or another domain estimate; DFS needs only a stack and successor relation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Skolemization removes existential quantifiers by replacing each existential variable with a new Skolem term based on the universal variables in whose scope it occurs. The outer ∃u has no preceding universal variables, so u becomes a new constant a. The later ∃y is inside ∀v∀x, so y becomes a new function g(v,x). The remaining formula is universally quantified, exactly as shown.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Simplification is a general rewriting term and does not describe the systematic introduction of Skolem symbols. Unification finds substitutions that make terms equal. Resolution derives clauses by cancelling complementary literals. Neither performs these existential replacements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Existential with no preceding universals→Skolem constant; with preceding universals→Skolem function of them.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Represent a state as (water in 5-L jug, water in 3-L jug). A shortest path is (0,0)→(5,0)→(2,3)→(2,0)→(0,2)→(5,2)→(4,3). The final state has exactly 4 litres in the 5-L jug. This path contains seven states, including the initial state, and breadth-first exploration confirms no shorter state path reaches a quantity of 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Three, four and five states do not allow enough fill, pour and empty transitions to isolate 4 litres using unmarked 5-L and 3-L jugs. Be careful that the path has six actions but seven states; the question asks for states.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: In state-space problems, count nodes (including the start), not just operator applications along the path.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Treat each map region as a variable, its allowed colors as the domain, and every shared border as a binary constraint requiring different colors. A solution is an assignment satisfying all adjacency constraints, which is exactly a constraint-satisfaction problem. Backtracking, constraint propagation and variable-ordering heuristics can then solve it efficiently.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Means-end analysis reduces differences between current and goal states but is not the natural formulation. AO* targets AND-OR graphs. Breadth-first search could enumerate assignments but ignores the problem's constraint structure and is not the defining technique.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Map coloring is the canonical CSP: regions=variables, colors=domains, adjacent regions≠constraints.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A frame is a structured collection of slots, values, defaults and attached procedures used to describe a stereotyped object or situation. A 'restaurant table,' 'classroom' or 'room' frame can supply normal attributes while allowing instance-specific values to override defaults. That matches the wording 'stereotype situation.'
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A semantic network emphasizes concepts and labeled relations. A script specifically represents a stereotyped sequence of events, such as the steps of dining at a restaurant, rather than a situation's slot structure. Conceptual dependency represents sentence meaning through primitive actions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Frames model stereotyped situations; scripts model stereotyped event sequences.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A fuzzy set A is convex when the membership at every point between x1 and x2 is at least the smaller endpoint membership: μ_A(λx1+(1−λ)x2)≥min(μ_A(x1),μ_A(x2)) for 0≤λ≤1. Geometrically, membership cannot dip below the lower endpoint level along a line segment. Equivalently, every α-cut of a convex fuzzy set is a convex ordinary set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Support is the set of points with positive membership. An α-cut is a crisp level set derived from a fuzzy set. 'Concave' is not the name assigned to this standard minimum-based inequality.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Convex fuzzy set: membership along a segment is never below the minimum membership of its endpoints.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: For a fuzzy union, take the componentwise maximum: μ_(A∪B)={0.9,0.5,0.6,0.8,0.8}. The question asks for the complement of that union, whose standard membership is 1−μ. Subtracting each entry from 1 gives {0.1,0.5,0.4,0.2,0.2}, option C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A is the union itself before complementation. B is the componentwise minimum, namely the intersection A∩B. D matches the first four complement values but incorrectly computes 1−0.8 as 0.3 instead of 0.2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Fuzzy union=max; standard complement=1−membership. Apply them in the order indicated by the bar.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The perceptron's net input is t=−1.5+x+y. For binary inputs, (0,0) gives −1.5, (1,0) and (0,1) each give −0.5, and (1,1) gives 0.5. With f(t)=1 only when t>0, the outputs are 0,0,0,1. That is exactly the AND truth table.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: OR would need output 1 for the single-1 inputs, but their net value is −0.5. XOR would require the two single-1 inputs to produce 1 and (1,1) to produce 0; one linear threshold cannot do that. Therefore it cannot solve all three.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2013-june-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Evaluate bias plus weighted inputs on all four binary cases; threshold −1.5+x+y implements AND.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Use inclusion-exclusion. Among 1,…,1000 there are floor(1000/3)=333 multiples of 3 and floor(1000/5)=200 multiples of 5. Multiples of both were counted twice; they are the multiples of lcm(3,5)=15, of which there are floor(1000/15)=66. Therefore the union contains 333+200−66=467 numbers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 533 simply adds the first two counts and double-counts multiples of 15. 66 counts only the overlap. 599 adds rather than subtracts that overlap. Inclusion-exclusion gives 467.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For 'divisible by a or b,' add the two counts and subtract multiples of lcm(a,b).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Let x be the number of degree-1 vertices. Then the tree has n=x+4 vertices. Its degree sum is 2·4+3+2+x=13+x. A tree with n vertices has n−1 edges, so the handshaking lemma gives degree sum 2(n−1)=2(x+3)=2x+6. Equating the sums yields 13+x=2x+6, hence x=7 and n=7+4=11.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 5 and 20 do not satisfy both the specified degrees and the tree identity sum deg=2(n−1). 'n−3' is not a numerical vertex count and does not result from solving the equation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For a tree, combine the handshaking lemma with |E|=|V|−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Both B and C are correct; the question has no unique correct option. The graph is K₃,₃, which is bipartite and Hamiltonian but not complete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The vertices split into {A,C,F} and {B,D,E}, with every edge joining the two sets and all nine cross-set edges present. Thus the graph is the complete bipartite graph K₃,₃, so B is true. It also has a Hamiltonian cycle, for example A–B–C–D–F–E–A, so C is true. It is not the complete graph K₆ because same-part pairs such as A–C are not adjacent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A is false because a complete six-vertex graph would contain every pairwise edge and have 15 edges, not 9. D is false because 'all' includes A. Since B and C are separately true, the MCQ is defective rather than having one valid label.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: K₃,₃ is bipartite by definition and contains a six-vertex alternating cycle, so it is Hamiltonian too.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: On each independent draw, P(k≠1)=1−1/1,000,000. The probability that 1 never appears in one million draws is (1−10^−6)^1,000,000. Therefore P(at least one 1)=1−(1−10^−6)^1,000,000≈1−e^−1=0.63212056, which rounds to 0.632121.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The expected number of occurrences is 1, but that does not make the occurrence probability 1 or 0.5. The other decimals do not match the complement of the zero-occurrence probability.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: At least once = 1 − never; (1−1/n)^n approaches e^−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: First, f(x)=x⁴. Then g(f(x))=√((x⁴)²+1)=√(x⁸+1). Applying h squares this result and adds 72: h(g(f(x)))=x⁸+1+72=x⁸+73. Function composition is associative, so h∘(g∘f) and (h∘g)∘f give the same result, x⁸+73.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The −71 and −73 choices use subtraction even though h adds 72. The +71 choice mishandles the +1 inside g. Correctly squaring the square root contributes +1, producing +73.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Composition is associative; substitute from the innermost function outward and preserve the square root before h squares it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The initial addition of two 4-bit BCD digits and the input carry uses four full adders. If the binary result exceeds 9 or produces a carry, the correction circuit adds 0110. The least significant bit passes unchanged; adding the conditional correction bit at bit 1 needs a half adder, bit 2 needs one full adder because it also receives a carry, and bit 3 needs another half adder. Total: 5 full adders and 2 half adders.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A uses one extra full adder, while B and C use three half adders even though the correction path needs only two. The optimized correction stage is one full plus two half adders beyond the four-full-adder binary stage.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: BCD addition = 4-bit binary addition plus a conditional +0110 correction optimized to 1 FA and 2 HAs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Excess-3 encodes decimal digit d as the four-bit binary value d+3. Bitwise complementing that code produces 15−(d+3)=12−d=(9−d)+3, which is precisely the Excess-3 code for the decimal 9's complement 9−d. Therefore the complement digit is generated simply by inverting every bit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Excess-3 is nonweighted, so B is false. A and D describe numerical sums rather than the defining bitwise self-complement property; D also invokes the wrong decimal complement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: In Excess-3, one's-complementing all four code bits maps digit d directly to digit 9−d.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Intended answer: B, 5 PUSH and 4 POP instructions, under the counting convention assumed by the options.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The postfix form is A B * C D * E / +. It contains five operands, so each operand is pushed once: five PUSH instructions. It contains four binary operators, and the question's convention counts one stack-reduction/POP instruction for each operator, giving four POPs. This matches B. A literal low-level implementation may count two operand pops and one result push per operator; none of the options uses that convention, so the exam is clearly using the simplified expression-stack count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A omits one operand and one operator reduction. C invents a sixth operand push. D has the correct five operands but counts only three of the four operators (*,*,/,+).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Count five operand tokens and four binary-operator reductions in the postfix expression.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: With an 8-bit excess-128 exponent, stored values 0 through 255 represent exponents −128 through 127. A normalized 23-bit binary fraction ranges from 0.100…₂=2^−1 to 0.111…₂=1−2^−23. Thus the smallest positive normalized magnitude is 2^−1·2^−128=2^−129, and the largest is (1−2^−23)·2^127.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A misses the leading fractional factor 2^−1 in the minimum. B reverses and misstates the endpoints. C incorrectly uses the mantissa bit count as the maximum exponent. The question is not using IEEE-754's bias-127 hidden-leading-one convention; it explicitly specifies excess 128 and a binary fraction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Range = normalized mantissa endpoint × exponent endpoint; for a binary fraction the minimum mantissa is 0.1₂.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A ROM needs one address bit for every input bit. The two 8-bit operands contribute 16 address inputs, while mode control and carry input add two more, for 18 address bits and 2^18 words. Each word must produce the 8-bit sum/difference plus carry output and overflow output, totaling 10 output bits. The required organization is therefore 2^18×10.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The 2^16 choices ignore mode and carry input. The 8-bit-wide choices omit the carry and overflow outputs. Only B includes all 18 inputs and all 10 outputs.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: ROM realization: 2^(number of input variables) words × number of output functions bits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2014-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Post-increment yields the old value of x for the current expression, then increments x as a side effect. Thus x++ contributes 128 to 1+x++, making the printed expression 1+128=129. After evaluation x itself becomes 129, but that new value is not substituted back into the already evaluated addition.
