sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Apply the extension principle separately to each possible sum z. For z=12 only 1+11 contributes, giving min(0.3,0.5)=0.3. For z=13 take max{min(0.3,1),min(0.6,0.5)}=0.5. Continuing in the same way gives memberships μ(14)=0.6, μ(15)=1, μ(16)=0.7, μ(17)=0.5, and μ(18)=0.2. Thus A+B={(0.3,12),(0.5,13),(0.6,14),(1,15),(0.7,16),(0.5,17),(0.2,18)}, exactly option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 incorrectly retain membership 1 at several sums even when no pair producing that sum has both memberships equal to 1. Option 3 underestimates z=14: the pair 2+12 has minimum membership min(0.6,1)=0.6, which dominates the other pairs.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: For fuzzy addition, enumerate all pairs with x+y=z, take min for each pair, then take the maximum of those minima.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.additionalInformation: The printed formula shows μ_B(x) in the minimum, but the constraint x+y=z makes μ_B(y) the mathematically intended term; the computation above uses that standard extension-principle definition.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: First compute the weighted input: W₁x₁+W₂x₂=(−3.9)(1.3)+(1.1)(2.2)=−5.07+2.42=−2.65. A binary perceptron with threshold T=0.3 outputs 1 only when the weighted input reaches or exceeds 0.3; otherwise it outputs 0. Since −2.65<0.3, the output is 0, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: −2.65 is the net input before the activation function, not the perceptron's binary output. −2.30 results from incorrectly adding the threshold to the net value. Output 1 would require the threshold test to succeed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Separate the two stages: calculate the weighted sum, then apply the threshold activation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: The command wc -l counts input lines. The redirection < a makes file a its standard input, and > b sends its standard output to file b, replacing b if it already exists. The trailing & asks the shell to run the command asynchronously as a background job. Therefore option 2 describes all four parts correctly.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Option 1 ignores the trailing background operator. wc does not count execution errors, so option 3 is unrelated. The -l option counts lines; it does not copy a specified number of lines, so option 4 is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Read a shell command left to right: program and option, input redirection, output redirection, then job-control operator.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.additionalInformation: The source prints 'WC' and a typographic dash, but UNIX command names and options are normally written exactly as wc -l; the answer choices make that intended command unambiguous.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: cron is a long-running scheduling daemon. It starts as part of system initialization, reads scheduling configuration, and runs commands at their prescribed times. Individual users can install personal crontab entries, and those crontabs are stored in an implementation-specific spool or service database. Consequently the claim that individual-user crontabs are not stored is false; option 4 is the requested answer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 3 describe the ordinary cron model: scheduled command execution, a daemon available while the system runs, and configuration containing command/time specifications.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: cron is the scheduler daemon; crontab is the stored schedule submitted by a system administrator or an individual user.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Each octal permission digit encodes read=4, write=2, and execute=1 for owner, group, and others. Mode 641 therefore expands to owner 6=rw−, group 4=r−−, others 1=−−x, exactly as statement I says. Mode 100 expands to owner −−x, group −−−, others −−−, so only the owner may execute and every other permission is absent. Statement II is also correct. Hence option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 wrongly reject one true decoding; option 4 rejects both. The quickest check is to translate each octal digit independently using 4-2-1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: UNIX permission digits are sums of r=4, w=2, x=1, ordered as owner-group-others.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Let P be −2≤x≤−1 and Q be 1≤x≤2. The statement is P∨Q, so De Morgan's law gives ¬P∧¬Q: x must lie outside both closed intervals. On the real line that complement is x<−2, or −1<x<1, or x>2. This is exactly option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Option 2 omits the entire gap between −1 and 1. Option 3 omits both exterior rays. Option 4 uses non-strict endpoints and would include −2 and 2 even though both satisfy the original statement and therefore must be excluded from its negation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: The negation of membership in a union of closed intervals is the complement: the two outer open rays plus every open gap between the intervals.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A Management Information System converts routine organizational data into structured summaries and reports for managers. It draws on several known, recurring data sources and aggregates them into predictable information products. That is the description in option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Option 1 describes a Decision Support System, which helps formulate problems and compare alternatives. Option 3 describes a Transaction Processing System's high-volume data-capture role. Option 4 emphasizes efficient movement, processing, and integration of transaction streams rather than the management-reporting role that distinguishes an MIS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: TPS records operations; MIS summarizes recurring data for management; DSS supports less-structured choices among alternatives.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: Randomized hill climbing samples a move from the current state's legal moveset—that is, from neighboring states—and accepts it only when it improves the evaluation value. It is still a hill-climbing method, so it does not deliberately accept a downhill move in this definition. Therefore option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 4 sample from the whole state space rather than from legal neighboring moves. Option 1 accepts every sampled move, including worse ones, which turns the procedure into a random walk rather than hill climbing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Hill climbing is local and improvement-driven; randomization changes which neighbor is tried, not the requirement to climb.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: A is MAX and its children B and C are MIN nodes. Evaluating B from left to right gives min(3,12,8)=3, so A's current alpha becomes 3. At C, the first examined child G has value 2, making C's beta equal to 2. Because beta≤alpha (2≤3), MAX would never choose C over the already available value 3, regardless of C's remaining children. The branches to H and I are therefore pruned. This is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: I alone understates the cutoff: both unvisited siblings H and I are skipped. C itself and G must be visited before the cutoff can be established, so CHI and GHI are incorrect.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: At a MIN node, prune remaining children as soon as beta≤the alpha inherited from a MAX ancestor.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: For a 2×2 sliding puzzle, reachability preserves the parity of (number of inversions among the numbered tiles + blank row counted from the bottom). The initial state [1,2; blank,3] has 0 inversions and blank-row value 1, so the invariant is odd. Option 1 has 2+1=3, option 2 has 3+2=5, and option 4 has 1+2=3; all are odd and therefore reachable. Option 3, [1,3; blank,2], has one inversion and blank-row value 1, totaling 2, which is even. It lies in the other parity class and cannot be reached.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: The other three states have the same solvability parity as the initial state. A sequence of legal blank moves can reach each of them; visual similarity or tile order alone is not a reliable test.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: On an even-width sliding puzzle, compare the parity of inversions plus the blank's row from the bottom.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.explanation: An inference engine is the reasoning component of a knowledge-based or expert system. It applies rules or other inference procedures to facts in the knowledge base, derives consequences, and adds newly inferred knowledge. That is precisely the function described, so option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.whyOthersFail: A data dictionary describes data definitions and metadata. 'Reference mechanism' is not the reasoning component named here. A control strategy determines the order in which rules or search alternatives are considered, but the inference engine is the program that performs the overall infer-and-manipulate operation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2016-july-paper-3.pdf.keyIdea: Knowledge base stores facts and rules; inference engine applies them; control strategy helps choose which inference to perform next.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Generate terms directly from the recurrence. F₀₀(2)=(10·1+100)/1=110 and F₀₀(3)=(10·110+100)/1=1200. Next, F₀₀(4)=(10·1200+100)/110=110, F₀₀(5)=(10·110+100)/1200=1, and F₀₀(6)=(10·1+100)/110=1. The ordered pair of consecutive values has now returned to (1,1), so the pattern repeats. The only distinct values ever produced are {1,110,1200}, which is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other options include 2, 55, or 600, none of which is produced by substituting into the recurrence. Because this is a second-order recurrence, recognizing the repeated consecutive pair (1,1) proves the later sequence repeats rather than merely suggesting a pattern.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For a deterministic second-order recurrence, repetition of the same consecutive pair forces the entire future sequence to repeat.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Absurd means clearly impossible or contrary to evident truth, so a→i. Ambiguous means capable of multiple interpretations, so b→ii. An axiom is accepted as a starting assertion without proof, so c→iii. A conjecture is a proposed opinion or claim supported by observation or experience but not yet proved, so d→iv. The sequence i, ii, iii, iv is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The remaining codes interchange terms with definitions that have sharply different logical status: an axiom is assumed, a conjecture awaits proof, ambiguity concerns multiple meanings, and absurdity concerns contradiction or impossibility.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Axiom = accepted starting point; conjecture = plausible unproved claim; ambiguous = multiple meanings; absurd = evidently impossible.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Composition is evaluated from right to left. Since g(x)=1/(x²+1), (h∘g)(x)=h(g(x))=[1/(x²+1)]⁴=(x²+1)⁻⁴. For the three-function composition, first f(x)=x³−4x; then g(f(x))=1/[(x³−4x)²+1]; finally raising this to the fourth power gives (h∘g∘f)(x)=[(x³−4x)²+1]⁻⁴. Both expressions match option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 give the wrong positive exponent for h∘g. Option 3 changes f(x) from x³−4x to x²−4x and also gives the wrong sign on the final exponent.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: In h∘g∘f, apply f first, substitute that result into g, and only then apply h.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Interpret 'between' as excluding both endpoints. Between 0 and 100, the multiples are 6,12,…,96, so their count is floor(99/6)=16. Between −6 and 34, they are 0,6,12,18,24,30, giving 6 values. Therefore the pair is 16 and 6, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Counts of 17 arise only when an endpoint that is itself a multiple of 6 is included: including 0 in the first interval gives 17, and including −6 in the second gives 7. That is not the strict-between convention used by the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Before counting integer multiples, translate 'between' into explicit inequalities and decide whether endpoints are included.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: The English word 'between' can be used inclusively in informal speech. If both endpoints were included, the answer would be 17 and 7 (option 3); the exam's intended mathematical reading is the open interval in option 1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: No listed option is necessarily true — the three conditions are sufficient for Hamiltonicity, not necessary
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A Hamiltonian graph contains a spanning cycle, but it need not be dense. Take the cycle graph C₆. It is Hamiltonian, simple, and has n=6. Every vertex has degree 2, so statement 1 fails because 2<6/2. It has only 6 edges, so statement 2 fails because 6<(1/2)(5)(4)+2=12. Any two nonadjacent vertices still have degree sum 2+2=4<6, so statement 3 also fails. Thus a single valid Hamiltonian graph refutes all three universal claims, and 'all of the above' is also false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Statements 1 and 3 are the hypotheses of Dirac's and Ore's theorems: if a graph satisfies them, Hamiltonicity follows. The implication does not reverse. The large edge-count bound is likewise a sufficient density condition, not something every Hamiltonian graph must satisfy.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Do not reverse a sufficient-condition theorem: dense enough ⇒ Hamiltonian does not mean Hamiltonian ⇒ dense enough.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: This question is defective as printed because it provides no 'none of the above' option. A cycle Cₙ for sufficiently large n is the standard counterexample.

sources.title: Biton, Levi and Medina — Dirac and Ore graph conditions

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Use proof by cases on P∨R. If P is true, P→Q gives Q, hence Q∨S. If R is true, R→S gives S, hence Q∨S. Since at least one of P and R holds, Q∨S follows in every case. This inference is the constructive dilemma, so option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: P∨R is merely the second premise, not the new conclusion. P∨S and Q∨R mix an antecedent from one branch with a consequent from the other and do not follow: the premise permits P true with R false, or conversely.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Constructive dilemma: (P→Q)∧(R→S), P∨R ⊢ Q∨S.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Emitter-coupled logic uses a differential-amplifier arrangement whose transistors switch current between branches without being driven into saturation. Avoiding saturation eliminates the storage time needed to remove excess charge from a saturated transistor, producing very small propagation delay. Therefore the blank is saturation, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Race condition is a timing interaction between signals or processes, not a transistor operating region. Delay is the quantity ECL reduces, not a state into which the transistor is driven. High impedance is associated with tri-state outputs and does not explain ECL's differential switching speed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: ECL is fast because its transistors remain in the active region and avoid saturation-storage delay.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A J-K flip-flop toggles when J=K=1. In a synchronous binary down counter, the least-significant bit Q₀ toggles on every clock, so J₀=K₀=1 (II). Q₁ toggles when the lower bit is 0, giving J₁=K₁=Q̅₀ (IV). Q₂ toggles when both lower bits are 0, giving J₂=K₂=Q̅₁Q̅₀ (VI). Thus II, IV, and VI are correct, which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: I would freeze Q₀ instead of toggling it. III and V are the enabling conditions used for an up counter, where a higher bit toggles when all lower bits are 1. A down counter uses the complemented lower bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Synchronous up counter: toggle on all lower 1s; synchronous down counter: toggle on all lower 0s.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Each octal fractional digit has weight 8⁻ᵏ. Therefore (0.4051)₈=4/8+0/64+5/512+1/4096=0.5+0+0.009765625+0.000244140625=0.510009765625. Rounded to the digits shown in the choices, this is 0.5100098, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other values do not use the positional weights 8⁻¹,8⁻²,8⁻³,8⁻⁴ correctly. Octal digits after the point are not interpreted as a decimal fraction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For a radix-r fraction, the first digit after the point has weight r⁻¹, the next r⁻², and so on.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Convert through binary because each octal digit represents three bits: 2→010, 3→011, 5→101, 7→111. Thus (2357)₈=(010011101111)₂. Regroup into four-bit hexadecimal nibbles: 0100 1110 1111, which map to 4, E, F. Hence the hexadecimal number is 4EF, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 2EE, 2FF, and 4FE result from incorrect bit grouping or digit conversion. The grouping must preserve the original bit order and proceed in groups of four from the radix point.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Octal↔binary uses 3-bit groups; hexadecimal↔binary uses 4-bit groups.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A function argument must be an expression or object whose value/reference can be supplied to a parameter. Constants can be passed by value, structures can be passed by value or reference, and an array argument is passed through a pointer-like adjustment or can be bound to an array reference. A header file is source text included during preprocessing, not a runtime value or object. Therefore option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A literal such as 5 is a valid constant argument. A structure object is an ordinary object that may be copied or referenced. An array name commonly supplies the address of its first element to a pointer parameter, despite the informal phrase 'passing an array.'
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Headers participate in translation through #include; function arguments exist in the C++ expression and object model.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Each overload is a distinct function with its own parameter-type list and, normally, its own definition and generated function body. During overload resolution the compiler chooses the best matching overload for a call. Thus the compiler sets up a separate function for every overload definition, as stated in option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 2 denies the distinct implementations that overloading creates. Handling different argument types is the main purpose of overloading, so option 3 is false. Overloads may have the same number of arguments as long as parameter types or qualifying details distinguish their signatures, so option 4 is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Overloading reuses one name for several compile-time-distinguishable function signatures.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The extern storage-class specifier declares an object or function whose definition may occur elsewhere and normally has external linkage, allowing the same entity to be referenced across translation units. In the textbook terminology of the question, this is global visibility. Therefore option 2 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: auto and register describe block-scope automatic objects. static does not by itself mean globally visible: at file scope it gives internal linkage, restricting the name to that translation unit, while a block-scope static name remains visible only in its block even though its lifetime spans the program.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Keep scope, storage duration, and linkage separate: extern is associated with external linkage; static commonly restricts file-scope linkage or extends local lifetime.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In C++, the member-access operator . (called 'structure reference' here) cannot be overloaded. Its built-in meaning is fundamental to selecting a member from an object. Therefore option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The compound right-shift assignment operator >>= can be overloaded. Unary address-of & and unary indirection * can also be overloaded for class or enumeration operands. The question says C/C++, but user-defined operator overloading is a C++ feature; plain C has no such mechanism.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Common non-overloadable C++ operators include ., .*, ::, ?:, and sizeof.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A positive power of two has exactly one set bit: X=100…0₂. Subtracting one clears that position and makes every lower bit 1: X−1=011…1₂. The two numbers therefore have no position in which both bits are 1, so X & (X−1)=000…0₂. This is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: All-ones is X−1, not the AND result. 100…0 is X itself. 000…1 would require the least-significant bit to be set in both operands, which cannot happen for a power of two larger than 1; for X=1 the result is still zero.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: The test X>0 and (X & (X−1))=0 recognizes powers of two because subtraction removes the only set bit.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Under the question's single-byte-character convention, VARCHAR(20) stores only the characters actually present, up to a maximum of 20. 'Ram' therefore occupies 3 character spaces. CHAR(20) is fixed-width and blank-pads 'Sita' from length 4 to length 20, so it occupies 20 character spaces. The pair is 3,20, which is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options assigning 20 to A treat variable-width VARCHAR as fixed-width. Options assigning 4 to B ignore CHAR padding. The declared size is a maximum for VARCHAR but the fixed stored width for CHAR in the simplified model.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: CHAR(n) is fixed and padded; VARCHAR(n) is variable and stores the actual value length up to n.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: Real Oracle storage includes length metadata and may use multiple bytes per character depending on the character set. The MCQ uses the usual one-character/one-byte teaching model and asks only for the value-space comparison.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A primary-key column is subject to entity integrity, so an instructor identifier chosen as the primary key cannot be NULL; A is true. Aadhaar ID is intended to identify one citizen uniquely, so a uniqueness constraint expresses B; B is true. There is no general database-integrity rule that a company's budget must equal zero; a sensible domain rule might require it to be nonnegative instead. Thus C is false and option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 incorrectly accept C. Option 2 also rejects the NOT NULL property of a primary key. Option 4 rejects both standard key constraints.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Primary key means unique and not NULL; other domain constraints must reflect a valid business rule rather than an arbitrary value.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Create one table for entity M and one for entity N. A one-to-many relationship R₁ with no relationship attributes can be represented by placing the primary key of the 'one' side as a foreign key in the table on the 'many' side, so it needs no separate relation. The many-to-many relationship R₂ requires a junction table containing the keys of M and N. Total tables=2 entity tables+1 junction table=3, which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Four would unnecessarily create a separate table for the one-to-many relationship. Six and seven count each conceptual component or attribute as though it always required an independent table, which is not how ER-to-relational mapping works for simple single-valued attributes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: 1:N relationship → foreign key on N side; M:N relationship → separate junction relation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The decomposition preserves dependencies because M→N can be enforced entirely in R₁(MN), while P→Q can be enforced entirely in R₂(PQ). For a binary decomposition to be lossless, the common attributes must functionally determine all attributes of at least one component. Here R₁∩R₂=∅, so that condition fails. Joining arbitrary R₁ and R₂ instances forms a Cartesian product and can create combinations of MN with PQ that were not together in the original R. Hence the decomposition is dependency preserving but not lossless, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 3 incorrectly claim losslessness despite the empty intersection. Option 4 incorrectly denies dependency preservation even though each given functional dependency is contained in one projected schema.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Binary lossless test: (R₁∩R₂)→R₁ or (R₁∩R₂)→R₂ must follow from F⁺.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: No listed option — the stated sizes allow 48 leaf entries (or 49 internal children), not 16, 63, 64, or 65
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A B+ tree leaf stores search-key/data-pointer pairs and normally one pointer to the next leaf. Each pair here needs 14+7=21 bytes. Even ignoring every other overhead, floor(1024/21)=48 pairs fit; reserving a 7-byte next-leaf pointer still gives floor((1024−7)/21)=48. If the question instead intended an internal-node order p, its size equation would be 7p+14(p−1)≤1024, yielding p≤49. Neither standard interpretation produces any listed option.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 16 is merely a smaller number that would fit, not the maximum. Values 63, 64, and 65 would require at least 1323, 1344, and 1365 bytes respectively for 21-byte entries, already exceeding a 1 KiB block before overhead.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Derive B+ tree capacity from a byte inequality; do not choose an option merely because that many entries would fit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: The stem is also terminologically loose: leaf nodes have data pointers/entries and a sibling pointer, whereas 'number of children' conventionally describes internal-node fan-out. This item is defective as printed.

sources.title: University of Cape Town — B+ tree internal and leaf-node structure

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In a contiguous sorted array, inserting an element may require shifting every larger element one position, so worst-case insertion is O(n). Deleting an element may require shifting all following elements left, so deletion is O(n). Because the array is sorted, the minimum and maximum are stored at the two endpoints and each can be read in O(1). This combination is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 ignore the shifting cost of maintaining sorted order. Option 4 ignores the direct endpoint access that makes extrema constant-time. Searching for an arbitrary value may be logarithmic, but the question asks only for known endpoints and update operations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Sorted arrays trade expensive O(n) updates for O(1) endpoint extrema and O(log n) binary search.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Pushing in reverse order gives the stack bottom-to-top as G,F,E,D,C,B,A. Five pops produce A,B,C,D,E, which enter the queue in that order; the stack then contains G,F with F on top. Dequeue A and push it, then dequeue B and push it. The stack is now G,F,A,B from bottom to top. The next pop therefore returns B, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A was pushed back first and lies immediately below B. F was the old stack top before the two queue items were restored. G remains at the bottom throughout. Confusing FIFO queue order with LIFO stack order leads to the other choices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Write each container explicitly after every phase: stack reverses order; queue preserves order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A valid binary max-heap must be a complete binary tree and every parent must be at least as large as each child. Tree B has root 16, then 14 and 10, and every lower comparison also satisfies the max-heap order: 14≥8,7; 10≥9,3; 8≥2,4; and 7≥1. Its last level is filled from left to right, so it is complete. Therefore B, option 2, is the valid heap.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: In A, node 4 has child 8, violating parent≥child. In C, node 1 has child 7. In D, node 3 has children 9 and 10 (and other lower violations also occur). Each fails the max-heap order even though the drawing resembles a tree.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Heap validity requires both shape (complete tree) and order (parent dominates children); neither property alone is enough.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: For every other stored key y, universality gives Pr[h(x)=h(y)]≤1/m. Let an indicator variable record whether y collides with x. By linearity of expectation, E[collisions involving x]≤(n−1)/m<n/m. The standard specific bound requested is therefore n/m, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 1/n and 1/m are bounds for a single probability scale, not the sum over up to n−1 other keys. Because n≤m, the derived expectation is also less than 1, so option 1 is a weaker true statement; option 4 is the tight standard expression in terms of n and m.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Universal hashing: sum at most n−1 collision probabilities, each at most 1/m, to obtain expected collisions below n/m.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: The choices are not logically exclusive: <n/m together with n≤m implies <1. For an MCQ, choose option 4 because it states the informative theorem-level bound; option 1 is only a consequence.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Statement B is false. To find connected components, start BFS from any unvisited vertex, mark every reached vertex as one component, and repeat from another unvisited vertex until none remain. Thus BFS works perfectly well. A is true because optimal-BST construction has a standard dynamic-programming solution. C is true for a general binary tree because preorder plus postorder does not uniquely locate a single child as left or right. D is true because the same repeated-search method works with DFS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A, C, and D are true statements, so selecting them would not answer 'which is false.' The qualification 'general binary tree' matters for C; additional restrictions such as every internal node having two children can restore uniqueness.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Both BFS and DFS can enumerate connected components; the traversal strategy changes the exploration order, not reachability.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: HTTP is an application-layer protocol, so a→iv. TCP provides transport, so b→i. IP provides network-layer addressing and routing, so c→ii. PPP frames data over a point-to-point link and belongs to the data-link layer, so d→iii. The mapping iv,i,ii,iii is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes move TCP, IP, PPP, or HTTP to layers whose functions they do not perform. SSL/TLS protects the application exchange above TCP, but it does not change the four listed protocol-to-layer identities.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: HTTP/application → TCP/transport → IP/network → PPP/data link.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: No listed option — an application can pass an arbitrarily long byte stream to TCP, which segments it for transmission
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: TCP presents a reliable byte-stream service rather than a one-application-write/one-segment message service. An application may supply a large stream; TCP buffers it and divides it into segments governed by the effective MSS and the network path. The 16-bit IPv4 Total Length field constrains one IPv4 datagram, not the total amount an application may pass to TCP. Therefore none of the four fixed sizes answers the question as written.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 2¹⁶ and 2¹⁵ confuse per-packet header fields or historical window values with the application-to-TCP interface. Adding a TCP header is dimensionally backward for a payload limit, while subtracting only the TCP header ignores the IP header and still addresses one segment rather than the complete stream.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Application write size, TCP segment payload (MSS), IP datagram size, and link MTU are different limits at different interfaces.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: This defective item repeats a well-known networking MCQ whose correct answer is 'any size,' but that choice is missing. Actual APIs may impose implementation-specific buffer or call-size limits, not the protocol-level 2¹⁶ choices shown here.

sources.title: RFC 9293 — Transmission Control Protocol

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The host applies its subnet mask to determine whether the destination is on the local IP subnet. If it is not, the host sends the frame to the next-hop router configured by the default route—the default gateway—while keeping the remote host as the packet's IP destination. Therefore option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A file server supplies files, DNS resolves names to addresses, and DHCP supplies configuration parameters. None of those services forwards ordinary off-subnet traffic. The gateway's link-layer address is obtained locally, commonly through ARP.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: On-subnet destination → deliver directly; off-subnet destination → send the frame to a router/default gateway.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In distance-vector routing, each router periodically or reactively sends its current distance vector to directly connected neighbors. A router combines the received neighbor costs with its link cost to that neighbor, applying the Bellman-Ford recurrence to update its own table. Hence the tables are updated by exchanging information with neighbor nodes, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 'Automatically' is too vague and omits the mechanism. A central server and a backup database are not required by the distributed distance-vector algorithm.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Distance vector shares route costs with neighbors; link state floods topology information to all routers.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: After link-state packets have been flooded, every router has a link-state database representing the network graph and edge costs. Each router runs Dijkstra's shortest-path-first algorithm with itself as the source to construct a shortest-path tree and derive next hops. Thus option 2 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: DES and RSA are cryptographic algorithms, not routing algorithms. Packets carry the topology information but are not themselves a computation method.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Link-state routing = flood link-state information, build a topology map, run Dijkstra locally.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Read the expression in three parts: p+ requires one or more p characters; [3−5]* permits zero or more digits chosen only from 3,4,5; and [xyz] requires exactly one final x, y, or z. I, p443y, satisfies all three parts. IV, p35z, and V, p353535x, also satisfy them. II contains forbidden digit 6, III does not start with p, and VI ends in 5 rather than x/y/z. Thus I, IV, and V only—option 4—match.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Every other answer set includes at least one invalid string or omits a valid one. The plus applies only to p, the star only to the digit class, and the final character class is mandatory.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Parse a regular expression by quantifier scope: p+ · [3−5]* · [xyz].

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The first two moves make AX=0F0Fh because decimal 15 is 0Fh. xor al,al clears AL, so AX=0F00h. Shifting right by 3 gives AX=01E0h and CF=0 because the three discarded low bits were zero. add al,90h computes E0h+90h=170h, leaving AL=70h and setting carry. Finally adc ah,0 adds that carry to AH=01h, making AH=02h. Therefore AX=0270h, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 0170h omits the carry added into AH. 01E0h is the intermediate value immediately after the shift. 0370h adds an extra, nonexistent carry or miscomputes the shift result.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Track AX as AH:AL after every instruction, and remember that ADC adds both its explicit operand and CF.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Lexical structure—identifiers, literals, keywords, and similar tokens—is normally described by regular expressions and recognized by finite automata. Syntactic phrase structure is described by a context-free grammar and recognized by a parser whose stack gives pushdown-automaton power. Statement I assigns the syntax mechanisms to lexical analysis, while statement II assigns the lexical mechanisms to syntax analysis. Both are false, so option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 3 accept at least one reversed statement. The correct pairing is lexical→regular expressions/finite automata and syntax→context-free grammars/pushdown-style parsing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Scanner: regular language; parser: context-free language.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Compute the 8-bit subtraction AL−BL=3Ah−49h. Since 3Ah<49h, a borrow occurs and CF becomes 1. The difference is −0Fh; in 8-bit two's-complement arithmetic, 100h−0Fh=F1h. The most significant bit of F1h is 1, so SF=1. Thus AL=F1h, CF=1, SF=1, which is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 0Fh is the unsigned magnitude of the difference but ignores the negative two's-complement result. F0h is off by one. 1Fh reverses or otherwise miscomputes the operands. A borrow must set CF in x86 SUB semantics.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For 8-bit subtraction, wrap a negative result modulo 256; CF records unsigned borrow and SF copies the result's top bit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.additionalInformation: The source incorrectly calls this an 8085 question even though AL, BL, CF, SF, and the two-operand SUB syntax are x86/8086 conventions. The calculation follows the registers and instruction actually printed.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A linker combines separately assembled or compiled object modules and required libraries into one executable or load module, so statement I is true. It also resolves symbolic external references and relocates addresses. Statement II reverses that direction: the linker replaces unresolved symbolic references with resolved addresses, not absolute references with symbolic ones. Therefore only I is true, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 3 accept the reversed description in II. Option 4 incorrectly denies the linker's central module-combination role.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Compiler/assembler produces object modules with symbols and relocation records; linker combines modules, resolves symbols, and relocates addresses.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Start at semaphore value 1. P2 performs wait: 1→0 and enters. P1 performs wait: 0→−1 and blocks. P3 performs wait: −1→−2 and blocks. When P2 exits it performs signal: −2→−1 and wakes one waiter, P1. When P1 later exits, signal changes −1→0 and wakes P3. The final semaphore value is 0, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A final value 1 would mean the critical section is free with nobody admitted, but P3 has just been awakened/admitted. Values −1 or −2 would mean one or two processes remain queued, whereas both signals have removed the two waiters from the queue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: With the stated convention, wait decrements first and a negative value counts waiters; signal increments and wakes one waiter when needed.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A TLB hit costs the 30 ns lookup plus one 90 ns memory access, totaling 120 ns. On a miss, the system pays the lookup, one memory access for the page-table entry, and one for the requested word: 30+90+90=210 ns. With hit ratio 0.7, EAT=0.7(120)+0.3(210)=84+63=147 ns. Hence option 2 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 120 ns is only the hit-path time. 84 ns multiplies the hit time by the hit probability but omits the miss contribution. 48 ns comes from mixing probabilities or omitting required memory accesses.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Effective time is a weighted average of complete hit and miss paths, including the TLB lookup on both.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The device controller is hardware that performs the actual data transfer, so a→iii. The device driver translates and processes an OS I/O request for its particular device, so b→iv. When the device interrupts, the interrupt handler reads controller status/data registers and places or accounts for the data in a buffer, so c→i. The device-independent kernel I/O subsystem performs functions such as I/O scheduling, so d→ii. The code iii,iv,i,ii is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other mappings confuse hardware transfer, device-specific request handling, interrupt completion work, and device-independent scheduling. These roles occur at different layers of the I/O path.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Controller=hardware transfer; driver=device-specific request; interrupt handler=completion/status; kernel I/O subsystem=scheduling and common services.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Priority scheduling can indefinitely postpone low-priority jobs if higher-priority work keeps arriving. Shortest-process-next can indefinitely postpone a long job when short jobs keep arriving, and its preemptive version, shortest-remaining-time-first, has the same risk. FCFS serves jobs in arrival order, and Round Robin repeatedly gives each ready job a time slice, so neither normally starves a continuously ready process. Thus c,d,e—option 2—may cause starvation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 3, and 4 include FCFS or Round Robin, which provide eventual service under the standard assumptions, or omit one of the size/priority-biased schedulers that can postpone a job indefinitely.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A policy can starve jobs when a continuing stream of 'better' jobs—higher priority or shorter—can always overtake them.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A distributed operating system runs over loosely coupled hardware: autonomous computers have their own processors and memories and communicate through a network. Its OS software is tightly coordinated to present users and applications with a coherent single-system image. Therefore it is tightly coupled OS software on loosely coupled hardware, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Loosely coupled software on loosely coupled hardware describes a network operating-system style in which machines remain visibly separate. Tightly coupled hardware characterizes shared-memory multiprocessors rather than the standard distributed-system model.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Distributed OS: physically separate machines, logically integrated system service.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Software engineering applies systematic engineering principles, methods, tools, management, and quality practices throughout requirements, development, deployment, evolution, and maintenance. It is therefore concerned with all aspects of software production, as stated in option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 1 focuses on computer operation and architecture. Option 2 describes foundational computer-science theory. Option 4 is the broader discipline of systems engineering because it explicitly includes hardware and overall process engineering, not software engineering alone.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Computer science studies foundations; software engineering manages and performs the complete production and evolution of software; systems engineering spans the whole computer-based system.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: McCall groups software quality factors into product operation, product revision, and product transition. Operational characteristics correspond to product operation; ability to undergo change corresponds to revision; and adaptability to new environments corresponds to transition. Production costs and scheduling are project-management concerns, not one of these three product-quality aspects. Therefore option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 3 are plain-language descriptions of revision, transition, and operation respectively, so each belongs to McCall's framework.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: McCall's three views: operate the product, revise the product, transition the product.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: S1 is false because it defines cohesion—the logical relatedness of responsibilities within one module—not coupling. S2 is false because interaction or dependence between different modules is coupling, not cohesion. S3 is true: high cohesion localizes related work inside modules, while low coupling limits dependencies, so changing one module is less likely to affect others. Hence only S3 is true, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 3, and 4 accept S1 or S2 even though the two definitions have been swapped. The desired design combination is explicitly the one in S3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Cohesion is within a module; coupling is between modules. Prefer high cohesion and low coupling.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A prototype gives the customer something concrete to examine, making hidden needs, misunderstandings, interface expectations, and priorities easier to discover. It is especially useful when the customer cannot state requirements clearly at the outset. Therefore option 2 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: When requirements are already stable and complete, prototyping offers less discovery value. Team size alone does not make it the best process model. Prototypes can be throwaway or evolutionary and often provide valuable learning; they do not 'rarely' produce meaningful outcomes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Prototype to learn and clarify uncertain requirements through early feedback.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The Decorator pattern wraps an object in another object implementing the same interface and adds behavior before or after delegating to the wrapped component. Decorators can be composed at run time, so responsibilities can be added without changing the original class. Thus option 2 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Adapter changes one interface into another. Proxy controls access to another object, for example for lazy loading or remote access. Delegation is a general object-composition mechanism rather than the named pattern whose intent is dynamic responsibility enhancement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Decorator preserves the interface and layers optional behavior around an object dynamically.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Affiliate marketing uses partner links or logos that send prospective buyers to a vendor, so a→i. Viral marketing spreads a message through recipients' word-of-mouth sharing, so b→ii. Group purchasing aggregates many small buyers to gain volume bargaining power, so c→iii. Online bartering exchanges surplus goods or services through an intermediary and may use credits or points, so d→iv. The code i,ii,iii,iv is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes interchange mechanisms with different sources of value: referrals, social propagation, pooled demand, and reciprocal exchange. Each description in List II directly names one mechanism.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Affiliate=referral; viral=sharing; group purchase=aggregate demand; barter=exchange without ordinary cash settlement.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Data mining is the semi-automatic or computational discovery of useful, previously unknown patterns, associations, clusters, anomalies, or predictive relationships in large datasets. That definition matches the stem, so option 1 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A data warehouse integrates historical data for analysis but is a repository and architecture, not the pattern-discovery process itself. A DBMS manages storage and queries generally. Data mirroring maintains a duplicate copy for availability or recovery.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Warehouse stores analysis-ready data; data mining discovers patterns in it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A travelling salesperson or field-service worker benefits from mobile access to customer records, orders, schedules, inventory, and communication while away from an office, so A is a mobile-computing application. Location-aware services use a device's current position to provide navigation, nearby search, tracking, or contextual content, so B is also an application. Both are true, giving option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 incorrectly reject one established use case, while option 3 rejects both. Mobility supports both work performed while travelling and services whose behavior depends on location.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Mobile computing supports users on the move; location awareness is one important context-sensitive capability within it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The conventional 3.84 Mcps W-CDMA/UMTS carrier spreads information across approximately a 5 MHz radio channel. Among the listed allocations, 5 MHz is therefore the minimum single-carrier spectrum block intended by the question, so option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 2 MHz is too narrow for the conventional wideband carrier, while 20 kHz and 5 kHz are narrowband channel spacings many orders smaller than a W-CDMA carrier.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Classic UMTS/W-CDMA: 3.84 Mcps chip rate and approximately 5 MHz carrier bandwidth.

sources.title: ETSI/3GPP TS 25.201 — UMTS physical-layer general description

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: ERP systems integrate data and workflows across functions such as finance, procurement, production, inventory, sales, and human resources, so A is true. A shared integrated database makes current transaction information available across modules in near real time, so B is true in the textbook ERP model. ERP adoption requires software, infrastructure, configuration, data migration, process redesign, training, and change management, so it is not generally inexpensive; C is false. Therefore option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 reject the core integration and real-time-information benefits of ERP. Option 4 accepts integration but wrongly denies the shared current-information benefit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: ERP's value is enterprise-wide process and data integration; its major tradeoff is costly, complex implementation and organizational change.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: When interrupts are classified by their temporal relationship to the processor clock and instruction execution, the two broad classes are synchronous and asynchronous. A synchronous interrupt is caused by, and occurs at a defined point in, the execution of an instruction; division by zero is one example. The question asks for the interrupt class, not an example, so option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Maskable describes whether software or hardware can disable an interrupt, not its timing. Periodic describes how often an interrupt recurs. Division by zero is a cause of a synchronous exception, whereas the requested classification name is synchronous interrupt.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Classify interrupts along the dimension named in the stem: timing gives synchronous versus asynchronous; masking gives maskable versus non-maskable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Virtual memory lets a process use an address space larger than the currently available main memory, so large programs can run and more memory is addressable. The price is extra disk traffic when nonresident pages must be fetched, so more I/O can be required. It does not make process swapping inherently faster; disk transfers, page faults, and bookkeeping add overhead. Therefore the incorrect statement is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 3 are principal benefits of virtual memory. Option 2 states a real possible cost: demand paging may generate additional I/O. Option 4 reverses that performance tradeoff.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Virtual memory increases usable address space and flexibility, but page faults and backing-store transfers cost time rather than automatically speeding swapping.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The next-address generator computes the address of the next microinstruction from external conditions and the current microinstruction's next-address information. That address must first be latched in the control address register, which is block B. The register then selects a word from the control memory, block C; that word supplies control signals and feeds its sequencing information back to the next-address generator. Hence B and C are the control address register and control memory respectively.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A cache is not the defining store in a microprogrammed control unit, and a branch register is not the normal register between the sequencer and control store. Although a writable control store can physically use RAM, the functional block is specifically called control memory, making option 2 more precise than option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Microsequencer → control address register → control memory → control signals and next-address fields.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Implied addressing has no explicit operand field because the operand is fixed by the instruction definition, so a→iv. An immediate operand is contained in the instruction word and is therefore available with the instruction in the instruction register, matching the paper's wording b→iii. Register addressing names a CPU register that contains the operand, so c→i. Register-indirect addressing names a register that contains the operand's memory address, so d→ii. The code iv, iii, i, ii is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes confuse a register that holds an operand with one that holds an address, or treat an explicitly encoded immediate value as implied. Register and register-indirect addressing differ by exactly this extra level of indirection.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Immediate=value in instruction; register=value in named CPU register; register indirect=address in named register; implied=operand fixed by opcode.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The Intel 8085 was designed to operate from a single +5 V DC supply. The final digit distinguishes it from the older 8080 family design that required multiple supply voltages; it is not a clock-frequency label. Thus option 2 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A −5 V rail is not required, so options 1 and 3 fail. The 5 does not mean a 5 MHz clock; standard 8085 variants had clock specifications identified separately by part suffix and rating.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: 8085: an 8-bit Intel microprocessor built for a single +5 V power supply.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: LXI means 'load register pair immediate.' In `LXI rp, data16`, the two bytes following the opcode are copied into the selected register pair, such as BC, DE, HL, or SP. Therefore option 3 directly performs the requested operation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: `LDAX rp` loads the accumulator indirectly through BC or DE. `LHLD addr` loads L and H from two consecutive memory locations. `INX rp` increments a register pair; it does not load immediate data.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: In 8085 mnemonics, LXI = Load register pair eXtended/Immediate with a 16-bit constant.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Build a precedence graph from conflicting operations. In S1, `r1(Y)` occurs before `w2(Y)`, giving T1→T2, while `r2(X)` occurs before `w1(X)`, giving T2→T1. The cycle means S1 is not conflict serializable. In S2, both conflicts point T2→T1: `r2(X)` precedes `w1(X)` and `w2(Y)` precedes `r1(Y)`. Its graph is acyclic and is equivalent to the serial order T2 then T1. Therefore option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 incorrectly call S1 serializable despite its two-way cycle. Option 4 overlooks that S2 has only one edge direction and is therefore conflict serializable.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: A schedule is conflict serializable exactly when its transaction-precedence graph is acyclic.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A superkey is any attribute set that uniquely identifies a tuple. A candidate key is a minimal superkey, and the primary key is one candidate key selected as the relation's main identifier. Treating the terms as classes of allowable keys gives primary key ⊆ candidate key ⊆ superkey, which is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The reverse containments fail because many superkeys contain unnecessary attributes and are not candidate keys, while a relation can have candidate keys that were not selected as its primary key.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Every primary key is a candidate key, and every candidate key is a superkey; the converses need not hold.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Read the relationship as many R1 entities mapping to one R2 entity. Each R1 primary-key value identifies one R1 tuple and therefore determines the single related R2 primary-key value. Thus pk(R1) → pk(R2), which is option 2. Different R1 keys may determine the same R2 key, exactly expressing the many-to-one direction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: pk(R2) → pk(R1) would force each R2 entity to determine only one R1 entity, contradicting the 'many R1 to one R2' reading. The intersection expressions do not directly encode the cardinality between the entity identifiers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: For many-side R1 → one-side R2, the key of the many side functionally determines the key of the one side.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Because every domain contains atomic values, R satisfies first normal form. From A→C and B→D, neither A nor B alone determines all four attributes, but (AB)+ = {A,B,C,D}; hence AB is the candidate key. The non-prime attribute C depends on the proper subset A of AB, and D depends on the proper subset B. These partial dependencies violate second normal form. R is therefore in 1NF but not 2NF, so option 1 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 all require 2NF, which is impossible while non-prime attributes C and D depend on only parts of the composite key AB. Since 2NF already fails, claims about 3NF are unnecessary.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: For a composite key, 2NF forbids a non-prime attribute from depending on only a proper subset of that key.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: For each outer employee row T, the correlated subquery selects rows S having the same company name and computes that company's average salary. The outer predicate retains T when T.salary is greater than that company-specific average. Since there is no `MAX`, ordering, or limiting clause, every qualifying employee is returned, not only the highest paid. Option 4 precisely describes the result.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 incorrectly introduce 'highest paid,' which the query never computes. Options 2 and 3 use a single average across all companies, but the correlation `T.company_name = S.company_name` computes a separate average for each employee's company.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: A correlated subquery is reevaluated for the current outer row; here the correlation creates a per-company average.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: With K=3, an internal node has at most three child pointers and two separator keys, and the paper's diagrams use the high-key convention: each separator is the largest key in the child immediately to its left. Insert 8,5,1: the overflowing leaf splits into [1,5] and [8], with root key 5. After 7 and 3, the leaves become [1,3], [5], [7,8] and the root has separators 3,5. Inserting 12 splits [7,8,12] and forces the root to split, producing root 5 with two internal children. Inserting 9 makes the last leaf [9,12]; inserting 6 splits [6,7,8] into [6,7] and [8]. The final leaves are [1,3], [5], [6,7], [8], [9,12], with internal separators [3] and [7,8] under root [5]. This is Option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other diagrams do not match the stated high-key separator and split convention after the cascading split. A different textbook convention may copy the smallest key of the right child upward and draw another valid-looking shape, but among the paper's own diagrams and representation, Option 1 is the consistent result.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: In B+ tree questions, identify both the order and the separator convention before simulating leaf and internal-node splits.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: All three CRT statements are correct. Persistence is the time for emitted phosphor light to decay after excitation. The control grid/electrode controls electron-beam current and can blank the beam. The electron gun emits electrons and the focusing system forms them into a narrow beam aimed at the phosphor-coated screen. Therefore 'All of the above,' option 4, is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 each state only one true fact. Because none of those statements is false, the inclusive choice covering all three is required.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: CRT chain: electron gun creates the beam, control grid modulates it, focusing directs it, and phosphor persistence determines glow decay.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A GKS segment groups primitives so they can be transformed as one object. A segment transformation changes position by translation, orientation by rotation, and size by scaling. In viewing terms, translation supports panning and scaling supports zooming. Thus the most complete listed description is translation, rotation, panning and zooming, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 name only subsets of the supported effects. Option 3 includes scaling but pairs it with shearing; the standard GKS segment transformation is defined in terms of translation, scaling, and rotation rather than arbitrary shear. Option 4 covers those standard effects using both geometric and viewing terminology.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: GKS segment transformation changes a stored object's position, orientation, and size—translation/pan, rotation, and scaling/zoom.

sources.title: NIST FIPS 120-1 — Graphical Kernel System functional description

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Glass forms the transparent plates, contains the liquid crystal, and provides a bonding surface for the conductive layer, so a→i. The conductive coating acts as an electrode through which voltage is applied, so b→ii. The liquid-crystal material changes the polarization behavior of light under the electric field, so c→iii. Polarizing film is the transparent sheet that polarizes light, so d→iv. The sequence i, ii, iii, iv is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other codes interchange physical layers with incompatible jobs. Glass is the structural substrate, the conductive coating carries the applied voltage, the liquid crystal is the electro-optic medium, and the polarizer selects polarization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: LCD stack: glass substrate → transparent conductive electrode → liquid crystal → polarizing film, with each layer serving a distinct optical or electrical role.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Bresenham's circle algorithm begins at (0,r) with decision variable d=3−2r and generates one octant while x≤y. On every iteration x increases. When d≥0, the chosen pixel moves diagonally inward, so y must decrease: y=y−1, not y=y+1. The corresponding accumulated update is d←d+4(x−y)+10. Therefore step 4 contains the decisive wrong direction for y and is the incorrect step.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The initialization d=3−2r is standard, and stopping after x>y avoids repeating the symmetric octants. For d<0 the next point moves horizontally with x increasing and the decision value updated by 4x+6 (added to the old d). Option 4 instead moves y outward.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: While tracing the first octant of a circle from (0,r), x rises and y can only stay fixed or fall; it never rises.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Scan conversion maps continuous geometry onto a discrete pixel grid, so aliasing and its local or global forms can appear. A diagonal line can look dimmer than a horizontal line because its lit pixels are farther apart, causing unequal intensity. If two generated samples address the same photographic pixel, repeated exposure produces overstriking. Thus a, b, c, and d are all recognized scan-conversion side effects, giving option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one genuine artifact. Aliasing is the umbrella sampling distortion, local/global aliasing describe two manifestations, unequal intensity is an orientation-dependent brightness artifact, and overstriking is repeated writing or exposure of a pixel.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Rasterization can distort shape, spacing, brightness, and exposure because continuous primitives are forced onto discrete samples.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: For A=(0,0) and B=(8,4), Δx=8 and Δy=4. DDA takes steps=max(|8|,|4|)=8, so x increments by 8/8=1 and y by 4/8=0.5. The first four ideal samples are (0,0), (1,0.5), (2,1), and (3,1.5). Rounding each coordinate to the nearest raster pixel gives (0,0), (1,1), (2,1), and (3,2), which is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other sequences use y increments inconsistent with the line's slope 4/8=0.5. DDA must preserve this fractional accumulator even though the displayed pixel coordinates are rounded.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: DDA increments by (Δx/steps, Δy/steps) and rounds only for plotting; keep the accumulator fractional.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: A is regular because a two-state DFA can track whether the number of a's is even. B is not regular: a finite automaton cannot recognize exactly the unary prime lengths. C, the set of all palindromes over {a,b}, is not regular because recognizing an unbounded first half requires remembering it for comparison with the second half. D is also not regular because the gaps between successive square lengths grow without bound and violate the eventual periodicity of unary regular languages. Hence B, C, and D only are non-regular, which is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 wrongly include A, while option 4 omits the non-regular palindrome language C. Parity is finite-state; primality, arbitrary mirror equality, and perfect-square length are not.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Unary regular languages have eventually periodic accepted lengths; parity is periodic, but primes and squares are not.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The Kleene star always contains ε, even for the empty language, so L1*=∅*={ε}. Since L2={1}, L2*={1}*=1*. Concatenating with {ε} changes nothing: L2*L1*=1*{ε}=1*. Finally, {ε}∪1*=1* because ε is already in 1*. Therefore option 4 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 1 keeps only ε and loses the positive-length strings. Option 2 keeps only ε and 1, omitting 11, 111, and so on. Option 3 forgets that the star of even the empty language contains ε.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: ∅*={ε}; ε is the identity for language concatenation; and {1}*=1*.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Statement A is true by De Morgan's law: L∩M = complement(complement(L) ∪ complement(M)). Thus closure under union and complement supplies intersection. Statement B is false: closure under union and intersection alone does not create complements. For example, the class of finite languages over an infinite universe is closed under finite union and intersection, but the complement of a finite language is generally infinite and outside the class. Therefore option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 1 overlooks the De Morgan construction for A. Options 2 and 4 incorrectly treat complementation as a consequence of union and intersection without also assuming a universal language and an operation that can form relative differences.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Union + complement implies intersection by De Morgan; union + intersection does not by itself imply complement.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Every internal derivation-tree node has exactly K children. A tree of height h can have at most K^h leaves, so |W|≤K^h and h≥log_K|W|. In a full K-ary tree with I internal nodes and L leaves, L=(K−1)I+1. Here L=|W|, hence I=(|W|−1)/(K−1). A root-to-leaf path cannot contain more internal nodes than exist in the entire tree, so h≤(|W|−1)/(K−1). These are precisely the bounds in option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 propose logarithmic upper bounds. A highly unbalanced full K-ary derivation tree can have a path whose length is linear in its number of internal nodes, so no such logarithmic upper bound holds in general.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Maximum branching gives the logarithmic lower height bound; the full K-ary leaf/internal-node identity gives the linear upper bound.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: {a^n b^n | n≥0} is context-free. Removing the single string a^100b^100 preserves context-freeness—for example, intersect with the regular language that excludes exactly that string—so L1 is context-free. If L2 were context-free, then intersecting it with the regular language a*b*c* would also be context-free. That intersection is {a^n b^n c^n | n≥0}, a standard non-context-free language, giving a contradiction. Thus L2 is not context-free and option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 misclassify L1; deleting one exceptional string does not destroy its context-free structure. Option 2 misclassifies L2, whose three equal symbol counts can expose a^n b^n c^n through regular intersection.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Use closure with regular languages: CFL∩regular is CFL, which can both remove a finite exception and expose a known non-CFL contradiction.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Start with h(0)=k and h(1)=1. Then h(2)=2h(1)+4h(0)=2+4k. Next h(3)=2(2+4k)+4(1)=8+8k. Finally h(4)=2(8+8k)+4(2+4k)=24+32k. Setting this equal to 88 gives 32k=64, so k=2. Therefore option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Substituting k=0, 1, or −1 gives h(4)=24, 56, or −8, respectively, not 88. The recurrence must use both previous values at each step.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Expand the recurrence carefully from the two base cases; here h(4)=24+32k.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: Exactly one transmission requires choosing which one of the n stations transmits, probability factor n; that chosen station transmits with probability P; and each of the other n−1 stations remains silent with probability 1−P. Independence gives n·P·(1−P)^(n−1), which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: nP omits the requirement that all other stations be silent. P(1−P)^(n−1) describes success for one specified station but does not account for any of the n stations being the successful one. Option 1 uses the complemented probability for the selected station.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Binomial exactly-one probability: C(n,1)P(1−P)^(n−1).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The amount of data that can be in flight over one 40 ms round trip is bandwidth×RTT = 64,000 bit/s × 0.040 s = 2,560 bits. Each 32-byte packet carries 32×8=256 bits. The window needed to cover that bandwidth-delay product is 2,560/256=10 packets, so option 2 is correct under the question's stated RTT convention.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Windows 20, 30, and 40 packets represent two, three, and four times the calculated bandwidth-delay product. They are larger than needed merely to keep this bottleneck busy.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Optimal pipeline window in packets ≈ bandwidth-delay product ÷ packet size.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: For a binary CRC, evaluate an error polynomial E(x) at x=1. Any error affecting an odd number of bits has E(1)=1 in GF(2). If G(x) contains the factor x+1, then every multiple of G has value 0 at x=1, so no odd-weight error polynomial can be divisible by G; every odd number of bit errors is detected. The conventional answer is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Neither x nor 1+x² alone provides the general odd-weight guarantee. There is a flaw in the choices: over GF(2), subtraction equals addition, so 1−x=1+x and option 2 is algebraically identical to option 1. Also, a CRC detects such errors; it does not by itself correct them. Option 1 is the exam-intended notation, but the item has no mathematically unique choice.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: A CRC generator divisible by x+1 detects every error pattern with odd Hamming weight.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: The 48-byte message is divided among 24 packets, so each packet carries 48/24=2 bytes of message payload. Each packet also contains a 3-byte header. Therefore total packet size is 2+3=5 bytes, which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Option 1 gives only the payload size and ignores the header. Options 2 and 3 match neither the per-packet payload nor payload-plus-header total.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: Packet size = payload per packet + header = message size/packet count + header size.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: RSA encrypts and decrypts modulo n: C≡M^e (mod n) and M≡C^d (mod n), so statement I is correct. The exponents are chosen as multiplicative inverses modulo φ(n) in the formulation used here, so ed≡1 (mod φ(n)); statement III is correct. There is no general requirement ed≡1 (mod n), and the message operations are not performed modulo φ(n). Hence I and III are correct, giving option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Statement II uses the wrong modulus for the exponent-inverse condition. Statement IV uses φ(n) instead of n for encryption and decryption. Every option other than 2 includes one of those incorrect statements or omits a correct one.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.keyIdea: RSA data arithmetic is modulo n; the textbook key relation is ed≡1 modulo φ(n).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2017-jan-paper-3.pdf#.explanation: At full transmission rate, tokens are consumed at 10 Mb/s while new tokens arrive at 2 Mb/s.
