sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Running→Ready in (a) is preemption itself, commonly caused by a timer expiry or a higher-priority event. Waiting→Ready in (b) can make a newly ready higher-priority process preempt the current runner. Completion in (c) releases the CPU voluntarily and needs a scheduling decision but not preemption. Ready→Waiting in (d) is not a normal direct process transition; a process must be running before it can block. Thus only (a) and (b) identify preemptive circumstances, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 3 include the invalid Ready→Waiting transition. Option 4 includes process completion, where no running process is forcibly displaced. Preemption specifically means taking the CPU from a still-runnable process.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Preemptive scheduling matters when a running process is forced back to Ready or when a newly Ready process may displace it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: The total work satisfies T1(N)=4T1(N/2)+Θ(1), because all four subproblems must eventually execute; therefore T1(N)=Θ(N²). With unlimited processors, the four spawned calls at a level run concurrently, so only one recursive chain lies on the critical path: T∞(N)=T∞(N/2)+Θ(1)=Θ(log N). Parallelism is T1/T∞=Θ(N²/log N), option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 2 uses only linear work even though every one of the N² matrix entries is copied. Options 3 and 4 invert work and span, producing quantities that shrink toward zero; available parallelism cannot be less than one for this growing computation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Work counts every spawned branch; span follows only the longest dependent branch. Divide Θ(N²) by Θ(log N).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Only statement (b) is universally true. A dynamic program relies on a well-founded dependency order: every subproblem must be computable from already solved smaller subproblems. If the recurrence contains a cycle, that unmodified recurrence gives no such order, so it cannot directly define a correct bottom-up or memoized computation. Statement (a) is false because p counts subproblems but says nothing about the work per subproblem; the total is the sum of those costs, not always Θ(p). Statement (c) is false because bottom-up and memoized versions often have the same asymptotic complexity, while memoization may even avoid unreachable states. Statement (d) reverses a reduction: X≤pH shows that H is at least as hard as X, not that X is NP-hard. Therefore option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 3 include false claims about dynamic-programming running time. Option 4 includes the incorrectly directed NP-hardness argument. To prove X NP-hard, a known NP-hard problem H must reduce to X, written H≤pX.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Count both subproblems and work per subproblem, require an acyclic dependency order, and never reverse the direction of a hardness reduction.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Statements (a) and (b) are correct. Optical fibre is made from thin glass or plastic strands and is substantially lighter than comparable copper cabling. Its signal is light confined within the fibre rather than an electrical current in a conductor, so electromagnetic interference and ordinary electrical power surges do not corrupt the transmitted signal. Statement (c) is false: a basic optical link is directional—a transmitter launches light and a receiver detects it. Full-duplex or bidirectional communication must be engineered using two fibres, opposite-direction wavelengths, circulators, or other multiplexing arrangements; it is not an inherent property of a single optical transmission path. Thus option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 2, 3, and 4 all accept statement (c). They confuse a bidirectional fibre communication system with the direction of one transmitter-to-receiver optical channel.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Fibre is light, electrically nonconductive, and immune to EMI; bidirectionality requires link design rather than arising automatically.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Statements (a) and (c) are correct in the terminology used when these services were introduced. Windows Azure was described as Microsoft's cloud operating system and platform for running and managing applications in Microsoft data centres. Amazon CloudFront is a content-delivery-network service: it caches and serves content through geographically distributed edge locations. Statement (b) describes a consumer communication or social-services suite, not Google App Engine. App Engine is a platform-as-a-service environment in which developers deploy applications while Google manages much of the underlying infrastructure. Hence only (a) and (c) are correct, giving option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 incorrectly treat Google App Engine as a set of consumer sharing services. Option 3 rejects the historically correct description of Windows Azure as a cloud-based operating platform.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Distinguish cloud roles: Azure was presented as a cloud OS/platform, App Engine is PaaS, and CloudFront is a CDN.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: All three statements express core security services. Confidentiality prevents unauthorized disclosure, so the communicating parties can expect the message contents to remain private. Integrity assures that data has not been altered, inserted, deleted, or reordered without detection; in the question's wording, it arrives as sent. Authentication gives the receiver confidence about the claimed origin of the message—often called data-origin authentication. These properties answer different questions: who may read it, whether it changed, and who sent it. Since (a), (b), and (c) are all correct, the answer is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 each omit one valid security property. Confidentiality alone does not detect modification, integrity alone does not establish identity, and authentication alone does not keep content secret; all three definitions remain correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Confidentiality protects secrecy, integrity protects correctness, and authentication verifies origin or identity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: All three are standard difficulties of hill climbing. At a local maximum, every immediate neighbour looks worse even though a better state exists elsewhere, so the algorithm stops prematurely. On a plateau, many neighbours have the same evaluation, giving no preferred uphill direction and causing stagnation or an unproductive random walk. Along a ridge, improvement may require a sequence of moves or a diagonal move not represented by the available operators; individual local moves can appear flat or downhill, so progress becomes slow or impossible. Therefore (a), (b), and (c) are all shortcomings, which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 each exclude one genuine failure mode. Random restart can help with local maxima, sideways moves can help on plateaus, and richer operators or stochastic search can help traverse ridges, but none removes the fact that the condition is a limitation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Hill climbing sees only local improvement, making it vulnerable to local maxima, plateaus, and ridges.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Logistic regression is a classification method despite its name: it models a class probability, commonly with the logistic sigmoid, and converts that probability to a class using a threshold. Backpropagation is the learning procedure used to train multilayer neural networks; when the network's output represents class scores or probabilities, the trained network performs classification. Ordinary linear regression instead predicts a continuous numerical response by fitting a linear relationship. It is not, in its standard form, a classification algorithm. Consequently (a) and (b) only are the classification choices, so option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 2, 3, and 4 include linear regression as a classifier. Thresholding a regression output can be improvised, but that does not make standard linear regression a classification algorithm, and it lacks the probability constraints of logistic regression.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Logistic regression and classification neural networks predict classes; linear regression predicts continuous values.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Match each OSI layer by its defining responsibility. The physical layer transmits raw bits over the communication medium, so A-II. The transport layer provides process-to-process, end-to-end delivery from source to destination, so B-IV. The session layer establishes and controls dialogues and includes token management, so C-I. The presentation layer handles the representation, syntax, and semantics of exchanged information, including translation and encoding, so D-III. The complete mapping A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 1 swaps the session and presentation duties. Options 2 and 4 wrongly assign raw-bit transmission away from the physical layer and end-to-end delivery away from transport. Layer names should be tied to their scope: bits, end-to-end delivery, dialogue control, then representation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: OSI mnemonic by function: Physical=bits, Transport=end to end, Session=dialogue/token, Presentation=syntax and representation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: In ISO 9126, functionality concerns whether the software provides functions that meet stated needs—achievement of purpose—so A-II. Reliability is the capability to maintain a specified level of performance under stated conditions, so B-IV. Efficiency relates performance to the resources used, so C-I. Maintainability concerns the effort needed to analyse, modify, or improve the software, so D-III. This gives A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III, which is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 1 assigns the resource-performance relationship to functionality and improvement effort to efficiency. Option 2 swaps reliability and efficiency. Option 4 gives both functionality and reliability the wrong definitions. The noun in each definition—purpose, maintained performance, resources, improvement—points directly to the factor.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: ISO 9126: functionality meets needs, reliability sustains performance, efficiency balances performance and resources, maintainability measures modification effort.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Scrum maintains a sprint backlog containing the work selected and planned for the current sprint, so A-II. Adaptive Software Development organizes iterative work into time-boxed releases, so B-IV. Extreme Programming uses CRC—class, responsibility, collaborator—cards as a lightweight object-oriented design aid, so C-I. Feature-Driven Development expresses a feature using a short client-valued template such as ‘<action> the <result> <by/for/of/to> a(n) <object>,’ so D-III. The resulting mapping A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 move signature artifacts to the wrong methods. Sprint backlog is specifically Scrum vocabulary, CRC cards are associated with XP's simple collaborative design, and the action-result-object wording identifies an FDD feature.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Recognize agile methods by their artifacts: Scrum=sprint backlog, ASD=time boxes, XP=CRC cards, FDD=feature template.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: The legacy HTML table frame attribute controls which outer sides of a table's frame are rendered, giving A-IV. The <tr> element denotes and encloses a table row, giving B-III. The valign attribute specifies vertical alignment of content inside a table cell or row, giving C-II. The anchor element <a> creates a hyperlink, giving D-I. Therefore the correct mapping is A-IV, B-III, C-II, D-I—option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 4 misassign at least the frame or row role. Do not confuse frame, which selects outer table-border sides, with <tr>, which supplies row structure, or valign, which changes vertical placement rather than table structure.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: HTML roles: frame=outer border sides, <tr>=row, valign=vertical alignment, <a>=link.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: For direct addressing, the address field itself is the effective address, so A=400=III. For register-indirect addressing, the effective address is the content of Ri, so B=200=I. Indexed addressing adds the address field and index register: 400+200=600, so C=IV. In relative addressing, the displacement is added to the program counter after the two-word instruction has been fetched. With words at 500 and 501, the next-instruction PC is 502; hence 502+400=902, so D=II. The mapping A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 confuse an operand's field, a register's contents, and sums involving them. The common relative-addressing trap is adding 400 to 500 or 501; the PC points to 502 after fetching the complete instruction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Direct uses A, indirect uses (R), indexed uses A+(R), and PC-relative uses next-PC+A.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Isolated I/O has a separate I/O address space and special input/output instructions, so A-II. Memory-mapped I/O places device registers in the memory address space and uses the ordinary memory addressing and control framework, so B-I. An I/O interface bridges differences in speed, data representation, and control between the central computer and peripherals, so C-IV. With asynchronous transfer, independently timed units coordinate by exchanging control signals such as request and acknowledge, so D-III. Thus A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 1 gives memory-mapped I/O the separate-instruction property of isolated I/O and gives asynchronous transfer the memory-control property. Options 2 and 4 swap isolated and memory-mapped I/O. Their address-space distinction fixes the first two matches immediately.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Isolated I/O uses special instructions; memory-mapped I/O reuses memory operations; interfaces adapt devices; asynchronous units handshake.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: A microoperation is an elementary operation—transfer, shift, arithmetic, or logic—performed on data in registers, so A-IV. A microprogrammed control unit stores its control program in control memory, so B-III. Interrupts let the processor perform other useful work instead of continuously polling or waiting for a device, improving CPU utilization, so C-II. A microinstruction is a control word that specifies the microoperations to be activated in a microcycle, so D-I. Therefore A-IV, B-III, C-II, D-I is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 confuse an interrupt with a microinstruction or swap control memory with the elementary register operation. The pair microoperation/elementary operation and microinstruction/specification should be fixed first; the remaining two then follow directly.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Microoperations do the register-level work; microinstructions specify that work; a microprogrammed controller stores them in control memory.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Read the vertical activation rectangles, not merely the dashed object lifelines. Weather Data is active only while it performs summarize() and returns the result, so it has the shortest activation. Commslink remains active while it requests that summary and receives the response, making it longer. Weather Station is active across the larger get-summary interaction and the return of the report, so it is longer still. Sat Comms spans the entire report request and response exchange before acknowledgement, giving the longest activation among the four objects. The increasing order is therefore Weather Data → Commslink → Weather Station → Sat Comms, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 list roughly decreasing rather than increasing activation duration. Option 4 incorrectly places Weather Station before Commslink even though the station's activation encloses the Commslink interaction and is therefore longer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: In a sequence diagram, a nested callee's activation is shorter than the caller activation that surrounds the call-and-return interval.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: DES applies its round function f to the 32-bit right half and a 48-bit round key. First, the expansion permutation E expands the 32-bit half-block to 48 bits. Second, that result is XORed with the 48-bit round key. Third, eight S-boxes substitute the eight 6-bit groups with eight 4-bit outputs, returning the data to 32 bits. Fourth, the straight P permutation rearranges those 32 bits to spread each S-box's influence before the Feistel XOR. Hence the order is expansion → XOR → S-boxes → straight permutation, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 1 places substitution before key mixing, but S-box input must be the expanded data XORed with the round key. Options 3 and 4 begin with a 32-bit straight permutation and put the 32-to-48-bit expansion last, contradicting the required input sizes and DES structure.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: DES round-function mnemonic: E, key XOR, S, P—expand, mix, substitute, permute.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Count the circles in the flowgraph rather than the individual statement labels written inside them. The graphical nodes are 1, (2,3), 6, 7, 8, 9, (4,5), 10, and 11. The pairs (2,3) and (4,5) are each straight-line basic blocks drawn as one node, so neither pair contributes two separate flowgraph nodes. The total is therefore 9, which is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Ten, eleven, and twelve result from counting one or both labels inside a combined basic-block node separately or from confusing edges with nodes. A flowgraph node represents a maximal straight-line block and may contain several program statements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Count graphical basic-block circles, not the number of statement labels printed inside each circle.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: The flowgraph has three predicate nodes—1, (2,3), and 6—because each has two outgoing branches. For a connected structured flowgraph, cyclomatic complexity can be calculated as V(G)=P+1, where P is the number of predicate nodes. Thus V(G)=3+1=4. The edge-node formula confirms it: with E=11 edges and N=9 nodes, V(G)=E−N+2=11−9+2=4. Therefore option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Two or three undercount the independent decisions, while five adds a nonexistent predicate. A merge node such as 9 or 10 can have several incoming edges but is not a predicate unless it has more than one outgoing edge.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: For one connected flowgraph, V=E−N+2=P+1; predicate status depends on outgoing branches.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: A planar connected flowgraph has as many regions as its cyclomatic complexity when the unbounded outside area is included. From the graph, E=11 and N=9, so V(G)=E−N+2=4. Equivalently, the graph has three predicate nodes and hence P+1=4 regions. Visually these correspond to the bounded areas created by the branches and loop together with the exterior region. Therefore there are 4 regions, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Counts of two or three usually omit the exterior region or one branch-created region. Five treats a merge as if it opened another region. Region counting and cyclomatic complexity must agree for this connected planar flowgraph.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Include the outside region: for a connected planar flowgraph, number of regions = cyclomatic complexity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: A longest basic independent path may traverse the loop once before taking the exit. One such path is 1 → (2,3) → 6 → 7 → 9 → 10 → 1 → 11; replacing 7 by 8 gives another of the same length. Counting node occurrences along the path gives eight: the entry node 1 appears once before the loop body and once after the back edge returns. The alternative branch through (4,5) is shorter. Hence the longest independent path contains 8 node occurrences, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Six omits part of the longest branch or the loop return, seven commonly counts the repeated entry node only once, and nine requires adding a node that cannot occur on the same independent route without traversing mutually exclusive branches or repeating the loop.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Trace a complete entry-to-exit basic path; when a back edge returns to a node, count that second occurrence in the path length.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: A predicate node is a decision point with more than one outgoing edge. Node 1 branches either to the body node (2,3) or to exit 11. Node (2,3) branches to node 6 or node (4,5). Node 6 branches to node 7 or node 8. Every other node has exactly one outgoing edge, even if it has multiple incoming edges. Therefore the three predicate nodes are 1, (2,3), and 6, giving option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 2 names 4, which is only a statement label within node (4,5), not a branching node. Options 3 and 4 treat merge or exit nodes such as 9, 10, or 11 as predicates; multiple incoming edges do not make a decision node.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Predicate node = outdegree greater than one, not indegree greater than one.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Start by splitting the multiattribute right side A→BC into A→B and A→C. This gives A→B, A→C, D→E, BC→D, and A→D. The dependency A→D is redundant: from A→B and A→C we obtain A→BC, and BC→D then yields A→D transitively. Remove it. Neither B nor C is extraneous in BC→D because B alone and C alone cannot determine D under the remaining dependencies. The minimal cover is therefore {A→B, A→C, BC→D, D→E}, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 2 wrongly replaces BC→D by B→D and also keeps a nonsingleton right side. Option 3 drops the necessary BC→D, so it is not equivalent to F. Option 4 asserts both B→D and C→D, neither of which follows from the original set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Minimal cover: split right sides, remove extraneous left-side attributes, then remove dependencies implied by the rest.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Compute A's closure. From A→BC, add B and C. Because B and C are now both present, BC→D adds D. Finally D→E adds E. Thus A+={A,B,C,D,E}, so A is a superkey. It is also minimal because it contains only one attribute; therefore A is a candidate key and may be chosen as the primary key. Option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: BC+={B,C,D,E}, which lacks A, so BC is not a key. AD and AB both determine all attributes because they contain A, but each is a nonminimal superkey: removing D or B leaves A, which is already a key.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: A candidate key is a minimal determinant of all attributes; use attribute closure, then test minimality.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: All attributes are atomic, so R is in 1NF. Its only candidate key is the single attribute A. Because a one-attribute key has no proper nonempty subset, no nonprime attribute can be partially dependent on part of the key; therefore R is in 2NF. It is not in 3NF: dependencies BC→D and D→E have determinants that are not superkeys, while D and E are nonprime attributes. In particular A→D→E is a transitive dependency from the key to E. Hence the highest normal form is 2NF, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Option 1 understates the relation because a single-attribute key rules out partial dependency. Options 3 and 4 fail on D→E (and BC→D): the determinants are not superkeys and the right-side attributes are not prime.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Single-attribute keys make 2NF automatic; 3NF still fails when a nonkey determinant determines a nonprime attribute.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: Test A→D after temporarily removing it. Starting with A, dependency A→BC supplies B and C; then BC→D supplies D. Therefore D is already in A+ under the other dependencies, which proves that A→D is implied by them and is redundant. The remaining dependencies are needed to obtain B and C from A, D from BC, and E from D. Hence option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: Without BC→D, the remaining set cannot derive D from BC. Without D→E, no dependency supplies E. Without A→BC, A cannot derive B or C. Only A→D can be reconstructed transitively as A→BC and BC→D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: An FD X→Y is redundant exactly when Y⊆X+ computed using all the other dependencies.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.explanation: In R1(A,B,C), A determines B and C, so A is a key and every nontrivial projected dependency has a superkey determinant. R1 is therefore in BCNF, and consequently also in 3NF. In R2(A,D,E), A is the key and D→E creates no partial dependency because the key has one attribute, so R2 is in 2NF. But D is not a superkey and E is nonprime, so D→E violates 3NF; 2NF is R2's highest normal form. Thus option 4 gives the precise highest-normal-form description.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.whyOthersFail: The decomposition does not preserve BC→D: under the union of the projected dependencies, (BC)+ remains BC, so option 1 is false. Option 3 reverses the normal forms. Option 2 is only loosely true in the hierarchy because BCNF implies 2NF; the exam's mutually exclusive wording uses ‘in 2NF’ to mean highest normal form, under which R1 is BCNF/3NF and option 4 is the intended precise answer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2019-dec-combined-pap.keyIdea: Assess each component using projected FDs and report its highest normal form; also test preservation by closure under the union of projections.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: There are 50 odd positive integers from 1 through 100. The positive squares not exceeding 100 are 1² through 10², so there are 10 squares. Five of those squares are already odd—1, 9, 25, 49, and 81—because they come from odd bases. By inclusion–exclusion, the union contains 50+10−5=55 integers. Therefore option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Fifty counts only the odd integers. Adding all ten squares without subtracting the five odd squares gives 60, which is not even listed. Values 59 and 63 arise from an incorrect overlap count; an integer cannot be counted twice in the union.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For ‘odd or square,’ use |O∪S|=|O|+|S|−|O∩S|; an odd square has an odd base.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Because both the books and boxes are identical, an arrangement is determined only by the unordered positive occupancies of the nonempty boxes—an integer partition of 6 into at most four parts. List them: 6; 5+1; 4+2; 4+1+1; 3+3; 3+2+1; 3+1+1+1; 2+2+2; and 2+2+1+1. Empty boxes may fill the remaining positions, but identical empty boxes create no new arrangement. There are 9 ways, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Stars-and-bars would overcount because it treats boxes as distinguishable. Counts 4, 6, and 7 omit valid partitions. The capacity of six imposes no additional restriction because only six books exist.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Identical objects in identical boxes are counted by integer partitions, here partitions of 6 with at most four parts.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Pairs 1–3 are equivalences. For pair 1, both expressions simplify to (¬p∨r)∧(¬q∨r). Pair 2 follows because negating both sides preserves a biconditional. Pair 3 is the standard expansion of p↔q: both true or both false. Pair 4 is not equivalent. Let p=true, q=false, r=false. Then (p∧q)→r is true because its antecedent is false, but (p→r)∧(q→r) is false∧true=false. A single counterexample disproves equivalence, so option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 can each be transformed into the same formula on both sides using implication and biconditional laws. The invalid distribution in option 4 confuses implication from a conjunction with a conjunction of two implications.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: To disprove logical equivalence, find one valuation with different truth values; p=T,q=F,r=F separates pair 4.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A linear objective reaches its optimum at a feasible corner. The axis corners are (0,0), (2,0), and (0,2.5). The two constraint lines intersect where 2x1+x2=4 and x1+2x2=5. Solving gives x1=1 and x2=2. Evaluate z=2x1+3x2: the four values are 0, 4, 7.5, and 2+6=8. The maximum is 8 at (1,2), hence option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Values 23 and 13 do not result from any feasible corner. The value 9.5 would exceed the objective at every vertex and is therefore impossible. Checking the intersection alone is not enough in general, but here its value also exceeds both axis corners.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For a two-variable LP, enumerate feasible vertices and evaluate the objective at each.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Byte-addressable memory of 2^16 bytes needs a 16-bit address. Each cache block holds 8=2^3 bytes, so the lowest 3 bits select a byte within the block. A direct-mapped cache with 32=2^5 lines needs 5 index bits. The remaining high bits form the tag: 16−3−5=8. Thus tag, line, and offset widths are 8,5,3, which is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Every other option either allocates the wrong number of index/offset bits or fails to total 16 correctly. Six line bits would imply 64 cache lines, and a two- or four-bit offset would imply 4- or 16-byte blocks.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Address = tag | line index | block offset; widths come from log2(lines) and log2(bytes per block).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Trace the program in hexadecimal. CLA sets AC=0000H. ADD 217 gives AC=1234H, and INC makes it 1235H. STA 217 therefore changes M[217] to 1235H. LDA 218 loads 9CE2H. CMA takes the 16-bit one's complement, producing 631DH. Finally AND 217 computes 631DH AND 1235H nibble by nibble: 6&1=0, 3&2=2, 1&3=1, D&5=5. The result is 0215H, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other values result from forgetting that STA changes location 217 before the final AND, complementing the wrong operand, or performing arithmetic rather than bitwise AND. CMA flips every one of the 16 bits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Program tracing requires updating memory after STA, then applying 16-bit complement and bitwise AND exactly.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The non-pipelined time for 500 tasks is 500×50=25,000 ns. A k-stage pipeline completes n tasks in k+n−1 clock cycles, including fill time. Here the time is (6+500−1)×10=5,050 ns. Speedup=25,000/5,050≈4.9505, so the closest option is 4.95, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Six is the stage count, not the speedup; the original system takes only 50 ns while the pipeline's steady interval is 10 ns, so the limiting speedup is 5, not 6. Values 5.7 and 5.5 exceed that physical upper limit.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Finite pipeline time is (stages+tasks−1)×cycle time; compare it with tasks×nonpipeline time.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Interpret the numerals in base b: (10)_b=b and (26)_b=2b+6. For a monic quadratic with roots 4 and 7, Vieta's formulas give root sum 4+7=11 and root product 4×7=28. The coefficient of x equals the root sum, so b=11. The product check also works: 2b+6=22+6=28. Therefore the radix is 11, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Bases 8, 9, and 10 make the numeral (10)_b equal 8, 9, or 10, none matching the required root sum 11. Substitution into the constant term gives the same rejection.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Convert base-b coefficients to values, then apply Vieta: sum=b and product=2b+6.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Initially p=1=a^0 and x=0. Each iteration multiplies p by a and increments x, so after x iterations the invariant is p=a^x. The loop stops at the first integer x for which p≥b, that is, the smallest x satisfying a^x≥b. Taking base-a logarithms gives x≥log_a b, and the smallest integer meeting this inequality is ⌈log_a b⌉. Thus option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: a^b and b^a describe huge values unrelated to the loop counter. The floor is wrong whenever b lies strictly between consecutive powers of a; another multiplication is then required. Ceiling also handles the exact-power case correctly.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Establish p=a^x as a loop invariant; termination asks for the smallest integer exponent reaching b.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The HTML <map> element defines an image map. It is associated with an image through the image's usemap attribute and contains <area> elements whose shapes and coordinates identify clickable regions linked to different destinations. Therefore it is used to define clickable regions in an image, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: It does not describe graph paths between image nodes, visually highlight arbitrary areas, or create a website navigation sitemap. The name ‘map’ refers specifically to mapping portions of one image to links.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: <map> groups <area> hotspots for a client-side clickable image map.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Lay out one structure by offsets. short s occupies 0–1; char c uses 2; one padding byte moves short t to 4–5; char d uses 6. Seven must be padded so double r begins at offset 8 and occupies 8–15. int i occupies 16–19. Because each array element must preserve 8-byte alignment for its double, the structure receives four tail-padding bytes and has size 24. Ten elements consume 10×24=240 bytes, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Two hundred ignores tail/internal padding; 150 is merely the unpadded field-size total times ten. Three hundred twenty overpads each structure to 32 bytes even though the next multiple of the maximum 8-byte alignment after offset 20 is 24.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Align each field, then round total struct size up to the largest member alignment so every array element is valid.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Ignoring the distant integer-overflow stopping point, each recursive level contributes an alternating geometric term. At depth k the ratio is (2^k)/(3·3^k)=(1/3)(2/3)^k, and subtraction alternates signs. Thus f(1,3)≈(1/3)[1−2/3+(2/3)^2−…]. For |−2/3|<1, the sum is (1/3)/(1+2/3)=1/5=0.2. Java long overflow eventually makes an argument negative and returns 0, truncating the series far out; the requested best approximation remains 0.2, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other choices are not the sum of the alternating geometric series. Treating every recursive term as positive would give 1 instead, while stopping after only one or two terms gives a poor approximation and ignores the repeated recursion.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Translate recursive subtraction into an alternating geometric series with first term 1/3 and ratio −2/3.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In HDFS, a DataNode stores actual data blocks, serves client read/write requests, and reports block information to the NameNode; it is a worker node. The NameNode maintains filesystem metadata such as the namespace and block-to-file mapping and coordinates DataNodes; it is the master node in the classic HDFS architecture. Hence DataNode and NameNode are worker and master, respectively—option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 2 reverses the roles. Options 3 and 4 incorrectly place both components at the same level. Storing data blocks and managing metadata are deliberately separated between workers and the master.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: HDFS: NameNode manages metadata; DataNodes store and serve blocks.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Y and Z never appear on any dependency's right side, so every candidate key must contain both. Add one attribute that enters the U–V–X cycle. For UYZ: U→V, Y→W, and VW→X, giving every attribute. For VYZ: Y→W, then VW→X and X→U. For XYZ: X→U→V and Y→W. Each is minimal because removing Y or Z loses an underivable attribute, and removing the third attribute cannot generate U,V, or X. Thus UYZ, VYZ, and XYZ are the candidate keys—option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 omit mandatory Z. Option 3 includes VWZ, which cannot derive Y because Y never appears on a right side. It also omits the valid key XYZ.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Attributes absent from all FD right sides must be in every key; then use closures to find minimal additions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Trace three frames through A,B,C,D,A,B,E,A,B,C,D,E,B,A,B. FIFO faults on A,B,C,D,A,B,E,C,D,B,A and hits the other four references, giving P=11. LRU faults on A,B,C,D,A,B,E,C,D,E,B,A; only the second A, second B, and final B are hits, giving Q=12. Therefore (P,Q)=(11,12), option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The alternatives mis-handle hits when updating recency or incorrectly move a FIFO page to the back on a hit. FIFO replacement order changes only on a fault/insertion; LRU order changes on every reference.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Maintain separate state rules: FIFO evicts earliest loaded, whereas LRU evicts least recently referenced.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: SSTF always serves the pending request nearest the current head. The request at 20 is served immediately with zero movement. The remaining service order is 22, 10, 6, 2, 38, 40. The movements are |22−20|=2, |10−22|=12, |6−10|=4, |2−6|=4, |38−2|=36, and |40−38|=2, totalling 60 cylinders. At 2 ms per cylinder, the time is 60×2=120 ms, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other values come from preserving arrival order, skipping the zero-distance request incorrectly, or choosing a nonnearest cylinder at some step. SSTF must be reevaluated after every service because the head position changes.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For SSTF, repeatedly select the smallest absolute distance from the current head and sum all head movements.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A 32-bit address occupies 4 bytes, so one 2 KiB indirect block holds 2048/4=512 addresses. The inode reaches 12 direct data blocks, 512 blocks through the single-indirect pointer, and 512² blocks through the double-indirect pointer. Total data capacity is (12+512+262,144)×2048 bytes = 525,336 KiB, approximately 513 MiB. Thus option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The kilobyte answers ignore the double-indirect level. About 537 MB results from decimal/binary unit confusion or an incorrect pointer count. Metadata blocks store addresses and are not themselves counted as file-data capacity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Pointers per indirect block = block size/address size; double indirection contributes that number squared.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A TLB hit costs one TLB lookup plus one memory access: 15+150=165 ns. A miss costs the lookup, one access to read the page-table entry, and one access to the desired word: 15+150+150=315 ns. Weight these by the 0.8 hit and 0.2 miss probabilities: EAT=0.8×165+0.2×315=132+63=195 ns. Therefore option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Values 175 and 185 undercount the miss penalty. Two hundred five does not match the stated probabilities. Because the page table is in memory, every TLB miss needs two main-memory accesses, not one.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: EAT = hit ratio×(TLB+memory) + miss ratio×(TLB+page-table memory+data memory).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Among the listed choices, a use-case diagram gives the static use-case view: it records actors, use cases, and their relationships without ordering interactions in time. A collaboration diagram is an interaction view showing message exchange among objects. State-chart and activity diagrams both model changing behaviour—state transitions and workflow/control flow, respectively. Hence the static-view choice is the use-case diagram, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Collaboration, state-chart, and activity diagrams all emphasize runtime behaviour or interaction. Although use cases describe system behaviour at a requirements level, the diagram itself is a static structural snapshot of actors and use-case relationships in this classification.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Static diagrams show enduring elements and relations; dynamic diagrams show messages, transitions, or activity flow.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Restructuring changes internal organization to make software easier and safer to maintain without primarily correcting a reported fault or adapting to a new environment. It is preventive maintenance: improving code or data structure now reduces the likelihood and cost of future failures and changes. Therefore option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Corrective maintenance fixes discovered defects. Adaptive maintenance responds to environmental changes such as a new platform. Perfective maintenance adds or improves user-visible capability or performance. Restructuring for future maintainability best matches prevention.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Preventive maintenance improves internal maintainability before a failure; restructuring is its classic technique.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Let t be L1's size in thousands of LOC. L2 then has 3t thousand LOC. L1 development costs ₹70,000t and ten-year maintenance costs ₹10,00,000, so C1=70,000t+10,00,000. L2 costs ₹90,000×3t for development and ₹4,00,000 for maintenance, so C2=2,70,000t+4,00,000. Equating gives 6,00,000=2,00,000t, hence t=3. Thus L1 has 3,000 LOC, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other LOC values do not balance the larger L2 development cost against its smaller maintenance cost. The factor of three applies to L2's LOC before its per-man-year development rate is used.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Build total lifecycle cost for each language: development effort×rate plus ten years of maintenance.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: At 12 function points per person-month, 864 FP requires 864/12=72 person-months. With six people working together, duration is 72/6=12 months. Monthly team salary is ₹50,000+₹70,000+2×₹60,000+2×₹60,000=₹3,60,000. Multiplying by 12 months gives ₹43,20,000. Therefore option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other totals omit a role, use the productivity as team-month productivity rather than person-month productivity, or multiply by the wrong duration. The salary mix matters even though all six contribute to the person-month estimate.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Effort=FP/productivity; duration=effort/team size; cost=duration×sum of monthly salaries.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A full n-ary tree with I internal nodes has nI edges because every internal node contributes n child edges. It has I+L total vertices, and every tree has one fewer edge than vertices: nI=I+L−1. Rearranging gives L=(n−1)I+1. Substitute L=41 and I=10: 41=10(n−1)+1, so 40=10(n−1), n−1=4, and n=5. Hence option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Substituting n=3,4,6 would produce 21,31,51 leaves, respectively. The relation is not L=nI because one incoming edge links each nonroot vertex.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Full n-ary tree identity: L=(n−1)I+1, derived by equating nI edges with I+L−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A max-heap guarantees only that each parent is at least as large as its children. Therefore the minimum cannot be an internal node with children; it must be among the leaves. A binary heap has about n/2 leaves, and their internal order is unconstrained, so finding the smallest requires scanning all of them in the worst case. This takes Θ(n), represented by O(n) in option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: O(1) applies to finding the maximum at the root, not the minimum. Heap height O(log n) helps restore heap order along one path, but does not identify which of Θ(n) leaves is smallest. The double-log bound has no supporting property.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: In a max-heap, maximum is at the root; minimum may be any leaf, so scan Θ(n) leaf candidates.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: One second equals 10^6 microseconds. For A, require log₂n≤10^6, giving n≤2^(10^6). For B, require √n≤10^6; squaring yields n≤(10^6)²=10^12. Thus the largest sizes are 2^(10^6) and 10^12, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: 10^6 for B forgets to square after solving √n=10^6. Factors such as 6×10^6 or 6×10^12 incorrectly mix the number of microseconds with the exponent notation. A's logarithm must be inverted with a power of two.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Convert the time budget to microseconds, set each running time equal to it, and invert the function.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A seven-edge path is 1→3→9→10→11→33→99→100: four ×3 moves and three +1 moves. It corresponds to building the base-3 representation 100=(10201)₃ from left to right. Reversing confirms the count: 100→99 by −1, →33 by ÷3, →11 by ÷3, →10 by −1, →9 by −1, →3 by ÷3, →1 by ÷3. Each reverse step undoes one allowed edge, giving 7 steps. Therefore option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Ninety-nine uses only +1 moves. Twenty-three and four do not match the optimal combination of multiplication and increments; four ×3 moves alone produce powers of three and cannot reach 100 without the three required increments encoded by its base-3 digits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For operations ×3 and +1, base-3 digits expose an efficient path; reverse with ÷3 and −1 to verify it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Every L1 string has form 0^m1^m2 0^n1^n. An L2 string must have all 0s first, then all 1s, then all 2s. After L1's single 2, L2 permits no later 0 or 1, so intersection membership forces n=0. Consequently L={0^m1^m2 | m≥0}. This language is context-free—for example S→T2 and T→0T1|ε—but it is not regular because it must match equal numbers of leading 0s and 1s. Hence option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The language is decidable and context-free, ruling out options 1 and 4. It is not regular: removing the fixed final 2 by right quotient or a pumping-lemma argument leaves the classic nonregular language {0^m1^m}.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: First simplify the intersection using symbol order; it collapses to {0^m1^m2}, a CFL with one equality constraint.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: L2=(a+b)* is all strings over {a,b}. In L1=(a*+b)*, each repeated unit is either a string of zero or more a's or the single symbol b. Any word over {a,b} can be split into individual symbols: choose a from a* for each a and choose b for each b. Conversely, L1 can generate no symbols outside {a,b}. Hence both languages equal {a,b}*, so L1=L2, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Neither language is a proper subset of the other, and their intersection is certainly not empty—it contains ε and every binary word. The ε alternative inside a* does not restrict the language.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Show two inclusions: every L1 token is over {a,b}, and every {a,b} word can be tokenized using a from a* or b.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Context-free languages are closed under union: given grammars for L1 and L2, add a fresh start symbol S with productions S→S1|S2. They are not closed in general under intersection or complement. They are also accepted in general by nondeterministic pushdown automata; deterministic CFLs form a proper subclass, so not every CFL has a DPDA. Therefore only statement 1 is true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: If CFLs were closed under complement as well as union, De Morgan's law would imply closure under intersection, which is known to fail. Statement 4 confuses NPDA power with the strictly weaker DPDA class.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: CFL closure mnemonic: union, concatenation, and star—yes; arbitrary intersection and complement—no; NPDA, not always DPDA.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Standard broad forms of intermediate representation include syntax trees, postfix notation, and three-address code. A quadruple is not a separate code form in that classification; it is one concrete record layout for storing three-address instructions, with fields such as (operator, argument1, argument2, result). Therefore the item that is not itself an intermediate-code form is quadruples, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Syntax trees represent hierarchical expression structure, postfix notation gives a linear stack-oriented form, and three-address code gives an explicit low-level statement sequence. Quadruples, triples, and indirect triples are implementation representations of the third form.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Distinguish an IR form from its storage layout: quadruples encode three-address code rather than defining a fourth form.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Minimum-distance decoding chooses the valid codeword with the fewest bit disagreements with the received word r=0000000111. Compare r with the four candidates: d(r,0000000000)=3, d(r,0000011111)=2, d(r,1111100000)=8, and d(r,1111111111)=7. The unique nearest codeword is therefore 0000011111, option 1. The stated minimum distance 5 also means the code can correct up to floor((5−1)/2)=2 errors, exactly the distance found here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: 0000000000 is three bits away, so it is not the nearest valid word. The two codewords beginning with five 1s are seven or eight bits away. Decoding is based on distance from the received word, not merely on sharing a long prefix.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For a block code, compute the Hamming distance to every valid codeword and select the unique minimum.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: RSA uses n=pq=3×11=33 and φ(n)=(p−1)(q−1)=2×10=20. The public exponent e must satisfy ed≡1 (mod 20). With d=7, e=3 works because 3×7=21≡1 (mod 20). Encryption of m=19 is c=m^e mod n=19³ mod 33. Since 19²=361≡31 (mod 33), c≡31×19=589≡28 (mod 33). Thus (e,c)=(3,28), option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: 20 is φ(n), not the public exponent; 33 is the modulus n, not e. The value 77 does not satisfy the standard choice 1<e<φ(n) and is merely congruent to 17 modulo 20, whose product with 7 is not 1 modulo 20.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Find e as the modular inverse of d modulo φ(n), then compute c=m^e mod n.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In stop-and-wait operation, the sender transmits exactly one frame and pauses until it receives the acknowledgement for that frame. Only after the ACK arrives does it send the next frame. Under the terminology used in the question, this is the simplex stop-and-wait protocol, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: An unrestricted simplex protocol assumes an ideal channel and does not require this per-frame waiting behavior. ‘Simplex protocol’ alone is too broad, while ‘restricted simplex protocol’ is not the standard name for the described acknowledgement-controlled scheme.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: One outstanding frame followed by an ACK wait is the defining pattern of stop-and-wait.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Frequency is the reciprocal of period: f=1/T. Convert 100 ms to seconds: T=100×10⁻³=0.1 s. Hence f=1/0.1=10 Hz. Because 1 kHz=1000 Hz, 10 Hz=10/1000=0.01 kHz=10⁻² kHz. Therefore option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: 10 kHz would mean 10,000 Hz and a period of 0.1 ms. The values 10⁻¹ and 10⁻³ kHz correspond to 100 Hz and 1 Hz respectively, whose periods are 10 ms and 1 s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use f=1/T, convert milliseconds to seconds first, and convert hertz to kilohertz last.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Abstraction deliberately suppresses details that are irrelevant to the current reasoning task while retaining the features needed to solve it. In state-space problem solving, this produces a simpler representation, often reducing the number of distinctions and transitions the search must consider. Thus the described process is abstraction, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Extraction retrieves selected information, mining discovers patterns in data, and selection chooses among existing items. None of these terms specifically denotes constructing a less detailed state representation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Abstraction means retaining essential structure while hiding problem details that do not affect the solution.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: AI problem solving is commonly characterized by search through a state space, frequent use of heuristics, and reasoning with incomplete or imprecise knowledge. The route to a solution may emerge through search rather than being listed in advance as a fixed sequence of explicit steps. A generic ‘repetition mechanism,’ however, is an ordinary control feature of conventional algorithms and is not a distinguishing characteristic of AI problem solving. Hence statement 4 is the one that is NOT true in this classification.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Heuristic search is central to many AI solvers. Non-explicit solution paths describe search procedures that discover a path at run time, and imprecise knowledge is routinely handled by AI representations and inference methods.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Separate AI search and knowledge characteristics from generic programming control structures such as repetition.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The word ‘some’ introduces an existential quantifier: there is at least one object x. That same object must satisfy both conditions: it is my friend, f(x), and it is not perfect, ¬p(x). Joining the conditions with conjunction gives ∃x(f(x)∧¬p(x)), option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: A universal quantifier would claim every object is a non-perfect friend. Negating the existential says no friend is imperfect, the opposite of the sentence. Using ¬f(x) describes someone who is not a friend and therefore fails the required meaning.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Translate ‘some A are not B’ as ∃x(A(x)∧¬B(x)).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Conjunctive normal form is a conjunction of clauses, and each clause is a disjunction of literals. A literal is either an atomic proposition or its negation. For example, (p∨¬q)∧(r∨s∨¬t) is in CNF: the parenthesized groups are disjunctions of literals. Therefore option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Variables alone exclude negated occurrences and are too narrow. A conjunction of literals may be one special CNF formula, but it is not the general structure of each CNF clause; conjunction connects the clauses to one another.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: CNF has AND between clauses and OR inside each clause; the clause elements are literals.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: R is not reflexive because a reflexive relation on A must contain (x,x) for every x∈A, but (c,c) and (d,d) are missing. It is antisymmetric: whenever two different elements x and y occur as (x,y), the reverse pair (y,x) does not occur; the off-diagonal pairs are (c,d), (c,g), and (d,g). It is not symmetric because, for example, (c,d)∈R but (d,c)∉R. Thus B holds and A does not, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Only A and both A-and-B incorrectly call the relation reflexive. Only C incorrectly calls it symmetric. Self-loops do not violate antisymmetry, because the condition permits x=y.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Test relation properties by definition: all self-loops for reflexivity, reverse pairs for symmetry, and no two-way off-diagonal pair for antisymmetry.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Step B is valid: universal instantiation gives P(c)∨Q(c). The error occurs next. Simplification permits deriving either conjunct from P(c)∧Q(c), but it cannot derive either disjunct from P(c)∨Q(c). The disjunction may be true because only P(c) is true or only Q(c) is true. Therefore both C and E are invalid inferences, making option 2 correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The argument is invalid: a one-element model with P true and Q false satisfies ∀x(P∨Q) but not ∀xQ. Universal generalization and the final conjunction are not the first faulty rules; their premises were obtained illegitimately at C and E.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: From P∨Q one may not infer P or infer Q separately; simplification applies only to a conjunction.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A symbol table stores attributes associated with names, such as declared type, scope, memory location or offset, and diagnostic state. These entries support storage allocation (A), permit semantic analysis to check type compatibility (B), and allow the compiler to remember that an error for a name has already been reported so repeated diagnostics can be suppressed (C). All three are applications, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Each of options 1–3 omits one valid use. A symbol table is shared infrastructure used by semantic analysis, code generation/storage layout, and error reporting—not merely a dictionary of identifier spellings.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Think of a symbol-table entry as the compiler’s central record for a name’s semantic, storage, and diagnostic attributes.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The common terms A'B' and AB cover minterms {0,1,2,3} and {12,13,14,15}. The only uncovered required minterm is 6=0110. In expression B, A'CD' covers minterms 2 and 6, both in F, so B is valid. In expression D, BCD' covers 6 and 14, again both in F, so D is valid. Thus B and D are equivalent simplified covers of F, giving option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Expression A adds A'C'D', which covers minterms 0 and 4; minterm 4 is not in F, and minterm 6 remains uncovered. Expression C adds BC'D', which likewise includes the unwanted minterm 4 and does not cover 6. Therefore A and C are invalid.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Check each added implicant: it must cover the missing minterm 6 without introducing any zero-minterm.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: For n tasks in an ideal balanced k-stage pipeline with stage time t, sequential time is nkt while pipeline time is (k+n−1)t. Hence speedup S=nk/(k+n−1). Its limit as n grows is k, so A is true. For every finite n and k>1, k+n−1>n, which makes S<k; exact maximum speedup is not attained, so B is true. Pipeline stages need not inherently have equal computation delays—the clock is set by the slowest stage and balancing is a design objective—so C is false. Therefore option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options containing C treat an ideal simplifying assumption as a necessary property of every pipeline. Different stage delays are possible; they simply create idle slack in faster stages and reduce efficiency.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: The ideal speedup k is an asymptotic upper bound; equal stage times improve utilization but are not required by the definition of pipelining.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A tightly coupled multiprocessor contains multiple processors that share system resources and are managed as one computer by a single operating system, so A is true. The processors can execute different instruction streams on different data, placing a general multiprocessor in Flynn’s MIMD class, so C is true. Statement B instead describes a loosely coupled multicomputer or distributed system whose independent computers communicate over links. Thus A and C only, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: B confuses processors inside one shared system with autonomous computers joined by a communication network. Any option containing B is therefore wrong, while option 1 omits the valid MIMD classification.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Multiprocessor means tightly coupled processors under one OS; networked independent computers form a multicomputer/distributed system.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: XML provides a general syntax for structuring and exchanging data; an application or vocabulary assigns meaning to the element names, so B is true. XML names are case-sensitive, making <Letter>, <LETTER>, and <letter> three distinct tags, so C is true. Statement A is false because it describes display-oriented markup such as HTML; XML tags are user-defined and primarily express data structure, not fixed browser presentation. Therefore B and C only, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 4 include A. A browser may display or transform XML with additional styling rules, but XML itself is not a predefined collection of presentation tags.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: XML is extensible, application-semantic, and case-sensitive; it structures data rather than prescribing page display.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Perspective projection maps a 3D point approximately as x'=d·x/z and y'=d·y/z. Lines parallel to the view plane share the relevant depth behaviour and remain parallel, while other parallel lines generally converge toward a vanishing point; thus A is true. The formulas explicitly require division by depth (or, in a homogeneous pipeline, division by w after the projection matrix), so B is true. Because division by a coordinate does not preserve addition or scalar multiplication in ordinary Cartesian coordinates, the complete perspective mapping is not linear there; C is false. Hence option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Every alternative containing C confuses the homogeneous matrix step with the full Cartesian mapping. A projection matrix acts linearly on homogeneous vectors, but the subsequent perspective divide makes the Euclidean transformation nonlinear.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Perspective projection uses depth division: it creates vanishing points and makes the final Cartesian transformation nonlinear.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Gouraud shading evaluates the illumination model at polygon vertices and linearly interpolates the resulting vertex colours across the interior, so B is true. Phong shading instead interpolates vertex normals across the polygon and evaluates illumination per pixel, so C is true. Per-pixel lighting is more expensive than interpolating already computed colours; therefore A reverses the cost comparison and is false. The correct combination is B and C, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 4 include A. Gouraud shading is normally cheaper, though it can miss a narrow specular highlight whose peak lies between vertices; Phong shading better captures such highlights at a higher computational cost.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Gouraud interpolates colour; Phong interpolates normals and lights each pixel.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Two schedule operations conflict exactly when all three conditions hold: they belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one is a write. Their relative order can then affect the value read or the final database state. Thus A, B, and C are required, while D states the opposite of B. The answer is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Two reads never conflict because swapping them changes nothing. Operations on different items do not conflict, and operations within one transaction are not the inter-transaction pairs used to construct a precedence graph. Options 1, 3, and 4 omit a required condition or include D.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Conflict test: different transactions + same item + at least one write.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Software-reliability models can use three common time domains. Execution time is the CPU time actually spent running the software. Calendar time is ordinary elapsed date-and-time over development or operation. Clock time is elapsed running-system time from start to finish, including time the program waits while the computer remains in operation. Therefore reliability can be described with respect to A, B, and C, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 2, and 4 each omit one recognized time domain. A model may prefer execution time because it more closely represents operational stress, but that preference does not make calendar or clock-time descriptions invalid.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Reliability has multiple possible time bases: CPU execution time, ordinary calendar time, and elapsed clock time.

sources.title: Software Reliability Engineering introduction — Chinese University of Hong Kong

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: An object-behavioural model is derived by examining the use cases, recognizing the events that drive object interactions, representing states and event-triggered transitions in state diagrams, and reviewing the resulting model for completeness and consistency. Accordingly A, B, and C are valid steps. D says to identify events that do not drive the interaction sequence; the useful step is the opposite—identify the events that do drive it. Hence option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 include D or omit one of the valid evaluation, modeling, and review steps. A state model must be connected to observable events from the scenarios rather than to irrelevant non-driving events.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use cases reveal driving events; state diagrams model their effects; review checks the behavioural model.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Behavioural or black-box testing derives tests from externally visible requirements rather than from program structure. Equivalence partitioning groups inputs expected to behave alike; boundary-value analysis targets class edges; and graph-based testing models externally visible objects, states, or relationships. Thus A, B, and C are behavioural techniques. Data-flow testing follows definition–use paths and loop testing examines loop structure, so D and E are white-box techniques. Therefore option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 1 mixes a black-box graph method with white-box data-flow testing. Option 3 contains two structural techniques. Option 4 incorrectly includes loop testing and omits graph-based testing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Black-box techniques use the specification; data-flow and loop testing use internal code structure.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Let Tbest(n) and Tworst(n) be the minimum and maximum running times over inputs of size n. Every actual running time T(n) satisfies Tbest(n)≤T(n)≤Tworst(n). If Tworst(n)≤c₂g(n) and Tbest(n)≥c₁g(n) for sufficiently large n, then c₁g(n)≤T(n)≤c₂g(n) for every input, exactly the definition of T(n)=Θ(g(n)). Conversely, a uniform Θ(g(n)) bound supplies those worst-case O and best-case Ω bounds. Therefore A only, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: B reverses the useful inequalities and need not squeeze every input between constant multiples of g. O(g) and Ω(g) are not equal sets, so C is false. No function can grow strictly faster than g (ω(g)) while also being asymptotically upper-bounded by a constant multiple of g (O(g)); hence the intersection in D is empty.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Worst-case O gives the upper side and best-case Ω gives the lower side; together they squeeze every input into Θ.

sources.title: Introduction to Algorithms — asymptotic growth exercises

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A tree has no cycle, hence no odd cycle, so it is bipartite and 2-colourable: A is true. A graph is bipartite exactly when it has no odd cycle; it may certainly contain even cycles such as C₄, so B is false and C is true. Greedy colouring uses at most d+1 colours when maximum degree is d, so D is true. E is false: take a clique on about √n vertices plus enough isolated vertices to make n total vertices. It has Θ(n) edges but needs Θ(√n) colours, not O(log n). Thus the incorrect statements are B and E, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 wrongly mark C incorrect, even though bipartite and 2-colourable are equivalent. Option 4 wrongly rejects the standard tree and greedy-colouring facts.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Bipartite forbids odd—not even—cycles, and a sparse whole graph can still contain a moderately large clique.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A conventional cryptographic hash maps messages of arbitrary finite length to a digest of a fixed length determined by the algorithm, so A is true and B is false. Because infinitely many possible messages are mapped into only finitely many fixed-length digests, the pigeonhole principle guarantees that distinct messages can share a hash value; this is a collision, so C is true. Therefore A and C only, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Variable-length output is not the defining hash interface. Collision resistance means finding a collision should be computationally difficult for a secure hash; it cannot mean collisions are mathematically impossible.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Hashing compresses an unbounded input space into a fixed-size output space, so collisions must exist.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Equivalence of two arbitrary context-free grammars (A) is undecidable, and so is inclusion L(G₂)⊆L(G₁) (B). For C, equality between an arbitrary CFL and a supplied regular language is also undecidable: choose the regular language R=Σ*. Then asking whether L(G₁)=R is exactly the universality problem for context-free grammars, which is undecidable. Consequently A, B, and C are all undecidable, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one undecidable problem. The fact that membership and emptiness for CFLs are decidable does not extend to equality, inclusion, or universality; regularity of one comparison language does not rescue C because Σ* is already regular.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Reduce C to CFG universality by choosing R=Σ*; equality and inclusion between arbitrary CFLs are also undecidable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: L₂ contains exactly the unary strings of even length: L₂=(aa)*, so it is regular. L₁ has lengths 1,2,4,8,… (powers of two). A unary regular language has an ultimately periodic set of lengths, but the gaps between consecutive powers of two grow without bound, so L₁ is not regular. L₃ is the copy language {ww}; a finite automaton cannot remember an arbitrary first half and compare it with the second, and the language is nonregular. Hence only L₂ is regular, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Every other option includes L₁ or L₃. A simple pumping or Myhill–Nerode argument separates both from regular languages, whereas L₂ needs only a two-state parity automaton.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Unary regular languages have eventually periodic lengths; powers of two do not, while even lengths do.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.answerText: All three grammars (A), (B), and (C) are ambiguous; the scanned question contains no option expressing this correct combination.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Grammar A is ambiguous immediately: ε has the direct derivation S⇒ε and also S⇒SS⇒εε. Grammar B is ambiguous for abab. One parse uses S⇒aεb(aεbε), while another uses S⇒a(bεaε)bε; these place the recursive substring in different S children. Grammar C is ambiguous for abbbb: either the first A produces bbbb and the final B produces ε, or the first A produces bb and the final B⇒A produces bb. Each case gives a distinct parse tree. Therefore A, B, and C are all ambiguous.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1, 3, and 4 each omit one grammar that has an explicit ambiguity witness; option 2 omits two. Since the source lists only pairwise combinations or B alone, none of its four option texts is mathematically complete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: One counterexample string with two parse trees proves ambiguity; ε, abab, and abbbb witness A, B, and C respectively.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Virtualization abstracts physical compute, storage, and network resources into logical instances that can be allocated among multiple tenants or workloads. That abstraction is the enabling cloud concept for pooling and sharing a common physical resource base, so C is correct. Virtual memory is an operating-system memory abstraction, and ‘service’ describes how capability is offered to a consumer rather than the mechanism that pools infrastructure. Therefore C only, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 omit virtualization. Virtual memory can share RAM within a system, but it is not the cloud concept meant by resource pooling; a service may consume pooled resources but does not itself perform the pooling.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Virtualization decouples logical resources from physical hardware, allowing the hardware pool to be shared safely and flexibly.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In the textbook POP model, a client can operate in delete mode or keep mode, so A is true. Delete mode retrieves a message and arranges for it to be removed from the server mailbox after retrieval/session completion, making B true and C false. Keep mode leaves the server copy in the mailbox after the client retrieves it, so E is true and D is false. Thus A, B, and E only, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 1 omits the defining keep-mode behaviour E. Options 2 and 3 include D, which contradicts keep mode; option 3 also includes C, which incorrectly places deletion before retrieval.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Delete mode removes the server copy after download; keep mode leaves that copy available after download.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A packet-filtering firewall can examine network-layer source and destination IP addresses and transport-layer protocol/port fields. It may therefore match source IP (A), destination IP (B), TCP source port (C), UDP source port (D), and TCP destination port (E). Rules often combine these fields with direction, protocol, interface, or connection state. All five listed fields are usable, so option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 list valid subsets but omit other equally filterable header fields. UDP ports are just as available for filtering as TCP ports once the IP protocol identifies the transport header.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Basic packet filters match the IP five-tuple: protocol, source/destination addresses, and source/destination ports.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: For a radical person, the sentence says electability occurs exactly when conservatism occurs: R→(E↔C), which is B. Expand the biconditional as (E→C)∧(C→E), replace each implication, and distribute R: (¬R∨¬E∨C)∧(¬R∨¬C∨E). This is precisely D. Hence B and D are equivalent formalizations, giving option 4.
