sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: A, (R∧E)↔C, wrongly forces any conservative person to be both radical and electable. In C, (C→E)∨¬E simplifies to (¬C∨E∨¬E), a tautology, so the formula imposes no required relationship between E and C when R holds.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: ‘If conservative, electable; otherwise not’ makes E and C equivalent within the radical case: R→(E↔C).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Entailment α⊨β requires β to be true in every model in which α is true, not merely a few, so A is false. Horn clauses naturally support forward chaining from known facts and backward chaining from a goal, so B is true. Soundness means every derived conclusion is entailed; deriving every entailed conclusion is completeness, so C is false. A finite propositional vocabulary cannot naturally represent arbitrarily many objects in an unbounded environment, so D is true. Therefore B and D only, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 include the incorrect entailment definition or confuse soundness with completeness. Remember: sound means no false positives; complete means no entailed conclusion is missed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Entailment uses all models, Horn rules suit chaining, and soundness is ‘only entailed,’ not ‘all entailed.’

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Minimax values are normally computed by a depth-first recursive traversal, so A is false. Alpha–beta pruning discards branches that cannot affect the minimax choice; good move ordering produces early bounds and far more pruning, making B true. Pruning never changes the backed-up minimax value, so C is true. Imperfect-information play requires maintaining and predicting belief states about hidden information, making D false. Thus B and C only, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 include the false breadth-first claim. Option 4 includes D, which denies the central role of belief-state reasoning in games with hidden information.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Alpha–beta is an exact minimax optimization: ordering changes work saved, not the chosen optimal move.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The union contains every pair appearing in either relation, so A→I. Removing the common pair (1,1) from R₁ leaves {(2,2),(3,3)}, hence B→IV. The only common pair is (1,1), so C→II. Removing (1,1) from R₂ leaves {(1,2),(1,3),(1,4)}, hence D→III. The mapping A-I, B-IV, C-II, D-III is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The distractors exchange intersection and one or both directional differences. Set difference is directional: R₁−R₂ and R₂−R₁ need not be the same.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Treat a relation as a set of ordered pairs and apply ordinary union, intersection, and directional difference.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Asynchronous handshaking coordinates sender and receiver with request/data-valid and acknowledgement signals travelling in opposite directions, so A→II. In programmed I/O, the CPU repeatedly checks the status flag and performs the transfer itself, so B→IV. In interrupt-initiated I/O, the interface signals the CPU when service is needed, so C→I. An I/O processor has its own control and local storage to manage many devices, so D→III. This is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other mappings confuse polling with interrupts or assign the autonomous I/O-processor description to a basic interface. The CPU’s role distinguishes programmed, interrupt-driven, and processor-controlled I/O.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Polling keeps the CPU checking; interrupts notify it; an I/O processor offloads control; handshaking uses two-way control signals.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Matrix A multiplies both x and y by 2, so A→II (uniform scale). Matrix B has translation entries 2 in the last column, so B→IV. Matrix C has cos60°=0.5 and sin60°≈0.866 in the standard rotation pattern, so C→III. Matrix D maps x' = x+2y and y'=y, an x-shear of factor 2, so D→I. Therefore A-II, B-IV, C-III, D-I, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Translation is recognized by the last column, scaling by diagonal entries, rotation by the sine/cosine block, and shear by an off-diagonal factor. The other options interchange these distinctive patterns.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Read a homogeneous 3×3 matrix by pattern: diagonal scale, last-column translation, sine/cosine rotation, off-diagonal shear.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In Chen ER notation, a double rectangle denotes a weak entity, so A→II. Underlining an attribute name marks a key attribute, so B→I. A double oval denotes a multivalued attribute, so C→IV. A double line from an entity to a relationship means total participation, so D→III. The complete mapping is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The distractors swap the visual conventions. A weak entity is not a multivalued attribute, and total participation is shown by a double connecting line rather than by an oval or rectangle alone.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: ER visual mnemonic: double rectangle=weak entity, underline=key, double oval=multivalued, double line=total participation.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: At CMM level 1, Initial, work is unpredictable and ad hoc: A→IV. Level 2, Repeatable, establishes basic project-management discipline so successful practices can be repeated: B→III. Level 3, Defined, standardizes and documents organization-wide process definitions: C→II. Level 4, Managed, uses quantitative process measurement and control: D→I. Thus option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The options mainly confuse adjacent maturity levels. The progression is ad hoc → managed projects → defined organizational process → quantitatively measured process; level 5 would add continuous optimization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: CMM ladder: Initial/ad hoc, Repeatable/project management, Defined/process definition, Managed/measurement, Optimizing/improvement.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Branch-and-bound maintains promising partial paths and their bounds for later exploration, so A→I. Steepest-ascent hill climbing evaluates the available successors and chooses the best improvement, so B→IV. Constraint satisfaction seeks assignments or states satisfying all stated constraints, so C→III. Means–ends analysis compares the current and goal states, identifies a difference, and selects an operator to reduce it, so D→II. Therefore option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other options confuse the goal-difference mechanism of means–ends analysis with the best-neighbour selection of hill climbing, or replace branch-and-bound’s frontier of partial candidates.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Match each strategy to what it stores or compares: partial paths, neighbours, constraints, or current–goal differences.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Topological sorting visits each vertex and edge once, giving Θ(V+E), so A→III. Bellman–Ford performs up to V−1 passes over E edges, giving O(VE), so C→II. Floyd–Warshall has three nested vertex loops, giving Θ(V³), so D→IV. The remaining listed match is Kruskal→I, yielding option 3. This I=O(V+E) bound assumes edges are already sorted or can be sorted linearly; with ordinary comparison sorting, standard Kruskal is O(E log E), usually written O(E log V).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 assign Bellman–Ford or Floyd–Warshall the wrong bound; option 4 calls topological sorting only O(V+E) while assigning Θ(V+E) to Kruskal. Under standard unsorted input none of the listed Kruskal bounds is fully general, so the paper’s intended assumption matters.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Memorize the robust three: topo Θ(V+E), Bellman–Ford O(VE), Floyd–Warshall Θ(V³); treat Kruskal’s sorting assumption explicitly.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Recursive languages are closed under complement and are recursively enumerable. Therefore complement(L_REC)∪L_RE is recursively enumerable, giving I→A. Every CFL is recursive; its complement is also recursive, and recursive languages are closed under union, so complement(L_CF)∪L_REC is recursive, giving II→B. L_R* remains regular, and CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages, so L_R*∩L_CF is context-free, giving III→C. Thus A-I, B-II, C-III, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other mappings ignore one of the containment or closure facts. CFLs are not closed under complement as CFLs, but their complements are still recursive because CFL membership is decidable.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use the hierarchy Regular⊂CFL⊂Recursive⊂RE plus closure of CFL under intersection with a regular language.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: SLIP frames IP datagrams over a serial line and is placed at the data-link access layer, so A→III. BGP exchanges routing information and is classified here with the network/routing layer, so B→IV. UDP is a transport protocol, so C→II. SNMP is an application-layer network-management protocol, so D→I. The mapping is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The distractors place UDP outside transport or SNMP outside application. BGP runs over TCP in implementation, but its functional role in this exam classification is inter-domain network-layer routing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: SLIP links, BGP routes, UDP transports, and SNMP provides an application service.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Greedy best-first search follows the smallest heuristic h and, without repeated-state control, may cycle even in a finite state space, so A→II. A* can require exponential memory as well as heavy computation because it retains a large frontier, so B→IV. RBFS emulates best-first search recursively using linear memory—more precisely O(bd), often written O(d) for fixed branching factor—so C→I. SMA* uses all available memory and is optimal when the optimal path fits; otherwise it returns the best solution reachable under the memory limit, so D→III. Hence option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The alternatives misassign SMA*’s memory-bounded guarantee or RBFS’s linear-space property. A* is optimal under standard conditions, but its main practical weakness is memory consumption, not the SMA* guarantee.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: A* stores the frontier, RBFS trades re-expansion for linear space, and SMA* makes best use of a fixed memory bound.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Compare strings left to right using 0<1; if one string is a prefix of another, the shorter string comes first. First, 0001<001 because the first difference is the third bit, 0<1. Next 001<010 because their second bits are 0 and 1. Since 010 is a prefix of 0101, 010<0101. Finally 0101<011 because their third bits are 0 and 1. Thus 0001<001<010<0101<011, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other choices treat the strings as binary numbers, sort partly by length, or place an extension before its prefix. Lexicographic order is dictionary order, not numeric order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Stop at the first differing symbol; only when no difference appears before one string ends does the shorter prefix come first.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In Flynn’s taxonomy, SISD has one instruction stream and one data stream: a conventional sequential machine. SIMD applies one instruction stream to many data elements, requiring multiple processing lanes but common control. MIMD supports multiple independent instruction streams operating on multiple data streams, demanding the richest control, coordination, and interconnection. Descending architectural complexity is therefore MIMD>SIMD>SISD, or B,C,A: option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Any order placing SISD above a parallel organization reverses the increase in hardware/control complexity. SIMD is less general than MIMD because its processing elements execute the same instruction in lockstep.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: More independent instruction streams mean more control complexity: MIMD highest, SIMD middle, SISD lowest.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Perspective projection needs homogeneous coordinates because the perspective divide makes its Euclidean-coordinate formula non-linear, so D is perspective-only. Orthographic projection does not shrink an object merely because it is farther from the viewer, so C is orthographic-only. Both projections map a 3-D straight line to a straight line in the image (apart from degenerate cases), so A belongs to both. General projection does not preserve all distances and angles, so B belongs to neither. The required order is D, C, A, B: option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 wrongly treat preservation of distance and angle as a general projection property. Option 2 assigns B to perspective projection and D to both projections, reversing the essential distinction created by the perspective divide.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Perspective changes apparent size with depth and needs a homogeneous divide; orthographic size is depth-independent; both preserve straightness, not general lengths or angles.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Cohesion is stronger when a module's elements contribute to one focused purpose. Sequential cohesion is strongest here because one activity's output becomes the next activity's input. Communicational cohesion follows because the activities operate on the same data. Procedural cohesion merely groups steps that must follow a control sequence; temporal cohesion groups activities executed at the same time, such as initialization; logical cohesion groups activities only because they belong to the same broad category. Thus B > C > E > D > A, which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 put logical cohesion first even though it is weak. Option 3 places procedural cohesion above communicational cohesion, although sharing data creates a tighter relationship than sharing only an execution order.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For these five types, remember: sequential > communicational > procedural > temporal > logical.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Prim's algorithm repeatedly adds the lightest edge crossing from the current tree to an outside vertex. From a, add (a,b) of weight 4. The two weight-8 choices (a,h) and (b,c) tie, so choosing (b,c) is valid. The successive lightest crossing edges are then (c,i)=2, (c,f)=4, (f,g)=2, (g,h)=1, (c,d)=7, and (d,e)=9. This produces exactly the sequence in option 3 and connects all nine vertices without a cycle.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 1 chooses (a,h)=8 legitimately at the tie but later lists (g,h) before either endpoint g has joined the tree, so it is not a valid Prim step. Option 2 chooses the heavier (b,h)=11 while weight-8 (b,c) is available. Option 4 begins adding edges disconnected from the tree rooted at a.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: At every Prim step, inspect only edges crossing the current cut; ties may give several valid orders, but a disconnected edge can never be chosen.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The expression r=a(a+b)* describes every nonempty binary string beginning with a, including the one-symbol string a. The expression s=a(a+b)+ describes the same kind of string but requires at least one further symbol, so L(s) is a proper subset of L(r). Finally, t=aa*b describes strings a^k b with k≥1; every such string begins with a and has length at least two, so L(t) is a proper subset of L(s). Therefore L(r) ⊇ L(s) ⊇ L(t), option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The reverse containments fail immediately: a belongs to L(r) but not L(s), while ab or aa belongs to L(s) but not every such string belongs to L(t). Options 3 and 4 incorrectly place the restricted a^k b language above the general beginning-with-a language.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Translate each regular expression into a plain-language shape, then use short witness strings to test each containment.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: For Statement I, Fermat's congruence gives n^5≡n (mod 5) for every integer n, including multiples of 5; hence 5 divides n^5−n. For Statement II, factor n^3−n=n(n−1)(n+1), the product of three consecutive integers. One factor is divisible by 3 and at least one is even, so the product is divisible by 2×3=6. Both statements are correct, giving option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 declare at least one true divisibility statement false. The factorization of n^3−n proves Statement II directly, and residues modulo 5—or Fermat's theorem—prove Statement I.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use modular residues for n^5−n and the three-consecutive-integers factorization for n^3−n.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A hardwired control unit implements control signals with fixed logic rather than fetching and interpreting microinstructions. Its logic can therefore be optimized for high speed, so Statement I is true. In indirect addressing, the address field points to a memory location that contains the effective address; memory is accessed once to obtain that address and again to fetch the operand. Counting operand-fetch accesses after the instruction has been read, this is two memory references, so Statement II is also true. Hence option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 reject one or both standard properties. Indirect addressing is slower precisely because of the extra pointer lookup; hardwired control is fast but less flexible than microprogrammed control.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Hardwired control trades flexibility for speed; memory-indirect addressing adds one pointer access before the operand access.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A Bézier curve is controlled by its control polygon but generally does not pass through every interior control point; it interpolates the first and last control points. Thus Statement I is false. A Bézier curve of degree n has n+1 control points, so a cubic (degree-3) curve has four control points. Statement II is true, and option 4 follows.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 3 confuse approximation with interpolation. Option 2 also rejects the degree-plus-one rule that gives four control points for a cubic curve.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Degree n means n+1 control points; an ordinary Bézier curve passes through its endpoints, not all control points.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Correlation +1 means V1 and V2 have an exact positive affine relationship, V2=aV1+b with a>0, assuming nonzero variances. Every data point therefore lies on one straight line. A k-means centroid is the coordinate-wise mean of its cluster, and the mean of points satisfying that affine equation also satisfies it; all three centroids are consequently collinear. Correlation 0 only means zero linear covariance. The data may have a nonlinear pattern or a two-dimensional spread, so its three centroids need not be collinear. Statement I is true and Statement II false: option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 incorrectly treat zero correlation as a geometric straight-line constraint. Option 2 misses that perfect positive correlation does impose an exact line and that averaging preserves the line.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Perfect correlation gives an exact affine line; zero correlation does not imply either independence or collinearity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In P1, each of the three fork calls doubles the number of running processes: 1→2→4→8. All eight reach the single printf, so Statement I is true. In P2, the first fork creates 2 processes and the first printf executes twice; the second fork creates 4 processes and the next printf executes four times; the third creates 8 processes and the last printf executes eight times. The total is 2+4+8=14, not 12, so Statement II is false. The answer is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 accept the incorrect total of 12, while option 2 rejects the correct 2^3=8 count for P1. The intended exam model counts each reached printf once; it abstracts away unusual output-buffer duplication when stdout is redirected.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: After each unconditional fork, double the process count; for interleaved output, add the number of processes present at each printf.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Quality control is product-focused: inspections, reviews, and testing detect whether each software work product conforms to its stated requirements. Quality assurance is process- and management-focused: it audits how the process is being followed and reports evidence to management. The two statements describe these complementary roles correctly, so both are true and option 1 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 make at least one correct definition false. QC does not replace QA: QC examines deliverables, whereas QA supplies confidence and visibility through planned process activities and audits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: QC asks whether the product is correct; QA asks whether the process provides confidence and is being followed.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: During DFS of an undirected graph, an edge either becomes a tree edge or joins a vertex to one of its ancestors, making it a back edge; undirected DFS has no cross edges. Statement I is therefore true. In BFS, an edge can join vertices on the same level or on adjacent levels, so the guaranteed condition is |i−j|≤1, not |i−j|=1. A triangle rooted at one vertex gives an immediate same-level edge between the other two vertices. Statement II is false, so option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 accept the too-strong equality for BFS levels. Option 2 incorrectly imports directed-graph DFS edge categories into a simple undirected DFS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Undirected DFS non-tree edges go to ancestors; BFS endpoints of an edge differ in depth by at most one.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The emptiness problem for a context-sensitive language is undecidable. If an algorithm could decide whether L1∩L2 is empty for arbitrary context-sensitive L1 and L2, set L2=Σ*: it would then decide whether L1 itself is empty, which is impossible. Thus Statement I is true. A context-sensitive language is accepted by a linear-bounded automaton. For a fixed input, the machine has only finitely many bounded-tape configurations, so its reachability/acceptance question can be decided; membership is decidable (indeed PSPACE-complete in general). Statement II is also true. Hence option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 deny one of the two standard decision results. Closure of context-sensitive languages under intersection does not make intersection emptiness decidable; closure and decidability of an associated property are different questions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: For context-sensitive languages, membership is decidable but emptiness—and therefore general intersection-emptiness—is undecidable.

sources.title: Uniform vs. Nonuniform Membership for Mildly Context-Sensitive Languages: A Brief Survey
Formal languages and automata — Encyclopedia of Mathematics

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: For an ideal noiseless band-limited channel of bandwidth H, the Nyquist sampling/signalling limit is 2H independent symbols per second; with V signal levels, the corresponding bit rate is 2H log2 V. Thus Statement I is correct. For a noisy channel with signal-to-noise power ratio S/N, the Shannon–Hartley capacity is C=H log2(1+S/N) bits/s, exactly as stated. Statement II is also correct, so option 1 is the answer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 make at least one fundamental limit false. Nyquist addresses symbol rate in the noiseless idealization, while Shannon gives an upper bit-rate capacity in the presence of noise; the formulas are complementary, not competing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Nyquist: 2H symbols/s for a noiseless band-limited channel. Shannon: H log2(1+S/N) bits/s for a noisy channel.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The Caesar cipher is a monoalphabetic substitution cipher: each plaintext letter is replaced by the letter a fixed number of alphabet positions away, so Statement I is true. Diffie–Hellman lets two parties use public values and private exponents to derive the same shared secret over an insecure channel, so Statement II is true in the exam's sense of secret-key establishment. Therefore option 1 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 reject one or both true descriptions. More precisely, Diffie–Hellman agrees on a shared secret rather than transmitting the secret itself, and it needs authentication to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks; neither qualification makes the statement false in this context.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Caesar is fixed-shift substitution; Diffie–Hellman is shared-secret key agreement.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A genetic algorithm performs stochastic population-based search: selection favours fitter states while crossover and mutation generate new candidates, making the hill-climbing characterization reasonable. Statement I is true. In a nondeterministic problem, one action can have several possible outcomes. An AND–OR search tree uses OR nodes for the agent's choice and AND nodes for the outcomes that the plan must handle, thereby representing a contingent plan that succeeds for every possible result. Statement II is also true, so option 1 follows.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 reject at least one standard AI characterization. Ordinary single-path search is insufficient for nondeterministic outcomes, while genetic algorithms differ from deterministic hill climbing precisely through random variation and a maintained population.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Genetic algorithms search with a stochastic population; AND–OR search builds plans with a branch for every nondeterministic outcome.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: One track contains 50 sectors, each of 512 bytes. Therefore T=50×512=25,600 bytes=25 KBytes when K=1,024 bytes. One surface contains 2,000 such tracks, so S=2,000×25 KBytes=50,000 KBytes. Hence (T,S)=(25 K, 50,000 K), which is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 1 doubles the track capacity. Option 2 uses only 1,000 tracks when finding surface capacity. Option 4 matches neither the 50-sector track nor the 2,000-track surface specified in the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Track capacity = sectors per track × bytes per sector; surface capacity = tracks per surface × track capacity.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: From Q141, each surface holds 50,000 KBytes. Five double-sided platters provide 5×2=10 recording surfaces. Thus disk capacity = 10×50,000 KBytes = 500,000 KBytes, giving option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 4 is the capacity of one surface, not the whole disk. Option 3 effectively counts only five surfaces and ignores that each platter is double-sided. Option 1 is only half of one surface's capacity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Total disk capacity = bytes per surface × two surfaces per platter × number of platters.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A cylinder consists of all equal-radius tracks across the recording surfaces. Since each surface has 2,000 tracks, the disk has 2,000 cylinders; Statement I is true. In the logical-block model used by this exercise, a valid block size must be a whole multiple of the 512-byte sector size. Because 51,200=100×512, it is a valid block size even though it spans more than one 50-sector track. Statement II is therefore false, so option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 4 incorrectly deny the cylinder count. Options 1 and 4 impose a one-track maximum that the source exercise does not impose on a logical block. Do not confuse a sector-alignment requirement with a track-boundary restriction.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Cylinder count equals tracks per surface; a logical block must contain an integral number of sectors and may span tracks in this model.

sources.title: Database Management Systems exercise solutions — disk geometry and valid block sizes

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: At 5,400 revolutions per minute, one revolution takes 60/5,400 seconds = 1/90 second ≈0.0111 second. The maximum rotational delay occurs when the desired sector has just passed the head, requiring almost one full revolution. It is therefore approximately 0.011 second, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: The other values are displaced by factors of 10 or 100. Also note that average rotational delay would be half a revolution, about 0.0056 second, but the question asks for the maximum.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Maximum rotational delay = time for one revolution = 60/rpm seconds.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: A track holds 25 KBytes. The rotation speed is 5,400 rpm = 5,400/60 = 90 revolutions per second. If one track is transferred per revolution, the rate is 25 KBytes/revolution ×90 revolutions/second = 2,250 KBytes/second. This is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 do not equal track capacity multiplied by revolutions per second. Using rpm directly without dividing by 60 would also give a rate 60 times too large.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Transfer rate = data per revolution × revolutions per second.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The predicate PROJNO IN (20,30,40) selects rows for any of the three listed project numbers. Because PROJECTWORK's key is (EMPID,PROJNO), one employee can have several selected rows—one for each project—and selecting EMPID alone can therefore produce duplicates. DISTINCT removes those repetitions. The correct query is SELECT DISTINCT EMPID FROM PROJECTWORK WHERE PROJNO IN (20,30,40), option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 use invalid equality syntax for a list. Option 2 uses IN correctly but omits DISTINCT, so an employee assigned to more than one listed project can appear more than once.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use IN for membership in a value list and DISTINCT when projection can collapse different source rows to the same selected value.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Statement I joins EMPLOYEE.DEPTNO to DEPARTMENT.DID and then keeps the row whose department name is ENGLISH. Its four aggregate functions therefore summarize exactly the English-department employees. Statement II filters DEPARTMENT to ENGLISH but never joins it to EMPLOYEE. The comma-separated FROM then forms a Cartesian product; since DNAME is unique, the English row is paired with every employee, so the aggregates cover all employees rather than only the English department. Statement I is correct and Statement II false: option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 accept the query with the missing join condition, while option 2 rejects the correctly joined query. A WHERE predicate on one table does not automatically connect it to another table in FROM.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: With old-style comma joins, an explicit equality condition is essential; otherwise the result is a Cartesian product.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: The requirement is salary greater than every salary returned for department 20. SQL expresses that universal comparison as SALARY > ALL (subquery), and query C also includes the required ORDER BY EID. Queries A and B compare a scalar value with a subquery that can return many rows—one per department-20 employee—so they fail with a multirow scalar-subquery error in the general case. Only C is correct, giving option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 include A, and option 3 includes B; neither scalar comparison represents a general multirow 'greater than all' test. B additionally omits the required ordering.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use >ALL(subquery) for 'greater than every returned value'; a bare scalar comparison requires the subquery to return at most one row.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: In the SQL form used by the question, ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE DROP SALARY may be followed by CASCADE or RESTRICT. CASCADE removes dependent schema objects along with the column; RESTRICT permits the drop only when no dependent object blocks it. No dependency on SALARY is specified, so both A and B are valid column-drop statements. C is syntactically invalid because ALTER must be followed by TABLE. Therefore A and B only, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Options 2 and 3 include invalid statement C. Option 4 excludes the valid RESTRICT form. CASCADE and RESTRICT change dependency handling, not the fact that the command is a column drop.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: DDL syntax begins ALTER TABLE; CASCADE and RESTRICT specify what happens when other schema objects depend on the dropped column.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.explanation: Query A is valid DML: it identifies the DID of the SCIENCE department, selects employees whose DEPTNO matches it, and replaces each salary by 1.20 times its current value. Query B is invalid because SQL uses UPDATE EMPLOYEE, not UPDATE TABLE EMPLOYEE, and DNAME is not an EMPLOYEE column. Query C uses ALTER TABLE, which changes a schema rather than row values; it also compares numeric DEPTNO with DNAME and subtracts 1.20 instead of granting a 20% rise. Thus A only is correct, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.whyOthersFail: Option 1 includes invalid B, while options 3 and 4 include invalid C. A salary increase is a row update, not a table-alteration operation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2020-nov-combined-wit.keyIdea: Use UPDATE…SET for data changes; use a subquery to translate the department name into the department key stored in EMPLOYEE.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Alternative text gives a nonvisual replacement for meaningful image content, so a screen reader can communicate the image's purpose to a user who cannot see it; A is correct. Browsers can also display alt text when the image resource cannot be loaded, so B is correct. Descriptive alternative text helps search engines understand the image and its surrounding page context, so C is also a legitimate SEO benefit. Therefore A, B, and C are correct: option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one valid purpose. Alt text should describe the image's function or content rather than be stuffed with keywords; decorative images normally use an empty alt attribute, but the attribute itself should still be present.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: The alt attribute supplies a text alternative for accessibility, loading failure, and machine understanding of meaningful images.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Each loop adds N&1, the least-significant bit of N, to counter and then shifts N right. The loop therefore counts the number of 1 bits in the original binary representation. The final comparison is true only when that count is exactly one. A positive unsigned integer has exactly one set bit precisely when it is a power of two; N=0 returns false. Thus option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Oddness checks only the lowest bit, while this function examines every bit. A number of the form 2^k−1 has all 1s and usually produces a count greater than one. 'Any 1' would require counter>0, not counter==1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Repeated N&1 plus right shift computes population count; popcount one characterizes powers of two.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Build only the needed smaller results. F(2)=F(−1)+2+F(0)+2="-2-2". Also F(1)="-1-1", so F(3)=F(0)+3+F(1)+3="-3-1-13". Therefore F(5)=F(2)+5+F(3)+5="-2-25-3-1-135". This matches option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: The alternatives lose, reorder, or duplicate pieces generated by the two recursive calls. String + performs concatenation from left to right; it does not numerically add the displayed integers.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: For recursive string functions, compute a small call table and preserve exact left-to-right concatenation order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: JNDI is the Java Naming and Directory Interface, the API for naming and directory services, so A→II. RMI is Remote Method Invocation, providing support for calls on remote objects, so B→III. The JDK contains development tools such as the compiler plus class libraries, so C→IV. The JRE supplies the runtime needed to execute Java programs, so D→I. The mapping is option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: The other mappings confuse the development kit with the runtime environment or assign naming services to RMI. The acronym expansions make each match direct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: JNDI names, RMI invokes remotely, JDK develops, and JRE runs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Imperative programming describes computation through commands and state-changing procedures, so A→IV. Object-oriented programming remains imperative but organizes data and operations into abstract data types/objects, so B→III. Logic programming uses declarative clauses and theorem-proving or inference, so C→I. Functional programming emphasizes expression evaluation and avoids side effects, so D→II. Hence option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 interchange the characteristic declarative styles of logic and functional programming or label command-based imperative programming as side-effect free.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Commands=imperative, objects/ADTs=object-oriented, clauses/inference=logic, expressions/pure functions=functional.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: A black-and-white image needs 1 bit per pixel. Eight 1-megapixel images therefore need 8×1 million bits=8 million bits≈1 MB. Eight colours require log2 8=3 bits per pixel. One 8-megapixel, 8-colour image needs 8 million×3=24 million bits≈3 MB. Total storage is approximately 1+3=4 MB, option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Option 3 omits the black-and-white images. The GB choices confuse bits, bytes, and megapixels by orders of magnitude. The calculation assumes uncompressed pixel data and the units intended by the choices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Image bits = pixels × bits per pixel, and bits per pixel = log2(number of colours).

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Bresenham maintains an incrementally updated decision/error variable, so B is true. Its update can be expressed entirely with integer additions and comparisons, avoiding the floating-point slope accumulation and per-pixel rounding associated with basic DDA; hence A and C are true. A raster has a discrete pixel grid, so most geometric lines appear as a staircase approximation rather than an exact continuous straight line; D is false. Thus A, B, and C only: option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 2 omit a real algorithm property. Option 3 incorrectly claims that finite square pixels can represent every continuous line exactly. DDA is also incremental, but Bresenham's integer incremental error update is the key optimization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Bresenham replaces floating-point rounding with an incremental integer decision variable; rasterization still approximates the ideal line.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Compare each source and target: y is unchanged, while x increases by y. Thus x'=x+y and y'=y, an x-direction shear of factor 1. In homogeneous coordinates this is [[1,1,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,1]]. Checking (1,1) gives (2,1), and the same matrix maps the other three vertices to the stated targets. Therefore option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Option 2 performs the opposite shear, y'=x+y. Option 3 adds a translation of 1 in x, which would move every target. Option 4 is singular and maps both output coordinates to x+y.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Derive coordinate equations from corresponding points first; then read their coefficients into the homogeneous transformation matrix.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Four control points define a cubic Bézier curve, so A is true. At t=1/2 the Bernstein weights are 1/8, 3/8, 3/8, and 1/8. Hence P(1/2)=[P0+3P1+3P2+P3]/8=(0,24/8)=(0,3), making B true. Bézier weights are nonnegative and sum to one for 0≤t≤1, so every curve point is a convex combination of the control points and cannot leave their convex hull. C is false. Therefore A and B only, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 accept the false claim C or omit the verified midpoint. The convex-hull property is one of the main geometric guarantees of Bézier curves.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: A Bézier curve with n+1 control points has degree n and stays inside their convex hull.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Clipping a convex m-gon against a convex n-gon can produce an intersection with as many as m+n sides. A triangle and rectangle can attain 3+4=7 sides—for example, when one triangle vertex lies inside the viewport and the other edges cut the rectangle so that all four rectangle edges contribute. Therefore the claimed maximum of 6 is false. Perspective projection uses coordinates such as x'=d·x/z and y'=d·y/z after the homogeneous divide, so it is nonlinear in z; Statement II is true. The answer is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 3 accept the incorrect six-side limit, while option 2 denies the reciprocal-depth dependence of perspective projection. A perspective matrix is linear in homogeneous coordinates, but conversion back to normalized Euclidean coordinates includes division by depth.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Triangle∩rectangle can be a heptagon; perspective normalization introduces 1/z and is nonlinear in Euclidean depth.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Beta testing places a prerelease product with representative external users in their normal environments. Those potential customers exercise the software at their own locations and report real-world failures and usability problems. This is option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Testing by users at the developer's site is alpha testing. Testing by product developers, whether at their own or a customer's site, is not the defining beta arrangement.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Alpha: selected users at the developer site; beta: external users in their own environments.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: In MVC, V means View. The View presents application data and forms the user-facing interface, so option 1 is correct. The Model holds domain state and business logic, while the Controller interprets user actions and coordinates operations on the model and selection of a view.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Security is a cross-cutting concern rather than the View's defining role. Option 3 describes the Model, and option 4 describes the Controller.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: MVC mnemonic: Model=data/logic, View=presentation, Controller=input coordination.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Formal methods specify and reason about software using mathematically defined syntax and semantics—commonly logic, set theory, relations, algebra, and proof rules. The predominant notation is therefore mathematical, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Text and diagrams may explain a formal model, and code may implement it, but neither provides the precise proof-oriented foundation that makes the method formal.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Formal methods replace informal ambiguity with mathematically precise specification and verification.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: An SRS is unambiguous when every stated requirement has exactly one interpretation. That definition matches the condition directly, so option 3 is correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Correct means the SRS reflects actual stakeholder needs; consistent means its requirements do not conflict; verifiable means an objective check can determine whether each requirement is satisfied. None of those specifically means one interpretation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: One requirement, one meaning = unambiguous.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Use availability A=MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR). Solving gives MTTR=MTBF(1−A)/A. With MTBF=1 day=86,400 seconds and A=0.9999, MTTR=86,400×0.0001/0.9999≈8.64 seconds. The nearest choice is approximately 9 seconds, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: The other choices are farther from 8.64 seconds. A useful mental check is that 99.99% availability permits only 0.01% downtime, roughly 8.64 seconds per day.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Rearrange A=uptime/(uptime+downtime); four nines leaves about 8.6 seconds of downtime per day.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Source control should preserve the authoritative material needed to understand, change, and rebuild the system. That includes code files (A) and documentation files (B). Ordinary output files are results of running the program, and automatically generated files should normally be recreated by a documented, reproducible build rather than stored as independent sources. Thus A and B only, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 include outputs or generated artifacts while omitting code or documentation. Generated files may be committed only in exceptional deployment workflows; the SCM rule tested here is to version inputs and reproducible build instructions, not derived products.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Version the sources of truth; regenerate derived outputs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Waterfall fixes a sequence of distinct stages and is difficult to revisit, so A→IV. Evolutionary development delivers and refines the product in increments, so B→I. Component-based engineering must adapt requirements to the capabilities and interfaces of available components, making compromises inevitable, so C→II. Spiral development makes risk identification and reduction central to every cycle, so D→III. The complete mapping is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: The other mappings assign explicit risk management away from the spiral model or confuse evolutionary increments with component constraints. Those are the distinguishing features of the four models.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Waterfall=rigid stages; evolutionary=increments; components=require compromise; spiral=risk-driven cycles.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: The five original CMM maturity levels ascend as Initial, Repeatable, Defined, Managed, and Optimizing. Translating those names to the letters gives C, E, A, D, B, which is option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 move Defined, Repeatable, Managed, or Optimizing out of their maturity sequence. The progression is from ad hoc work to repeatable projects, organization-wide definition, quantitative management, and continuous optimization.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: CMM ladder: Initial → Repeatable → Defined → Managed → Optimizing.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Cleanroom development builds increments and applies statistically based usage testing to certify the reliability of each accumulating increment, so Statement I is true. Its central philosophy is defect prevention through formal specification, correctness verification, and disciplined incremental development—not a conventional code-test-debug cycle focused on removing defects after they appear. Statement II is false. Therefore option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 accept a description opposite to Cleanroom's prevention goal, while option 2 rejects its statistical quality certification. Testing in Cleanroom estimates reliability rather than serving as the primary debugging mechanism.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Cleanroom prevents defects analytically and certifies reliability statistically; it does not rely on debug-after-test development.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Exhaustive testing would have to cover all input values, input sequences, states, paths, timing combinations, and environmental conditions. Even small-looking domains grow combinatorially, making the total number of tests too large to execute in practical time. Hence developers do not perform exhaustive testing in practice, and the computational explosion stated in R directly explains A. Both are true with R the correct explanation: option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 2–4 either break the genuine cause-and-effect link or declare a true statement false. Testing uses representative partitions, boundaries, risk priorities, and coverage criteria precisely because exhaustive enumeration is infeasible.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: The state/input space grows combinatorially, so practical testing samples strategically instead of enumerating everything.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Eliminate implications in pair A: ¬p→(q→r) becomes p∨¬q∨r, and q→(p∨r) also becomes ¬q∨p∨r, so A is equivalent. Pair B becomes (p∧¬q)∨r versus ¬p∨¬q∨r, which are not identical. Pair C becomes (p∧¬q)∨¬r∨s versus (p∧¬r)∨¬q∨s, also not identical. Therefore A only, option 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: For B, p=false, q=true, r=false makes the first formula false and the second true. For C, p=false, q=true, r=false, s=false makes the first true and the second false. These counterexamples eliminate every option containing B or C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Replace P→Q by ¬P∨Q, simplify, and use one counterexample when two forms differ.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Write x=n+r with n integer and 0≤r<1. Then floor(2x)=2n+floor(2r), while floor(x)+floor(x+1/2)=n+[n+floor(r+1/2)]=2n+floor(r+1/2). Both added floors are 0 for r<1/2 and 1 for r≥1/2, so Statement I is true. Statement II is false: x=y=0.6 gives floor(x+y)=1 but floor(x)+floor(y)=0. Hence option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 claim unrestricted additivity, but fractional parts can carry across an integer boundary. Option 2 rejects the valid doubling identity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Floor addition may gain a carry of one; splitting x into integer and fractional parts exposes when that happens.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Because five distinct jobs must reach four distinct employees with none empty, exactly one employee receives two jobs and the other three receive one each. Choose the employee receiving two jobs in 4 ways, choose that pair of jobs in C(5,2)=10 ways, and biject the remaining three jobs to the remaining employees in 3!=6 ways. Total=4×10×6=240, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: 1024=4^5 counts every unrestricted assignment, including employees with no job. The values 625 and 20 do not account for the onto constraint and all permutations of distinct jobs and employees.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: An onto assignment of 5 objects to 4 recipients must have occupancy pattern 2,1,1,1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: The number of distinct bins is 50 aisles ×85 locations per aisle ×5 shelves =21,250. With at most one product per bin, 21,250 products can still avoid a collision. The next product forces some bin to contain at least two by the pigeonhole principle. Therefore the least guaranteed number is 21,251, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 multiply only some dimensions or stop at the number of bins. A guarantee requires one more product than the total number of available bins.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: For N bins, N+1 objects guarantee a shared bin.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Let W(n) be the number of ways to climb n stairs. The final move is either one stair, leaving any W(n−1) route, or two stairs, leaving W(n−2), so W(n)=W(n−1)+W(n−2). With W(0)=1 and W(1)=1, the sequence is 1,1,2,3,5,8,13,21,34. Thus W(8)=34, option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: 21 is W(7), not W(8), while 24 and 31 do not satisfy the recurrence. Counting only the number of one- and two-steps without arranging them misses distinct orders.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Condition on the last step; the stair count is a Fibonacci recurrence.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Using the convention intended here, W_n=K1+C_n: it has an n-vertex rim plus a hub. Every rim vertex has degree 3—two rim neighbours and the hub—while the hub has degree n. The graph is regular exactly when n=3, at which point W_3 is K4 and every vertex has degree 3. Therefore option 2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: For n>3 the hub degree n differs from the rim degree 3; n=2 does not give the ordinary simple wheel construction. A notation caveat matters: some references define W_n as the wheel on n total vertices, K1+C_(n−1); under that alternate convention the same K4 is called W_4. The question is ambiguous unless its convention is specified.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Equate hub degree with rim degree: n=3 under the cycle-size convention.

sources.title: Wheel Graph — MathWorld (documents both numbering conventions)
NetworkX wheel_graph documentation (alternate total-vertex convention)

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: Planarity concerns whether a graph can be redrawn without crossings, not whether its supplied drawing already has crossings. Graph A can be redrawn by routing its crossed chords around the outer cycle, so it is planar. Graph B is K3,3: its six vertices split into two groups of three with every cross-group pair adjacent, and Kuratowski's theorem makes it nonplanar. Graph C is the Petersen graph, another nonplanar graph; it has a K3,3 minor. Hence only A is planar, option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1 and 4 classify K3,3 as planar, while option 2 excludes the redrawable graph A and includes the two nonplanar graphs. A visible crossing alone is not proof of nonplanarity because edges can often be rerouted.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Identify graph structure, not drawing style: K3,3 and the Petersen graph are nonplanar; incidental crossings can disappear under redrawing.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: In Boolean algebra, x+x=x is the idempotent law, so A→III. The equation x+0=x is the identity law, so B→I. The equation x+1=1 is domination, so C→IV. Finally x+xy=x is absorption, so D→II. The mapping A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II is option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: The other options interchange laws with visibly different roles: identity leaves x unchanged using 0, domination forces 1, idempotence removes duplication, and absorption removes the redundant xy term.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: 0 is the OR identity, 1 dominates OR, repeated x is idempotent, and x absorbs x·y.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: The hypothesis is equivalent to saying that commuting elements must be equal. First, a and a² commute because a·a²=a³=a²·a; hence a=a², proving A. Next, aba commutes with a: (aba)a=aba and a(aba)=aba using a²=a, so aba=a, proving B. Finally compare abc and ac. Using B, cac=c and aca=a; therefore (abc)(ac)=ab(cac)=abc and (ac)(abc)=(aca)bc=abc. They commute, so abc=ac, proving C. All three identities follow: option 4.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: Options 1–3 omit at least one consequence. The key is not to assume commutativity globally; instead construct pairs that associativity and the derived identities force to commute, then apply the contrapositive of the hypothesis.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: No two distinct elements commute forces an idempotent rectangular-band structure: a²=a, aba=a, and abc=ac.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.explanation: A linear objective reaches its optimum at a feasible corner. The important corners include (0,0), (5/2,0), (0,11/3), the intersection of 2x−3y=5 with 4x+y=15, namely (25/7,5/7), and the intersection of x+3y=11 with 4x+y=15. Solving the last pair gives x=34/11 and y=29/11; it also satisfies 2x−3y≤5. There Z=6(34/11)+5(29/11)=349/11≈31.72, larger than the objective at every other feasible corner. Thus option 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.whyOthersFail: 15 is the value at (5/2,0), and 25 occurs at (25/7,5/7). The value 41.44 is not attained by a feasible corner. Intersections must be checked against all constraints before comparing their objectives.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2021-nov-with-answers.keyIdea: Enumerate feasible boundary intersections, evaluate Z at each, and select the largest value.
